And Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Mas'ud. He said: We used to recite in the era of the Messenger of Allah: 'O Messenger, convey what was sent down to you from your Lord, that Ali is the master of the believers, and if you do not do, then you have not conveyed His message, and Allah protects you from the people.'

These are their hadiths, this is their book, and these chains of narration are not Shiite chains of narration. This book is a Sunni book, and these chains of narration are Sunni chains of narration, and the sources are Sunni sources. Meaning he did not transmit from a Shiite source. "And Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Mas'ud. He said: We used to recite in the era of the Messenger of Allah: 'O Messenger, convey what was sent down to you from your Lord, that Ali is the master of the believers.'" Thus they used to recite it. "And if you do not do, then you have not conveyed His message, and Allah protects you from the people." This is the speech of Ibn Mas'ud. And this book is Al-Durr al-Manthur.

And this issue is not an orphan. I bring a second example to confirm this meaning and to confirm this type of recitation, and it is an exegetical recitation. In the same Al-Durr al-Manthur, and this is the sixth volume. I only bring examples; my intention is not exhaustiveness and tracing. This is a television program; television programs do not bear exhaustiveness and tracing. And despite that, they are long programs that might tire the viewers, but what can I do? The nature of the time, the nature of the topic lead me and push me in many times to lengthen the talk. This is the sixth volume from the same edition, on page 521, in Surah al-Ahzab, in the tail of the 25th verse: "And Allah repelled those who disbelieved in their rage, they did not obtain any good. And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting, and Allah is Strong, Mighty." Page 521:

And Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir extracted from Ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, that he used to recite this harf: 'And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting by Ali ibn Abi Talib.'

This source is Sunni, this is the exegesis of Al-Durr al-Manthur by al-Suyuti, and the sources here from which he transmits, and the chains of narration, are not Shiite chains of narration. "And Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir extracted from Ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, that he used to recite this harf: 'And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting by Ali ibn Abi Talib.'"

I brought this example, and there are very many other examples, to support what came in the verse of Al-Ghadir, the verse of Tabligh: "Convey what was sent down to you... regarding Ali ibn Abi Talib" according to the recitation of Ibn Mas'ud. Meaning I wanted to say that this recitation mentioned in the verse of Tabligh did not only come once in this narration which I read, but rather other verses also came in their books with this same method of recitation. Let us call it the exegetical recitation. Even if the apparent of the narrations do not indicate exegesis, but let us call it the exegetical recitation. And the likes of this, by Allah, is abundant in their books! Abundant, and abundant, and abundant. And tomorrow I will bring you a practical example regarding the abundance of the hadiths. The third episode, our episodes are continuous, connected. Tomorrow in the third episode, God willing, I will bring you a practical example regarding the issue of the abundance of the hadiths present in the books of the people and which they pay no attention to. **Because the problem is not a problem of hadiths, nor is the problem a problem of chains of narration and sources... The problem is there is a disease. A disease in the hearts.** There is an enmity for Ali and the family of Ali. It is this problem.
Segment 6: Hisham ibn al-Hakam's Debate on the Necessity of the Imam

In this final segment, Sheikh al-Ghizzi recounts a profound theological debate preserved in Bihar al-Anwar, featuring Hisham ibn al-Hakam, a prominent companion of Imam al-Sadiq and Imam al-Kadhim. The debate takes place in the intellectual salon of Yahya ibn Khalid al-Barmaki, with the Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid secretly eavesdropping. Hisham masterfully dismantles the arguments of the Kharijites and Mu'tazilites, proving through pure logic that Allah’s justice necessitates the existence of an infallible, maximally knowledgeable, brave, and generous Imam to guide humanity. When asked to identify this Imam, Hisham fearlessly names Imam Musa al-Kadhim, infuriating Harun al-Rashid and forcing Hisham to flee for his life. The Sheikh concludes that just as Hisham demonstrated, the innate intellect and all historical evidence unequivocally point to Ali and his descendants, but the nation remains blinded by an "Umayyad virus" of deep-seated enmity.

This is the forty-eighth volume of the book **Bihar al-Anwar**. There is a very beautiful discussion that took place between Hisham ibn al-Hakam and a group of the opponents of the Ahlulbayt. I will read this debate and discussion, and after that, I will comment on some of its aspects according to what time permits, whether today in this episode or, God willing, in the coming episodes.

Sheikh al-Majlisi here transmits from Sheikh al-Saduq, from his book Kamal al-Din wa Tamam al-Ni'mah, with his chain of narration from Ibn Abi Umayr—and Ibn Abi Umayr is from the most majestic companions of the Imams—from Ali al-Aswari. He said: Yahya ibn Khalid had Yahya ibn Khalid is the Barmakid, the Barmakids who were the ministers of al-Rashid and were his advisors—Yahya ibn Khalid had a council in his house attended by the theologians from every sect and religion on Sunday, so they would debate regarding their religions and argue with one another. It was like a cultural and intellectual forum, as it is called in these days of ours.
So that reached al-Rashid, and he said to Yahya ibn Khalid: "O Abbasi, what is this council that has reached me is in your house, attended by the theologians?" So he said:

O Commander of the Believers, there is nothing of what the Commander of the Believers has raised me with, and what I have reached of honor and elevation, that has a better position with me than this council. For indeed it is attended by every people with the difference of their sects, so some argue with others, and the truthful among them becomes known, and the corruption of every sect from its sects becomes clear to us.
Al-Rashid said to him:
So I would love to attend this council and hear their speech without them knowing of my presence, lest they hold back and do not manifest their sects.

He said: "That is up to the Commander of the Believers whenever he wishes." Whenever you want, for that will be for you. He said: "So place your hand on my head..."—meaning, pledge to me, as if making him swear by his head that he will not inform the people that he is present—"So place your hand on my head and do not inform them of my presence." So he did; meaning that Yahya ibn Khalid the Barmakid did that.

And the news reached the Mu'tazilites from inside the palace. There is nothing that the slave girls, the servants, the police, and the guards keep secret; everything comes out. So the news reached the Mu'tazilites that al-Rashid would attend, but in secret. And the news reached the Mu'tazilites, so they consulted among themselves and resolved not to speak to Hisham except regarding the Imamate, for the sake of harming him. **They resolved not to speak to Hisham ibn al-Hakam except regarding the Imamate, due to their knowledge of the sect of al-Rashid, and that if he heard the speech of Hisham, he would harm Hisham, he would kill him.** Due to their knowledge of the sect of al-Rashid and his denial of anyone who professed the Imamate. We read yesterday in Al-Akhbar al-Tiwal by Abu Hanifa al-Dinawari how al-Rashid spoke to al-Asma'i regarding his two sons, al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, and that killing and shedding of blood would occur between them. So al-Asma'i said to him: "Is this from the speech of the astrologers?" He said: "No, this is speech the scholars transmitted from the Legatees, from the Prophets." Al-Ma'mun used to say in the days of his caliphate, as Abu Hanifa al-Dinawari transmitted, that al-Rashid heard this speech from Musa ibn Ja'far. **Al-Rashid knows who the Legatees are and who the Imams are, but he denies their Imamate for the sake of the chair...**

He said: So they attended, and Hisham attended, and Abdullah ibn Yazid the Ibadi attended. Ibadi, meaning from the Kharijites. And he was from the truest of people to Hisham ibn al-Hakam. Truest, meaning he was a friend to him, from the people with the most friendship, and he used to partner with him in trade. So when Hisham entered, he greeted Abdullah ibn Yazid from among them because he was a friend to him. So Yahya ibn Khalid said to Abdullah ibn Yazid, to this Ibadi who is the friend of Hisham: "O Abdullah, speak to Hisham regarding what you differed in regarding the Imamate." So Hisham said:

O Minister, they—meaning the Kharijites, the Ibadis—do not have against us an answer nor a question. These are a people who were gathered with us upon the Imamate of a man, then they separated from us without knowledge nor recognition.

Meaning, the Kharijites in their origin were with us, acknowledging the Imamate of Ali, then they separated from us. So neither when they were with us did they know the truth, nor when they separated from us did they know upon what they separated from us. So they do not have against us a question nor an answer.

So Bayan said—and Bayan was from the Haruriyyah, a sect of the Kharijites. Abdullah ibn Yazid was an Ibadi from the Ibadi sect. Ibadi in relation to Abdullah ibn Ibad, and he was a leader of the Kharijites, a leader of this sect. Haruriyyah in relation to Harura', those who rebelled in Harura' against the Commander of the Believers. So Bayan, who was from the Haruriyyah from the Kharijites, said:
Tell me about the companions of Ali on the day they appointed the two arbitrators, were they believers or disbelievers?
Hisham said: 'They were three categories...'

They were three categories: a believing category, a polytheist category, and an astray category. As for the believers, it is whoever spoke the likes of my saying, those who said that Ali is an Imam from Allah and Muawiyah is not fit for it... And as for the polytheists, they are a people who said Ali is an Imam and Muawiyah is fit for it. So they committed polytheism when they entered Muawiyah with Ali. And as for the astray, they are a people who went out for the zeal and tribalism for the tribes and clans...
He said: 'And what were the companions of Muawiyah?'...

They were three categories: a disbelieving category, a polytheist category, and an astray category. As for the disbelievers, they are those who said that Muawiyah is an Imam and Ali is not fit for it... And as for the polytheists, they are a people who said Muawiyah is an Imam and Ali is fit for it. So they associated Muawiyah with Ali, peace be upon him. And as for the astray, they are upon the path of those who went out for zeal and tribalism...

So his (Bayan's) clarification was cut off at that. It was cut off, meaning he fell silent. So Dirar said: "Then I will ask you, O Hisham, regarding this." So Hisham said: "You have erred." He said: "And why?" Dirar is from the Mu'tazilites; Bayan was from the Kharijites. So Dirar said: "Then I will ask you, O Hisham, regarding this." So Hisham said The opponents to us, if you enter the websites on the internet or even in their books, they say that the Mu'tazilites are from the Shia, or that the Shia are from the Mu'tazilites, and all of that is deception and distortion. **The Mu'tazilites are from the Sunnis and not from the Shia; return to history.** Where did the Mu'tazilites come from? The Mu'tazilites are the followers of al-Hasan al-Basri. Who is the one who founded the Mu'tazilite sect? Wasil ibn Ata'. Wasil ibn Ata' is the first Mu'tazilite. Wasil ibn Ata' is from the disciples of al-Hasan al-Basri and from his students. And al-Hasan al-Basri is from the Imams of the opponents of the Ahlulbayt.
So Dirar, and he is from the Imams of the Mu'tazilites, said: "Then I will ask you, O Hisham, regarding this." So Hisham said: "You have erred." He said: "And why?" He said:

Because you are all gathered—you Kharijites, Mu'tazilites, all of you are gathered upon repelling the Imamate of my companion... and this one has asked me about a question, and it is not for you to double up with the question against me until I ask you...
Dirar said: 'So ask.' He said:
Do you say that Allah is just and does not oppress?

He said: "Yes, He is just and does not oppress, Blessed and Exalted is He." Hisham said, he says to Dirar: "So if Allah tasked the crippled with walking to the mosques and Jihad in the path of Allah, and tasked the blind with reading the copies of the Quran and the books, do you see Him as being just or an oppressor?" Dirar said: "Allah would not do that." Hisham said:
We have known that Allah does not do that, but by way of debate and argumentation, if He were to do that, would He not be an oppressor in His action?

He said: "If He did that, He would be an oppressor." He said: "Then tell me about Allah the Mighty and Majestic. Did He task the servants with one religion in which there is no difference, and does He not accept from them except that they bring it as He tasked them?" He said: "Yes." He said:

So did He make a guide for them to the existence of that religion, or did He task them with what has no guide to its existence? For then He would be in the position of one who tasks the blind with reading books, and the crippled with walking to the mosques and Jihad.
He said: So Dirar fell silent for an hour. What could he say? Then he said:
There must be a guide, but he is not your companion.
He said: So Hisham laughed and said:
You have become Shiite by half, and you have come to the truth by necessity, and there is no difference between me and you except in the naming.
Dirar said: 'Then I will return to you in this speech...' He said: "Bring it." Dirar said:
How is the Imamate contracted?
Hisham said:
Just as Allah contracted Prophethood.

He said: "So is he a Prophet?" Hisham said: "No, because Prophethood is contracted by the people of the heaven, and the Imamate is contracted by the people of the earth. So the contract of Prophethood is by the angels, and the contract of Imamate is by the Prophet, and the contracting of all of it is by the permission of Allah the Mighty and Majestic." He said: 'So what is the proof for that?' **Hisham said: "The necessity."** It is a necessary matter. Dirar said: "And how is that?" Hisham said: "The speech regarding this is not devoid of one of three facets:

Either Allah the Mighty and Majestic lifted the task from the creation after the Messenger, so He did not task them, and did not command them, and did not forbid them, and they became in the position of beasts and cattle upon which there is no task.

Do you say this, O Dirar, that the task is lifted from the people after the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family?" He said: "I do not say this." Hisham said: 'So the second facet...'

It must be that the tasked people transformed after the Messenger into scholars in the exact same capacity as the Messenger in knowledge, until no one needs anyone, so all of them would be self-sufficient and would attain the truth in which there is no difference.

Do you say this, that the people transformed into scholars until they became in the exact same capacity as the Messenger in knowledge?" He said: "I do not say this, but rather they need others." He said: 'So the third facet remained...'
Because there must be a knowledge established for them by the Messenger, who does not forget, nor make mistakes, nor oppress.
He said: 'So what is the proof for him?'...

Eight signs: four in the description of his lineage, and four in the description of his self. As for the four that are in the description of his lineage: that he is known in race, known in tribe, known in house, and that there is a designation towards him from the Owner of the Religion and the Call.
An indication:

For no race from this creation was seen more famous than the race of the Arabs... And if it were permissible that the proof from Allah upon this creation... be in other than this race... then a lifetime of his era would pass for the seeking seeker without finding him.
And if it were permissible that he seek him among the races of this creation from the non-Arabs and others, it would be from where Allah intended it to be righteousness, it would be corruption.

So when that was not permissible, it was not permissible except that he be in this race... And since it was not permissible that he be from this race except in this tribe... it was not permissible that he be from this tribe except in this house... it was not permissible except that there be a specific designation pointing to him from the Owner of the Religion and the Call, by his person, his name, and his lineage, lest anyone else covet it.

A clear identification. He must be from the Arabs, he must be from Quraysh, he must be from the Hashemite House, there must be a clear identification, distinction, and indication. A clear deduction. This speech is not far from the deduction by which Umar ibn al-Khattab deduced... So Umar said:

Far from it! Two do not gather in a horn. By Allah, the Arabs will not be pleased to let you lead them while her Prophet is from other than you. But the Arabs do not refuse to entrust their affair to those among whom the prophethood was...

But this deduction is clear, and it can branch out. **So who is the most worthy and the closest? You, O Umar, or Ali and the family of Ali?** "If the issue was by kinship, and other than you is more worthy... So if by Shura you possessed their affairs, then how is that when the consultants were absent? And if by closeness you possessed their affairs, then other than you is more worthy of the Prophet and closer." If the issue is an issue of closeness, then the Ahlulbayt are closer!

Of course, the investigator wrote in the footnote: "And this also, its chains of narration should be looked into in its entirety." He did not look into its chains of narration, but when the speech reached this issue, the commentator commented, because it is clear! Anyone who reads this speech will say, "Then why were the Ahlulbayt distanced if the issue is an issue of closeness?" **This is the disease which I talk about and point to, and this Umayyad disease. This Umayyad virus that strikes the hearts and destroys them.**
We continue with the discussion and argumentation of Hisham, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him. He completed the argumentation:

And as for the four that are in the description of his self: That he be the most knowledgeable of all people regarding the obligations of Allah... And that he be infallible from sins... And that he be the bravest of people. And that he be the most generous of people.
He said: 'From where did you say that he is the most knowledgeable of people?'...
Because if he were not knowledgeable of all the limits of Allah... it would not be secure against him overturning the limits.
He said: 'So from where did you say that he is infallible from sin?'...
Because if he were not infallible from sins, he would enter into error, so it would not be secure that he would conceal against himself...
He said: 'So from where did you say that he is the bravest of people?'...
So if he were not brave, he would flee, and thus return with wrath from Allah, just as his (the Prophet's) companions fled.
He said: 'So from where did you say that he is the most generous of people?'...
Because he is the treasurer of the Muslims. So if he were not generous, his soul would yearn for their wealth...

So Dirar said at that: "So who is this with this description in this time?" Because he came to them with the speech of logic. This is the speech of the innate nature (Fitra), this is the speech of the conscience (Wujdan). This is the Will, this is the Ghadir, this is Ali. The Ali who points out that he is Ali.

So Dirar, and he is from the Mu'tazilites, and the Mu'tazilites had news that Harun al-Rashid was present behind the curtain. There was like a balcony in the house, and he was sitting in the balcony and in front of him was a curtain. So Dirar said at that: "So who is this with this description in this time?" **So Hisham said: "The master of the era, the Commander of the Believers."**

And Harun al-Rashid had heard the entire speech. He said: "The master of the era, the Commander of the Believers." So Harun said at that—he did not mention his name, he said the master of the era, who is the master of the era? He is the Infallible Imam.

So Harun said at that: **"By Allah, he has given us from the sack of lime!"** This is a proverb coined for the one who gives someone something that has no value... Woe to you, O Ja'far!" And Ja'far ibn Yahya was sitting. "Who does he mean by this?" He said: "O Commander of the Believers—meaning Musa ibn Ja'far." So what did Harun say? He said:
He did not mean by it anyone other than its people.
They know the truths. **He did not mean by it anyone other than its people.** Then Harun bit his lip and said:
Someone like this lives, and my kingdom remains for me for a single hour?! By Allah, the tongue of this one is more penetrating into the hearts of the people than a hundred thousand swords!

And Yahya knew that Hisham had been compromised. So he entered the curtain and said: "Woe to you, O Abbasi!" Yahya entered the curtain wanting to see what the stance of al-Rashid was. So he said: "Woe to you, O Abbasi, who is this man?" He said:
O Commander of the Believers, you are spared, you are spared, I will take care of his matter for you.

Then he went out to Hisham and nudged him a nudge. So Hisham knew that he had been compromised. So he stood up, showing them that he was going to urinate or fulfill a need... He went out fleeing to Kufa, and stayed with Bashir al-Nabbal, and he was from the bearers of the hadith from the companions of Abu Abdullah (al-Sadiq). So he informed him of the news.
Then Hisham fell severely ill. So Bashir said to him: "Shall I bring you a doctor?" He said:
No, I am dead.
So when death attended him, he said to Bashir:

When you have finished from my preparation... then carry me in the depth of the night and place me in the Kunasa... and write a note upon me and say: 'This is Hisham ibn al-Hakam whom the Commander of the Believers demanded, he died a natural death.'

He died a natural death, meaning he died a normal death, he was not killed. And Harun had sent for the brothers of Hisham and his companions, so the creation was seized because of him... So when the people of Kufa woke up, they saw him, and the judge attended, and the chief of police... and he wrote to al-Rashid regarding that. So he said: "Praise be to Allah who sufficed us his matter." So he released those whom he had seized because of him.

The discussion is clear. The discussion relied on what basis? It relied on this basis: that Allah the Glorified and Exalted tasked the servants with a religion, and this religion requires the people to act according to its obligations according to what Allah wants. And the people are upon three categories, three possibilities: either Allah did not task them, or they became scholars like the knowledge of the Messenger of Allah so they know everything about the religion of Allah, or that they were neither left without a task, nor did they reach the level of the knowledge of the Messenger of Allah. So there must be a guide pointing them to the religion of Allah.

And there are four personal specifications and characteristics: that he be the most knowledgeable of people, the bravest of people, the most generous of people, and that he be infallible from sins. **Where do we find these traits?** By Allah, Hisham mentioned a beautiful speech here, a very summarized phrase that can summarize all this speech. **He is needed, and he does not need anyone.** A word of al-Farahidi. He said: "The proof that Ali is the Imam of everyone... what is the proof that he is the Imam of everyone? **The need of everyone for him, and his independence from everyone.**" The need of everyone for him, and his independence from everyone, is a proof that he is the Imam of everyone. "He is needed, and he does not need anyone."

Is there any person in the time of the Prophet and after the Prophet, other than the Messenger of Allah, to whom these descriptions apply? And I do not demand fairness here, but rather I only demand a return to search and investigation in history, and biographies, and exegesis, and jurisprudence, and in every door from the doors of knowledge. **By Allah, all of them point to Ali. All the fingers point to Ali.** All the books, all the biographies, all the truths, all the proofs the conscience in its entirety, the logic in its entirety, and the innate nature in its entirety point to Ali!
But as we read in the Supplication of Nudba:
The nation is insistent on his enmity...

This is the completion of the second episode from the Alawi File. The continuation of the talk, God willing, comes to us continuously tomorrow, the third episode. I will complete my talk which I was not able to complete in this episode. I will meet you upon the affection of Ali and the family of Ali. I leave you in the care of Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Ali - Episode 3: The Proofs of Knowledge, Courage, and Generosity
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 3

In the third episode of **The File of Ali**, Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi explores the essential criteria for the Imam—absolute knowledge, courage, and generosity—as established through the logical proofs of Hisham ibn al-Hakam. He highlights the **deliberate intellectual distortion** practiced by opponents, specifically analyzing Ibn Hajar’s **Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah**, where historical facts are acknowledged but their meanings are systematically stripped away. To provide a definitive counter-argument, the Sheikh introduces the **massive 33-volume encyclopedia Ihqaq al-Haqq**, demonstrating that thousands of Sunni sources confirm the unique virtues of the Ahlulbayt. Finally, he conducts a comparative study of the Quran, showing that while the text records the **ignorance and flight of the Companions**, it uniquely identifies Imam Ali as the **singular acting witness** to verses of supreme knowledge and self-sacrifice.
Segment 1: Hisham's Proof and the Distortion of Al-Ghadir

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the third episode by summarizing the profound logical proof established by Hisham ibn al-Hakam in the previous episode: the Imam is the one who needs no one, yet everyone needs him. He reiterates that true leadership requires absolute knowledge, infallibility, courage, and generosity. The Sheikh argues that while the intellect naturally submits to these truths, "diseased hearts" throughout history have twisted them. To demonstrate this deliberate distortion, he introduces Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah, a famous anti-Shiite polemic by the Sunni scholar Ibn Hajar al-Haytami. The Sheikh shows how Ibn Hajar openly admits that the Hadith of Ghadir is authentic and narrated by 30 to 16 Companions, yet he deliberately manipulates the meaning of "Mawla" to merely mean "helper" or "supporter," ignoring the clear context of leadership.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the Radiant Face of Allah and His High Side. Peace be upon the ripe fruit and the brilliant star. Peace be upon the pleasing name of Allah, Abu al-Hassan Ali, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon you, O followers of Abu Turab, in every corner of existence—living and dead, young and old, men and women, elders and youth. Between your hands is the Alawi File, the third episode.

The talk reached us in the past episode in the discussion and argumentation of Hisham ibn al-Hakam in the house of the Barmakid minister within the hearing of the Abbasid caliph Harun. The summary of the speech in this argumentation is that **the Proof after the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, is the one who does not need anyone, and everyone needs him.** A proof to which the innate nature (Fitra) smiles, and the conscience rejoices, and the intellect acknowledges in submission, and the fair person surrenders to it. Is there a proof—small, summarized in its words and in the numbers of its letters, and deep in its significance—like this proof? **The Imam is the one who does not need anyone, and everyone is in need of him.** This is the essence and the core of what Hisham ibn al-Hakam uttered. As for the rest of the speech, it is detail.

I pointed to what Hisham ibn al-Hakam mentioned regarding four descriptions in the lineage of the Imam. And I also mentioned the exact same deduction by which Umar ibn al-Khattab deduced and argued against the Ansar in the Saqifa when they said "From us an Emir and from you an Emir." And he clarified to them how Quraysh is more worthy of the caliphate of the Messenger of Allah by the proof of closeness. I talked about this demand; I will not repeat what preceded of the speech. Then Hisham ibn al-Hakam mentioned descriptions, he numbered them as four, in the self of the Proof: **in knowledge, infallibility, courage, and generosity.**
Let us read lines from the argumentation and discussion of Hisham:

And as for the four that are in the description of his self: That he be the most knowledgeable of all people regarding the obligations of Allah, His traditions, and His rulings, until nothing of them is hidden from him, minute or magnificent. And that he be infallible from sins, all of them. And that he be the bravest of people. And that he be the most generous of people.
He said: "From where did you say..." The one debating Hisham is that Mu'tazilite whose name is Dirar. He said: "From where did you say that he is the most knowledgeable of people?" He said:
Because if he were not knowledgeable of all the limits of Allah, His rulings, His laws, and His traditions, it would not be secure against him overturning the limits.

And this is what occurred in the caliphate of the first, the second, and the third of the caliphs, and the clarification of that will come through the witnesses and through the proofs and the texts from the books of the people that are considered authentic by them. "It would not be secure against him overturning the limits. So he would flog the one upon whom amputation was obligatory, and he would multitask and amputate the one upon whom flogging was obligatory. So he would not establish a limit for Allah according to what He commanded by it, so it would be that from where Allah intended righteousness, corruption occurs."

He said: "So from where did you say that he is infallible from sins?" He said: "Because if he were not infallible from sins, he would enter into error, **so it would not be secure that he would conceal against himself, and conceal for his close friend and his relative.**" That he conceal... that he hide his flaws and hide what he perpetrated without accountability and without punishment. "He would enter into error, so it would not be secure that he would conceal against himself, and conceal for his close friend and his relative. And Allah the Mighty and Majestic does not establish proof... and Allah the Mighty and Majestic does not establish proof with the likes of this upon His creation."

He said: "So from where did you say that he is the bravest of people?" He said: "Because he is a refuge (fi'ah) for the Muslims to whom they return to in wars. And Allah the Mighty and Majestic said: 'And whoever turns his back to them on that day, except as a maneuver for fighting or to join a refuge (fi'ah), he has returned with wrath from Allah.' **So if he were not brave, he would flee, and thus return with wrath from Allah.** So it is not permissible that the one who returns with wrath from Allah be a proof of Allah upon His creation."

He said: "So from where did you say that he is the most generous of people?" He said: "Because he is the treasurer of the Muslims. So if he were not generous, his soul would yearn for their wealth, so he would take it, so he would be treacherous. **And it is not permissible that Allah establish proof upon His creation with a traitor.**"

Speech in the utmost of clarity. Speech in the utmost of clarification. Speech in the utmost of strength, and proof, and deduction, and demonstration. I do not believe that a human searches for the truth with this condition... but where are those who search for the truth? The searchers for the truth across history are a small few. Any searcher for the truth faces the likes of these truths and does not submit to them? That is impossible. **These are the proofs of logic, and the proofs of the intellect, and the proofs of wisdom, and the proofs of demonstrations.** As for the diseased hearts that have become accustomed to overturning the truths, it is not possible for them to submit to the truth. And therefore repeatedly I say that debate and discussion in the likes of these subjects has no benefit in it, never. Because debate does not lead to guidance.

I will bring examples and models, and I promised you yesterday that I would bring you a practical example, and I am upon my promise. This is the book **Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah**, and it is known, by Ibn Hajar al-Haytami. Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah is a book in refutation of the lovers of the Ahlulbayt. He named it thus: **The Burning Thunderbolts in Refutation of the People of Innovations and Heresy.** And of course, printed with it is a second book by the same author: **Purifying the Heart**—the heart (jinan)—**Purifying the Heart and the Tongue from the Passing Thought and Utterance of Disparaging our Master Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan.** Meaning he even demands the purification of the heart from passing a thought in the imagination that contains a deficiency for Muawiyah. Purifying the Heart and the Tongue from the Passing Thought and Utterance of Disparaging our Master Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah.

The talk in the past episode was about the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, and I talked about the encyclopedia of Al-Ghadir by our Sheikh al-Amini. And from among those whom Sheikh al-Amini mentioned among the known scholars from the scholars of the opponents to the Ahlulbayt, from those who authenticated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, is this Ibn Hajar in Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah. In the exact same book which he authored and in which he describes the Shia—in the title of the book—"in refutation of the people of innovations and heresy," he means by them the Shia. On page 63—this edition is the edition of Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, year 1999, Beirut, Lebanon—on page 63, while he is mentioning the doubts of the heretics, meaning the doubts of the Shia, the doubts of the Rafidha. From among the doubts which they bring regarding the issue of the rightfulness of Ali to the caliphate and the Will, he says:

The eleventh doubt: They claimed that from the detailed text stating explicitly the caliphate of Ali is his saying, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, on the day of Ghadir Khumm, a place in Juhfa upon his return from the Farewell Pilgrimage, after he gathered the Companions and repeated to them: 'Am I not closer to you than yourselves?' three times.
He says, and after that he authenticates the hadith. Now he transmits this hadith; he says that the Shia argue with this hadith. He accepts the hadith but he interprets it according to his whim.

...after he gathered the Companions and repeated to them: 'Am I not closer to you than yourselves?' three times, and they answer with confirmation and acknowledgment. Then he raised the hand of Ali and said: 'Whoever I am his master, then Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend the one who befriends him, and be the enemy of the one who is his enemy, so love the one who loves him, and hate the one who hates him, and support the one who supports him, and abandon the one who abandons him, and turn the truth with him wherever he turns.'

"They said"—meaning the Shia—"so the meaning of the Master (Mawla) is the closest (al-Awla), meaning so for Ali over them is from the Wilayah what belongs to him, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family," according to what he wrote. "So the meaning of the Master, meaning so for Ali over them is from the Wilayah what belongs to him from it, **by the proof of his saying: 'Am I not closer to you?' For there is no meaning for 'the supporter,' otherwise he would not have needed to gather them likewise.** With the supplication for him, because every one knows that. They said: And this supplication is not except for an infallible Imam whose obedience is obligatory. They said: So this is an explicit, authentic text regarding his caliphate. End of quote." Meaning the speech of the Shia regarding the meaning of this hadith has ended. You notice, he transmitted the speech of the Shia as it is, and he transmitted the hadith as it is, and there is nothing stronger than this speech.

He answers this doubt. He says: "And the answer to this doubt, which is the strongest of their doubts, needs an introduction, and it is the clarification of the hadith and its extractors." Its extractors, those who extracted the hadith. "And its clarification is that **it is an authentic hadith about which there is no doubt.** And a group such as al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and Ahmad extracted it, and its paths are very many. And from there, **16 Companions narrated it, and in a narration for Ahmad that 30 Companions heard it from the Prophet, and they testified to it for Ali when he was disputed in the days of his caliphate.**" What I mentioned of the Hadith of Rahba and the Hadith of the Day of the Riders, the exact same thing; he acknowledges all these meanings. "As has passed and will come. And many of its chains of narration are authentic and good. And there is no attention paid to the one who defamed its authenticity." Those who defamed its authenticity, no attention is paid to them for their lack of knowledge or for their enmity. "And there is no attention paid to the one who defamed its authenticity, nor to the one who rejected it by saying that Ali was in Yemen." Because there are those who said, as a fabrication, that Ali at this time was in Yemen, so how could he appoint him in Ghadir Khumm? "Due to the establishment of his return from there and his reaching the pilgrimage with the Prophet. And the saying of some of them that the addition 'O Allah, befriend the one who befriends him' to the end is fabricated is rejected, for that has come from paths, many of which al-Dhahabi authenticated."

"And in summary, so what they claimed is rejected." From those who claimed? The Shia. What the Shia claimed, that this hadith indicates the Wilayah, is rejected. He says: "We do not concede that the meaning of the Master (al-Wali) is what they mentioned. Rather, its meaning is the supporter." Meaning the Prophet gathered the people, and gathered the pilgrims in that heat, to say to them that Ali is your supporter. Because the Muslims did not know that Ali was a supporter for them in all the situations of war and peace? Who is the one who supported the Muslims in all situations so that it requires the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, to say to the Muslims that Ali is a supporter for you?

The point of evidence here is that he accepts the hadith, and says that the Companions narrated this hadith, and that the Companions testified for Ali to the authenticity of the pledge, to the authenticity of the hadith in the days of his caliphate. He accepts all of this, and says the chains of narration are authentic. **But he distorts the meaning of the hadith.** I do not want to comment anything; I only pass over some of what this man said so that the pictures become clear to us how truths are distorted.
Segment 2: The Shallow Logic of the Opponents of the Ahlulbayt

Sheikh al-Ghizzi reads directly from Ibn Hajar's Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah to expose the absurd and shallow logic used to elevate Abu Bakr and Muawiyah over Imam Ali. He mocks fabricated hadiths claiming Abu Bakr was the bravest at Badr despite never killing anyone, and highlights convoluted logic arguing that being the "foundation" of the city of knowledge makes Abu Bakr more knowledgeable than the Prophet himself. He also ridicules the notion that Ali and Muawiyah were just two equally valid Mujtahids. The Sheikh uses these examples to demonstrate the profound intellectual decay and shallowness of logic caused by hostility towards the Ahlulbayt.

In the same book, on page 47, I will read without comment. "From Ali, that he said: 'Tell me, who is the bravest of people?' They said: 'You.' He said: 'As for me, I have never dueled anyone except that I took my due from him—meaning I killed him, I took my right from him—but tell me about the bravest of people.' They said: 'We do not know, so who is it?' He said"—meaning Ali—"'Abu Bakr is the bravest of people. For when it was the Day of Badr, we made a shelter for the Messenger of Allah, so we said, who will be with the Messenger of Allah...'"

He (the Sheikh) interjects: There was originally no shelter or anything, nothing existed. **The Muslims did not own anything, they did not even own swords**; the number of swords did not exceed ten swords, and most of those who attended Badr did not participate in the fighting because they had no weapons, they stood as spectators, and the books of history are a witness to that.

...'who will be with the Messenger of Allah so that no one from the polytheists swoops down upon him? So by Allah, no one approached us except Abu Bakr, drawing his sword over the Messenger of Allah. No one swoops down upon him except he swoops down upon him'—meaning he killed a large number.
The Sheikh interjects: **The books of biography have not named for us a single person whom Abu Bakr killed.** I do not want to discuss this issue.

...'So this is the bravest of people.' Ali said: 'And I have seen the Messenger of Allah'—where did he see the Messenger of Allah? In Mecca before the Hijra—'And Quraish had taken him, so this one pushes him and this one shakes him roughly, and they say: Are you the one who made the gods into one God? He said: So by Allah, no one approached us'—meaning even Ali did not approach—'except Abu Bakr, striking this one, and pushing this one, and shaking this one, while he says: Woe to you! Do you kill a man because he says my Lord is Allah?' Then Ali lifted a cloak that was upon him and wept until his beard was drenched.
The Sheikh comments: **The signs of authenticity and truthfulness are clear upon the hadith!** I do not need to comment, I suffice with the understanding of the viewer.

On page 53... I mention this speech to see the strength of logic with Hisham ibn al-Hakam, which is the logic of the school of Ali, and to see **the shallowness of logic in Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah and the likes of Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah.**

Page 53: "It is not said, rather Ali is more knowledgeable than him"—in the comparison between Abu Bakr and Ali—"due to the coming report in his virtues"—the coming in the virtues of Ali: 'I am the city of knowledge and Ali is its gate'—"because we say it will come that this hadith is impugned"—a weak hadith—"and upon conceding its authenticity or goodness, then Abu Bakr is its prayer niche (mihrab)." Meaning there is a narration: 'I am the city of knowledge, and Ali is its gate, and Abu Bakr is its prayer niche.' "So whoever wants knowledge, let him come to the gate. It does not necessitate being the most knowledgeable."

He says what is in the hadith 'whoever wants knowledge let him come to the gate' does not necessitate being the most knowledgeable. "For it may be that the non-most knowledgeable is sought due to what he has of additional clarification and explanation"—meaning he has clarification but does not have knowledge. And this speech is also present even in our milieus; so-and-so is called the scholar who possesses such and such of knowledge, but he does not possess clarification. I do not know how this issue can be! Even this scholar who is said to be the certain scholar while he does not possess clarification, if he had a problem with a person, is he not able to clarify it? Yes, clarification is upon degrees, a person can be more clarifying than another, but to not possess clarification such that he cannot clarify the images present in his mind? I say if someone demanded money and this one did not pay him the money, is he not able to describe this issue? Knowledge is images in the mind, the tongue is only made as a guide to the heart. And the likes of these matters, just as people are deceived by them here, people are also deceived by them in our milieus. And this issue is not a new issue; this is an old issue, an issue as long as time.

"So whoever wants knowledge, let him come to the gate, does not necessitate being the most knowledgeable. For the non-most knowledgeable may be sought due to what he has of additional clarification, explanation, and free time for the people, contrary to the most knowledgeable. **In addition to that, this narration is opposed by the report of al-Firdaws: I am the city of knowledge, and Abu Bakr is its foundation, and Umar is its walls, and Uthman is its roof, and Ali is its gate. So this is explicit that Abu Bakr is the most knowledgeable of them.**"

And at that time, the matter... why is he the most knowledgeable of them? Because the narration said: 'and Abu Bakr is its foundation'. 'I am the city of knowledge and Abu Bakr is its foundation'. **If the foundation is the most knowledgeable, he will be more knowledgeable than the Prophet at that time, because he is the foundation of the city!** "So this is explicit that Abu Bakr is the most knowledgeable of them. And at that time, the command to aim for the gate is only for the likes of what we said, not for the addition of his honor over what preceded him, because it is necessarily known that each of the foundation, the walls, and the roof are higher than the gate."

What explanation for the hadiths is this? And what hadiths are these? He says: "And some of them deviated"—some of their scholars, what did he say? He said: Originally 'and Ali is its gate' does not mean Ali, but rather 'and its gate is high' (wa babuha 'ali). Only 'Abu Bakr is its foundation, Umar its walls, Uthman its roof, and its gate is high (wa ali babuha, meaning wa babuha ali).' It is not a name for Ali! He says: "And some of them deviated and answered that the meaning of wa Ali babuha is from highness (uluw)." Meaning he, may Allah reward him with good, rejects this speech. The hadith is thus: "I am the city of knowledge, and Abu Bakr is its foundation, and Umar is its walls, and Uthman is its roof, and Ali is its gate."

As for in his book Purifying the Heart and the Tongue from the Passing Thought and Utterance of Disparaging our Master Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan, on page 25, while he is talking about the praise of Ali for Muawiyah. He says: "And from them"—from the proofs that point to the virtue of Muawiyah—"And from them is the praise of Ali, may Allah honor his face, upon him, upon Muawiyah, by his saying: My slain and the slain of Muawiyah are in Paradise."

The story that I mentioned, the story of Sayyid Abd al-Husayn Sharaf al-Din, may Allah's mercy be upon him, when he visited Hujr ibn Adi. This servant, the caretaker who was at the shrine of Hujr ibn Adi, who answered the Sayyid and said: "This is the grave of our master Hujr ibn Adi, may Allah be pleased with him and satisfy him, killed by our master Muawiyah, may Allah be pleased with him and satisfy him, because he was upon the religion of Ali, upon the religion of our master Ali, may Allah the Exalted be pleased with him and satisfy him."

"My slain and the slain of Muawiyah are in Paradise, narrated by al-Tabarani with a chain whose men are trusted, despite disagreement regarding some of them. So this is an explicit statement from Ali." This speech from Ali is explicit! **"It does not accept an interpretation other than that Muawiyah is a Mujtahid in whom the conditions of Ijtihad were fulfilled, which obligate the prohibition of following (Taqlid) another."** I don't know, perhaps he also has a commentary on the Urwa al-Wuthqa! "That Muawiyah is a Mujtahid in whom the conditions of Ijtihad were fulfilled, which obligate the prohibition of following another. For it is not permissible for a Mujtahid to follow a Mujtahid by consensus, whether he opposed him in his Ijtihad, which is clear, or agreed with him. Because each one only took what he said from the proof, nothing else."

But he did not clarify who is the most knowledgeable. Ali is a Mujtahid and Muawiyah is also a Mujtahid! "Muawiyah is a Mujtahid in whom the conditions of Ijtihad were fulfilled, which obligate the prohibition of following another." **This book and its likes are packed with the likes of this shallowness of logic with which the author of Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah speaks.** And this is present all along the line in the two directions: in the direction whose beginnings start from Ali, and in the direction whose beginnings start from those who opposed Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.
Segment 3: The Story of Ihqaq al-Haqq and the Third Martyr

Sheikh al-Ghizzi shifts to a powerful "practical example" of the undeniable evidence for the Wilayah by pointing to a massive 33-volume encyclopedia on his desk: Ihqaq al-Haqq (The Vindication of Truth). He recounts the fascinating history behind this work, starting in the 8th Hijri century with the Mongol Sultan Uljaytu Khan, who converted to Shiism after Allamah al-Hilli logically dismantled the absolute authority of the four Sunni schools of jurisprudence. This led to a chain of theological refutations, culminating in the Indian subcontinent where Qazi Nurullah al-Shushtari authored Ihqaq al-Haqq to defend Shiism. The Sheikh vividly describes Qazi Nurullah's tragic martyrdom—tortured to death with thorny whips and boiling fire by the Mughal Emperor Jahangir for writing this very book. He concludes by emphasizing that this monumental 33-volume set, expanded by Ayatollah Mar'ashi Najafi, contains over a thousand Sunni sources entirely confirming the virtues and leadership of the Ahlulbayt.

I said I would bring a practical example. The practical example which I wanted to clarify to you, and after that I will return to the discussion of Hisham ibn al-Hakam... this massive collection of books which you see in front of me. There is a collection in front of the table, in front of this desk, a clear collection of books, and this collection too, all of it is one book! And it is the book **Ihqaq al-Haqq** by the Third Martyr, Sayyid al-Mar'ashi, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him, **al-Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari**. You notice, this is not a book; it is a complete library. All those books which are in front of the desk, and all these books, this collection, this line of books, all of it is one book. This is the last part, and it is the index, this is the 33rd part. You notice in front of the desk the books reach in their sequence to the 32nd part. **This is the book Ihqaq al-Haqq, 33 volumes of the large format, from the large size. A complete library.** Perhaps some of the viewers know this book, have read it, have seen it, have heard of it, have heard about it. I talk to you today about the story of this book, the book Ihqaq al-Haqq wa Izhaq al-Batil (The Vindication of Truth and the Crushing of Falsehood). Let us return to centuries, let us return to the eighth Hijri century, let us return to distant centuries.

The Mongol Sultan Uljaytu Khan, who converted to Islam upon the Sunni sect and named himself Muhammad, Uljaytu Khan Muhammad, the Mongol Sultan whose seat of power was in Iran. One day he became angry at his wife, so he divorced her. He divorced her thrice. And according to the jurisprudence of the sects opposing the Ahlulbayt, the divorce occurs thrice; the divorce becomes irrevocable. And I believe that many of you, from the lovers of the Ahlulbayt, know this story. How the Mongol Sultan gathered the jurists of the sects and requested from them to find him a solution to return his wife. And all of them said that the divorce had occurred irrevocably because he said to her "I have divorced you thrice." And this jurisprudential issue is known; the methodology of the Ahlulbayt is known, and the methodology of others is known. They said to him: "There must be a legalizer (muhallil); a person must marry her and must consummate the marriage with her." The Mongol Sultan refused that. He said: "Search for me for a jurist who speaks contrary to your saying." So some of them said: "There is a jurist in Iraq, in Hillah"—they mean by him **Allamah al-Hilli**. So the scholars sitting in the presence of the Sultan sharpened their tongues and said: "This is a man of little intellect. This is from the Rafidha, and the Rafidha are a people with no religion. He has little intellect and understanding." The Sultan said: "Bring him to me. I want him, I want to hear from him."

So Allamah al-Hilli was brought to the house of the Sultan, to the palace of the Sultan. And when Allamah al-Hilli entered, first, he did not kneel and bow between the hands of the Sultan. And second, he sat next to the Sultan, because those who were in the presence of the Sultan arranged the council such that they did not leave a place for the Allamah to sit in. And when he entered, he took off his sandals, he took off his sandals from his two feet, gathered them both, and took them both with him. So the people laughed, and pointed to the Sultan, they said to him: "Did we not tell you, Your Highness the Sultan, that the man is of little intellect? First, he did not greet the Sultan with the greeting of the Sultans, so he did not kneel and step down. And second, he sat next to the Sultan, and this is against custom. And third, he carried his sandals in his two hands."

So he said: "Ask him." And the translator translated, he did not know Arabic. So they said to him: "Why did you not greet the Sultan as Sultans are greeted?" He said: "Is he more honorable or the Messenger of Allah? The Messenger of Allah, when people entered upon him, they did not kneel to him nor bow to him; they greeted him with the greeting of Islam, and the people entered upon the Messenger of Allah." They said: "So why did you crowd the Sultan in his council?" He said: "You did not leave me a place; I did not find a place to sit in, so I did not find a sitting place except near him, so I sat near him." They said: "So why did you come with your sandals?" He said: **"It was said to us that Abu Hanifa, and that al-Shafi'i, and Ibn Hanbal, and that Malik stole the sandals of the Prophet."** So they laughed! They said: "These men were born after more than 100 years, the first of them was born after more than 100 years!"

So he turned to the Sultan, to the translator, he said: "Tell the Sultan"—because the Allamah was aware of the problem, the issue of the divorce—he said: **"Tell the Sultan: Then from where did they bring this jurisprudence? And why did they restrict jurisprudence to these four? And why did they take only from these four who were not born except after the many prolonged years after the death of the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family? As for me, I take my religion from Ali, who is the self of the Messenger of Allah, and from his children one after another."**

So he asked him: "What is your opinion regarding the divorce?" He said: "The divorce is invalid. Not because you said thrice or said once, but because the divorce requires for its validity the presence of witnesses. And you divorced her while you were sitting with her in your private room; there were no witnesses at all. No divorce occurred, neither one divorce nor three divorces." And he entered with them into a discussion regarding this issue. The Sultan was amazed by him, and the Sultan became a Shiite. And he was called Khodabande in the Persian language, meaning "The Servant of Allah." In their books, the opponents of the Ahlulbayt, when they talk about Uljaytu Khan, they call him Kharbande and not Khodabande, after he became a Shiite. Khar in the Persian language means donkey, Kharbande. In some books, I saw Khirabande, meaning the servant of feces, after he became a Shiite, they began to call him by these names.

Uljaytu Khan requested from Allamah al-Hilli to write a book for him summarizing in it all the true beliefs. Allamah al-Hilli wrote a concise book for the Mongol Sultan who became a Shiite, and it is his book Kashf al-Haqq wa Nahj al-Sidq (The Uncovering of Truth and the Path of Honesty). Refutations were written against this book. From among the refutations that were written against this book, there is a refutation by one of the famous scholars of the opponents, and he is al-Fadl ibn Ruzbahan. Fadl ibn Ruzbahan wrote a book in refutation of Allamah al-Hilli titled Ibtal al-Batil (The Nullification of Falsehood). The book of the Allamah is Kashf al-Haqq wa Nahj al-Sidq. Fadl ibn Ruzbahan's book was Ibtal al-Batil, meaning the nullification of what Allamah al-Hilli mentioned of truth in his book.

Al-Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him, was in India. So he wrote a book refuting Fadl ibn Ruzbahan, and it is this book **Ihqaq al-Haqq** (The Vindication of Truth). But it was not with this massiveness; I am now in the process of clarifying the story. Al-Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him, wrote his book Ihqaq al-Haqq, and he was at that time in India. Because he migrated from Iran, from Khorasan around the year 993 of the Hijrah, and reached the city of Lahore. And there was an Islamic emirate, an emirate for the Muslims, not Shiite, in Lahore and the areas neighboring it; its Sultan was Jalaluddin Akbar Shah the Timurid.

And al-Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari used to conceal his Shiism (practice Taqiyya). And when the Chief Justice died, the Sultan was amazed by Sayyid Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari, so he offered him to be the Chief Justice. Therefore, he was named al-Qadi (The Judge) Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari. And he used to judge and issue rulings according to the rulings of the four schools of thought. But he would search for whichever opinion in these schools is closest to the method of the Ahlulbayt, and he would rule by it. His status rose among the people and with the Sultan. Those who envied him increased, his haters increased, and they began to lay in wait for him.

One day, when the name of Ali, prayers of Allah be upon him, was mentioned, he said "Upon him be prayers and peace." So an uproar was raised against him, and the scholars issued a fatwa making his blood permissible. All the scholars who were in the presence of the Sultan, Akbar Shah the Timurid, issued a fatwa making his blood permissible. Except one of them, when he was given the paper which the scholars signed, he wrote this verse of poetry, a verse that is a mixture between Arabic, Persian, and Sanskrit. He wrote thus, he said: Qurra lahmuka lahmi bi-hadithi nabiyha / bi-salli 'ala nami Ali bi-adabha. His intention, he says: "If this hadith, 'O Ali, your flesh is my flesh and your blood is my blood, and faith is mixed with your flesh and your blood as it mixed with my flesh and my blood...' This is a Sunni scholar saying: "If this hadith is a prophetic hadith, then if we mention Ali and do not send blessings upon him, then that is from bad manners." Bi-salli 'ala nami Ali bi-adabha: if we mention Ali without sending blessings upon him, then that is from a lack of manners, from bad manners. The Timurid Sultan was amazed by the speech of this man, and Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari was spared with the Sultan.

But the people remained weaving plots against him. They planted a person among his students, and he took, little by little, until Qadi Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari began to reveal secrets to him. From among his secrets, he was writing this book, writing the book Ihqaq al-Haqq. This treacherous student began to copy and transcribe what the Qadi wrote, until he completed the book, so he handed it over to the evil scholars. They brought the book and presented it to the Sultan. The father Sultan had died, and his son Jahangir came, Jahangir Khan, Jahangir Shah the Timurid. So they gathered around him and clarified to him what Sayyid Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari wrote, and they agreed upon killing him.

But he was killed in a heinous way. They stripped him of his clothes, then they struck him with thorny whips, whips containing thorns. **They placed him in one of the public squares and released the people upon him, and the people were carrying in their hands branches from thorny trees, branches of thorns.** They struck him on his body from his head to his feet until the flesh of his body scattered. Then they came with a large pot, a brass pot, and they placed fire in it until this pot became a piece of embers. **They placed it on the head of Sayyid Nurullah al-Mar'ashi al-Shushtari. The historians mention that his brain began to boil.** He was killed in this way. Now he has a shrine in the area of Akbarabad in India and people visit him. He is called the Third Martyr.
