title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 1
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 5

In the fifth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi transitions from the introductory discussions on methodologies to the core of Islamic theology: **The Study of Monotheism (Tawhid)**. He begins by reaffirming that all philosophical or theological methods are only valid if they align perfectly with the teachings of the Ahlulbayt. The Sheikh outlines the upcoming curriculum, which will cover the existence of God, His Unity, His Attributes, and Divine Justice. He then conducts a deep critique of the traditional arguments used to prove God's existence. He dissects the **Theological Method (Mutakallimun)**, which argues that a changing universe requires an eternal creator, and the **Naturalist Method (Hukama Tabi'iyyin)**, which uses physics and motion to prove a Prime Mover. The Sheikh demonstrates that both methods are fundamentally flawed because they attempt to prove the Creator through the creation. Instead, he elevates the discussion to the divine paradigm of the Infallibles, famously echoed by Imam al-Hussein, asserting that **God is the most evident reality and must be known through Himself, not through inferior logical deductions.**
Segment 1: Recap of Methodologies and the Standard of the Ahlulbayt

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens by summarizing the eight methods of acquiring belief discussed in previous episodes. He reiterates that the chosen method for this series is to evaluate all intellectual, philosophical, and mystical approaches strictly through the lens of the Ahlulbayt's teachings. He illustrates this with the example of "Will and Volition," showing how philosophers err by considering them attributes of God's essence, whereas the Imams clearly define them as attributes of His action.
In the name of God, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to God, and blessings be upon the Messenger of God and his family.

In the past days, before speaking in the introduction to the lessons on belief and doctrine, we first spoke in the introduction regarding scientific truth. We reached the path of Divine Sciences through theoretical observation and through looking at life. We considered those who lived before us without relying on scriptural texts, without relying on the Book, and without the narrations of the Infallibles, prayers of Allah upon them all. If you remember, in the early parts of our invitation, the speech was regarding this.

Then we transitioned in the lessons that followed this to a rapid study of the paths taken by the people of knowledge in reaching the knowledge of the intellect. We addressed the theological method and clarified the meaning of this method and the evidences upon which it relies. We explained it thoroughly and there is no time to repeat it now. We addressed the science of theology, the philosophical method, the Sufi method, and the mystical method. We also addressed the method adopted by Sheikh Ahmad al-Ahsa'i in his treatises and books in reaching the knowledge of belief. Then we addressed the method taken by some scholars of hadith, compiling the honorable narrations. And we addressed the esoteric method taken by some people of hidden gnosis through the science of numbers and letters in extracting beliefs.

We finally addressed the eighth method, which is the method we adopt in studying the truth. **We agree with what came in these methods—the philosophical, theological, and mystical methods—on the condition that it conforms to what is stated in the words of the Infallibles.** I mentioned some examples where the philosophical method differs. If you recall, the philosophers cross over to the Divine Will and Volition as being from the Attributes of Essence. Meanwhile, in the narrations of the Ahlulbayt, the Will and Volition are from the Attributes of Action.

We will discuss the Attributes of God Almighty in the study of Monotheism, God willing. But regarding a profound issue like this, which is famous among them, we side with the Ahlulbayt. If the speech of the philosopher is acceptable, it is not because it is their own speech, but because it aligns with the acceptable speech we hold. The speech is accepted because it represents the reality of the divine speech. But if it differs, we abandon it. The Book and the Prophet's progeny are intertwined. Therefore, when addressing them in the honorable visitation, whoever wants to be with them must draw from the spring of their life and evaluate matters through the prayers of Allah upon them.
This meaning was clarified to the people. We can summarize this path in that segment of the profound supplication read after Ziyarat Ashura:
I have no end beyond You.

Beyond God, the ultimate end is with them, prayers of Allah upon them all. We conclude the introduction regarding the importance of Islamic gnosis and the study of beliefs, having cast a rapid glance at the methods used by the people of knowledge in reaching the correct beliefs.

When I say our lessons on belief are about the Imami beliefs, the research is not detailed in every single aspect. I try to summarize the speech and cast a rapid glance at some of the demands. I try to simplify the phrasing because many brothers may not be accustomed to philosophical or theological terminology. I try as much as possible, even with philosophical proofs if necessity dictates, to present them in simple terms so the meaning is clear. Their phrases might be obscured by complex terminologies that are unclear to those not accustomed to the Arabic phrasing in Iraq.
Segment 2: Introduction to Monotheism and the Evident Truth of God

The Sheikh transitions to the primary subject of the series: Monotheism (Tawhid). He outlines the curriculum, which will include the existence of God, His Unity, His Attributes, and Divine Justice (predestination and free will). He notes that while philosophers and theologians usually start by trying to prove God's existence, the Ahlulbayt teach that God's existence is overwhelmingly self-evident. He quotes Imam al-Hussein's Supplication of Arafah to demonstrate that God does not need creation to serve as proof of His existence.

God willing, in the first stage of the studies of beliefs, which is Monotheism, we will try as much as possible to limit ourselves to the essential demands. I will highlight the important demands. The research will not be a detailed philosophical discussion of every proof and every objection. We will only review them, and if an objection is raised, we will address it briefly. We are trying to manage our time because if we remain at this pace, we need to study more titles in the Islamic belief. We are trying in these lessons to cast a glance at the important titles in the true belief of the sect of the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah upon them.

Regarding the study of Monotheism, those who wrote in this chapter—from the theologians or the philosophers—often placed an introduction regarding the existence of the Creator. They consider this an introduction, not the core of Monotheism. They believe entering into Monotheism occurs only after knowing the existence of the Creator. Some made this research into the causes of the Creator's existence and His presence an introduction to the demand of Monotheism. We will try to include this chapter as well.

In the study of Monotheism, we will first address the subject of His existence, the Exalted. We will address the subject of His Unity, the Exalted. We will address the subject of the Attributes of Action and the Attributes of Essence. We will also address the studies of Justice, establishing it as a branch of Monotheism. This includes destiny and decree, predestination and free will, and the manifestations upon the truth in Justice. These discussions are intrinsically linked ideologically and scientifically to the study of Monotheism.

We will address this introduction which some philosophers and theologians make as a prerequisite to entering the study of Monotheism. Regarding the discussion of the existence of the Creator, for us, if we want to speak, the discussion must be in accordance with the Ahlulbayt. But for the sake of scientific benefit and so that you have a clear picture of the demands mentioned in the ideological books, we will address the issues mentioned by theologians today and some issues mentioned by philosophers.

**Otherwise, we are not in need of researching to prove the existence of God!** We are in need of the cognitive path of the Ahlulbayt. The Master of Martyrs, Imam al-Hussein, in his supplication of Arafah, says:

How can You be inferred through that whose very existence relies on You? Does anything else possess a manifestation that You lack, such that it would become the manifestation for You? When were You ever absent so that You would need a proof to point to You? And when were You ever far so that the traces would be the things leading to You? Blind is the eye that does not see You watching over it.

This is the path of the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah upon them all, in knowing God Almighty for research. This issue is found in what the Ahlulbayt mentioned in their supplications and intimate conversations. It is in the honorable scriptural texts found in our various true books. However, since this issue is researched by theology and philosophy, we will address it briefly so you have an understanding of it.
Segment 3: The Theological Proof of God and Its Flaws

Sheikh al-Ghizzi dissects the primary argument used by the Mutakallimun (theologians) to prove God's existence. The theologians argue that the universe is constantly changing (and therefore created), and every created thing requires a Creator who must be eternal. The Sheikh explains how this argument relies on disproving "Infinite Regress" (Tasalsul) and "Circular Reasoning" (Dawr). He then highlights the profound flaw in this method as pointed out by higher philosophers and the Ahlulbayt: it attempts to prove the Eternal Creator by looking at the flawed, created universe, which is an inferior and backwards way of attaining true divine knowledge.

The third point regarding those who researched this issue can be summarized in the paths taken. There is the path of the theologians, the path of the naturalists, and the path of the mystics. Regarding the theologians, their proof for the existence of the Creator is that they said:
The universe is changing, and every changing thing is created (Hadith). Every created thing must have a creator (Muhdith). And this creator must be eternal (Qadim).

This proof branches out into the issue of the invalidity of circular reasoning (Dawr) and the invalidity of infinite regress (Tasalsul), proving that this creator is the Eternal, the Necessary Existent, and the Creator, the Exalted. According to what they mentioned, what is the intent of their speech? The theologians say we found ourselves in this existence, and everything around us changes. Look at a human; he begins as a fetus, comes out as an infant, changes into a child, then an adolescent, and reaches maturity. The term adolescent in our jurisprudential terminology means one who is close to maturity, while in contemporary psychology it means one who has reached puberty. Everything around us changes. The human changes every day.

Since everything changes, they said the universe is changing. When they say the universe is created (Hadith), they mean it is changing and did not previously exist. Since it is changing, there must be a force that causes this change and creation. And this force must be constant and unchanging. Therefore, there must be a creator.

If this creator was also created, it leads to the invalidity of infinite regress (Tasalsul). The invalidity of infinite regress is one of the philosophical axioms. Just as one plus one equals two is an axiom in mathematics that no one disputes, the invalidity of infinite regress is an axiom in the rational sciences. Infinite regress is false. Circular reasoning (Dawr) is also false. Circular reasoning means that the existence of the first thing depends on the second, and the second depends on the first. This is blatantly false.

The theologians say that the universe is changing, so it needs a creator. This creator must be eternal (Qadim). The term eternal for theologians means something that does not change and is not subject to alterations. If the creator was also changing, he would need another creator, leading to infinite regress or circular reasoning. Thus, there must be an eternal creator who originated this universe.

If you review all the books written in the Islamic library, you will find that theologians consider this proof as their strongest argument. It is their primary rational proof. However, the philosophers consider this proof to be inferior. The philosophers say this proof is flawed because the one using it looks at the creations to prove the Creator. **A proof whose starting point is the creation is a flawed proof in the eyes of the philosophers.**
The philosophers say that God does not lack evidence; rather, there is no direct proof upon Him from creation. He points to Himself by Himself. As the Morning Supplication states:
O He who guided to His essence through His essence, and transcends being homogenous with His creations.

The theologians start their knowledge from the creation, which is an inferior epistemological starting point. Furthermore, the philosophers critique the theologians saying that while they proved the universe has an eternal creator, they failed to prove that this eternal creator is the "Necessary Existent" (Wajib al-Wujud). They have to add more arguments to prove that this eternal being is indeed God.
The Ahlulbayt teach us not to start from the creation. As Imam al-Hussein said:
When were You ever absent so that You would need a proof to point to You?

Starting from the traces and creations is a distant and inferior path. Pondering the creation and history is acceptable for general reflection, but in the realm of true divine knowledge and ideological deduction, it falls short. The theological proof is critiqued because it forces the intellect to leap from the created to the Creator without bridging the gap efficiently.
Segment 4: The Naturalist/Philosophical Proofs of God

The Sheikh moves to the arguments posed by the Naturalist Philosophers (Hukama Tabi'iyyin), which roughly correlates to early physicists or natural scientists. He explains three of their main arguments: The Argument of Motion (everything moving needs a prime, stationary mover), the Argument of Simplicity and Composition (composite physical bodies need a composer to assemble their parts), and the Argument of the Soul (the distinct characteristics and potentials of human bodies require an external specifier).

Regarding the expression "Naturalist Philosophers" (Hukama Tabi'iyyin), the term "Naturalists" can sometimes refer to materialists who deny the existence of God and attribute creation merely to nature. However, it also refers to the philosophers who specialized in the study of nature, physics, and the natural world, originating from ancient Greece. The study of the nature of material things and the laws governing the physical world is what is currently called the science of physics.

In ancient philosophy, a philosopher had to study physics, mathematics, and ethics as part of philosophy. Today, these sciences have separated into specialized fields. But historically, the Naturalist Philosophers looked at physical and natural phenomena to construct their proofs for God.

Their first proof is based on motion and rest. They looked at the universe and found movement everywhere, from celestial bodies to the smallest creatures. They argued that this movement is evidence of change and instability. **A moving thing inevitably requires a mover that possesses the power to cause motion.** They concluded that the universe is moving, and it must have a mover. This mover must be entirely stationary and perfectly stable; He is the one who grants motion but does not move Himself. If the mover was also moving, it would again lead to the impossible loops of circular reasoning or infinite regress. Therefore, there must be a prime, stationary mover, which is the Eternal Creator.

Their second proof is the issue of simplicity and composition. They looked at the physical bodies around us and saw that they are composite structures. A human body, for example, is composed of flesh, blood, and bone. They argued that a composite thing must have a composer because its parts need to be assembled in a specific order. The parts of a composite entity rely on each other. Before this composition existed, there had to be a composer who is utterly simple (non-composite) to bring these parts together.

Their third proof stems from human biology and the soul. They stated that all physical bodies are capable of division in three dimensions: length, width, and depth. They observed that while human bodies share these physical characteristics, they exhibit distinct behaviors, thoughts, and potentials. Since the physical bodies are fundamentally similar in their material dimensions, the distinct functions and life force (the soul) must come from an external source. There must be a specifier (Murajjih) who grants these specific qualities to specific bodies. To have a specification without a specifier is rationally impossible. Therefore, there must be a divine Specifier.

These are the proofs of the Naturalist Philosophers. They are similar to the theological proofs because they also start from the creation (motion, composition, the body) to prove the Creator. The philosophers apply the same critique here: these arguments are lengthy, complex, and still rely on the inferior method of starting from the effect to find the cause.
Segment 5: The Divine Proof and Conclusion

In the final segment, Sheikh al-Ghizzi introduces the superior, divine method of knowing God, often referred to by higher mystics and philosophers as the "Proof of the Truthful" (Burhan al-Siddiqin). This method argues from the Cause (God) to the effect (creation), rather than vice versa. He explains that true certainty only comes from knowing the Cause directly. The Sheikh concludes the episode by announcing the schedule for upcoming lessons.

The third and most elevated method is what the higher philosophers and mystics call the "Proof of the Truthful" (Burhan al-Siddiqin). They assert that there is indeed a proof upon God, but it is God Himself.
O He who guided to His essence through His essence, and transcends being homogenous with His creations.
This meaning is also profoundly expressed in the supplication of Abu Hamza al-Thumali and in numerous narrations that say:
Know God through God, and know the Messenger through the Message.

This higher proof generates true certainty. In logical terms, there is the Burhan Inni (arguing from the effect to the cause) and the Burhan Limmi (arguing from the cause to the effect). The philosophers state that arguing from the effect to the cause (like the theologians and naturalists do) does not yield absolute certainty because the effect only reveals a limited aspect of the cause. However, **arguing from the Cause to the effect yields absolute certainty because knowing the complete Cause naturally encompasses the knowledge of its effects.** The philosophers critique the theologians for using the inferior Burhan Inni, starting from the created universe to prove God. The "Proof of the Truthful" utilizes the superior Burhan Limmi, starting the epistemological journey from God Himself. True certainty and true knowledge are achieved when the intellect and the heart recognize God directly, without needing the universe as a middleman.

I do not want to dive deeper into these complex logical labyrinths and waste our time on endless philosophical differences. I wanted to present these summaries so that you have a clear understanding of the theological and philosophical statements regarding the existence of God. If we were to analyze every detail, it would take an immensely long time to cover all these partialities and similarities.
As for the divine, non-philosophical proof, whether you call it the theological, rhetorical, or mystical proof, we will rely on the teachings of the Ahlulbayt.

We will conclude here for today. The time for the lesson has extended beyond its usual limit. Because it is the month of Ramadan and the weather is intensely hot, there might be a change in the schedule. We may move the lesson to Thursday night. But for now, if I am present, we will continue our daily schedule. On Thursday night, after the prayer and the call to prayer, we will hold an ethics lesson. I will try to accommodate the schedule for the youth on Fridays as well.
After all of this, our final prayer is praise be to God, the Lord of the worlds.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 2
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 6

In the sixth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues the foundational study of **Monotheism (Tawhid)**, delving deeper into the proofs for God's existence and transitioning to the profound concept of humanity's intrinsic need for the Divine. He systematically reviews the theological and philosophical arguments (the Mutakallimun, Naturalists, and Philosophers), acknowledging their logical utility while reiterating their limitations, as they attempt to prove the Creator through the created. The Sheikh then elevates the discussion to the supreme proof: the innate disposition (Fitrah) of human deficiency. By drawing on the Quran and the supplications of the Ahlulbayt, specifically Imam al-Hussein's supplication on the Day of Arafah, he demonstrates that **humanity’s existential poverty and constant state of "brokenness" are the most profound, undeniable proofs of the Absolutely Self-Sufficient Creator (Al-Ghani).** Finally, he emphasizes the absolute prohibition of trying to comprehend God's Essence (Dhat), directing believers instead to reflect upon His manifest attributes and the Ahlulbayt themselves.
Segment 1: Recap of Theological and Naturalist Proofs

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by summarizing the arguments presented in the previous episode regarding the existence of God. He reviews the Theological Method (Mutakallimun), which argues that the changing universe requires an eternal creator. He then recaps the three main arguments of the Naturalist Philosophers: the argument of motion (requiring a prime mover), the argument of composition (requiring a simple composer), and the argument of distinct physical properties (requiring an external specifier). The Sheikh notes that while these proofs are logically sound for general understanding, they are fundamentally limited because they start from the flawed creation to prove the perfect Creator.

In the name of God, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to God, and blessings be upon the Messenger of God and upon the family of God, and curses be upon their enemies and the enemies of their weapons, the enemies of God, until the Day of Resurrection.

We advanced, and our progression reached the initiation of the first study of the blessed beliefs, which is Monotheism (Tawhid). The first issue and the first speech we mention in this stage relates to His existence, the Exalted. Last night, we discussed the proof of the theologians (Mutakallimun), which is their statement:
The universe is changing, and it is created (Hadith). And for every created thing, there must be an eternal creator (Muhdith Qadim).

I mentioned that the philosophers critique this proof because it starts from the creation to the Creator. On the one hand, and on the other hand, it requires completion because the conclusion of this proof is only that this universe has an eternal creator; it does not prove that this creator is the "Necessary Existent" (Wajib al-Wujud). Therefore, another proof is needed to establish that.

The theologians consider it a virtue in this proof that it distinguishes between the universe and God, because by establishing that the universe is changing and created, the universe is recognized as distinct from God. This is unlike the philosophical proof, which requires clarifying the distinction through another proof, the proof of the philosophers that the universe is a contingent existence.

We also mentioned the proofs of the Naturalist Philosophers (Hukama Tabi'iyyin). By Naturalists, we mean the philosophers who specialized in studying natural, physical phenomena. I mentioned their proofs. The first proof is the proof of motion and rest:
These existing creations are moving, so they must have a mover.
The issue of the invalidity of circular reasoning (Dawr) and infinite regress (Tasalsul) applies whether in the theological explanation or in this proof held by the Naturalist Philosophers.

The second is the issue of composition and simplicity. These creations and existences are composite. Therefore, they must have a separator who originated this composition within them and assembled them. Peace also leads to the invalidity of circular reasoning and infinite regress, so there must be a simple entity that was the cause of this composition.

The third proof, which we mentioned briefly, is that these physical creations exist in our world, these bodies in the philosophical sense. We said that a body, philosophically, is that which is capable of division in its dimensions. Meaning, these existing creations that we see and live with around us share physicality, yet they are distinct. This distinction present in them requires a specifier (Murajjih). The issues of infinite regress and circular reasoning, often raised by philosophers, are heavily debated in the streets, perhaps more so than in strict scientific circles.

There might also be objections regarding these deductions, with significant differences among scholars. However, as a result, these proofs in their overall form are acceptable. Through them, the human can arrive at a sound belief regarding the existence of the Creator, the Exalted. Starting from the truth in the previous indications mentioned, one can wait upon the proofs of the Naturalist Philosophers. The thing considered good in the proof of peace is near to existing, and the court is close to the speech here. So, what remains is the research into the issue of the philosophers, the philosophical path.

They argue that the contingent, changing existences around us absolutely require an inherently Necessary Existent to bring them out of non-existence, as infinite regress is logically impossible.

The philosophers state there is no absolute proof for God; rather, He points to His Essence by His Essence. title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 3

content.text: Overview: Episode 7

In the seventh episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the initial exploration of proving God's existence and transitions to a critical epistemological question: **Can the human mind truly know or define God?** He thoroughly deconstructs the limits of human comprehension, explaining that the human being is inherently **"limited" (Mahdud)** and therefore absolutely incapable of defining the **"Unlimited" (La Mahdud)**. By dismantling the logical mechanisms of definition (Genus, Differentia, Matter, and Form), he proves that God cannot be subjected to human categorization. The Sheikh then contrasts superficial, intellectual knowledge with **Presential Knowledge (Ma'rifah Huduriyyah)**, which is the direct witnessing of the heart. He explains that while the intellect can only grasp a mere "shadow" of the Divine, the purified heart can receive divine illumination, though even the greatest Prophets remain incapable of piercing the **absolute, hidden Essence of God (Al-Ghayb Al-Mutlaq)**.
Segment 1: Recap of Previous Proofs and Presential Knowledge (Ma'rifah Huduriyyah)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by summarizing the discussions from the previous sessions regarding the various methodologies used to acquire belief. He recaps the theological, philosophical, and esoteric approaches to proving God's existence. He then highlights the ultimate and purest method: the path of witnessing by the heart, which philosophers call "Presential Knowledge". He cites the profound words of Imam al-Hussein on the Day of Arafah to demonstrate this direct, unmediated witnessing of the Divine.
Prayers be upon the Messenger of Allah and his family, upon Allah, and curses be upon their enemies, the enemies of their Shia, the enemies of Allah, until the Day of Encounter.

In the past nights, the speech was presented as an introduction to the beliefs and doctrines carried by the human. We then addressed the first stage of the studies of beliefs, which is Monotheism (Tawhid). We initiated the first issue of the studies of Monotheism, which is the discourse on His existence. We mentioned in this issue, or in this section, the evidences brought forth by the theologians (Mutakallimun) and the Naturalist Philosophers, the philosophical paths, as well as the Iranian and Arab philosophers. We spoke somewhat about the proof of the Innate Disposition (Fitrah) and its awesomeness, an absolute covenant acknowledging this meaning. The first story was about Sayyid al-Mirza, may Allah have mercy on him, running around the issue of authoring a book.

We also addressed the fifth path, or the fifth proof from the evidences discussed in this section, which is **the path of witnessing by the heart (Mushahadah Qalbiyyah)**, which some philosophers termed as Presential Witnessing (Mushahadah Huduriyyah) or Presential Knowledge (Ma'rifah Huduriyyah) in the terminology of others. We spoke about this demand and cited what came in the supplication of the Master of Martyrs on the Day of Arafah, which speaks about this meaning of Presential Witnessing and the meaning of Presential Knowledge.

Segment 2: The Inability of the Limited to Comprehend the Unlimited

The Sheikh raises the fundamental question: Can a human being truly know God? He asserts that humans cannot encompass God in knowledge because the human is a "limited" (Mahdud) entity. In logic and philosophy, being "limited" means an entity can be defined by specific boundaries. Because God is infinite and boundless, the limited human intellect cannot possibly encompass or construct a true definition of the limitless Creator. He clarifies that while we can describe God with attributes of perfection (e.g., Lord of Lords), this is not a true, essential definition.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 4
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 8

In the eighth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues exploring the profound epistemological limits of humanity when attempting to comprehend the Divine. He systematically differentiates between mere intellectual **"Knowledge" ('Ilm)**—which relies on acquired mental images and logical deduction—and true **"Gnosis" (Ma'rifah)**—which is direct, unmediated illumination granted by Allah to the pure heart. To illustrate this, the Sheikh provides a comprehensive breakdown of the philosophical distinction between **"Acquired Knowledge" ('Ilm Husuli)** and **"Presential Knowledge" ('Ilm Huduri)**. He further exposes the inherent weakness of human logic, demonstrating that since human intellects cannot even grasp the true essence of lower creations (like defining an animal or a human perfectly), they are absolutely incapable of comprehending the absolute realities of the Imams, let alone the Essence of the Creator. **True knowledge, therefore, is not an academic achievement, but a divine light cast into the hearts of the faithful.**
Segment 1: Recap - The Prohibition of Thinking on God's Essence

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by reviewing the previous night's conclusion: the absolute impossibility of the limited human creature comprehending the Unlimited Creator. He reiterates the "Informative Disposition of Poverty" (Fitrah Istiqrariyyah), highlighting human fragility. He then references the explicit narrations from the Ahlulbayt that strictly forbid attempting to think about God's Essence, warning that such attempts only lead to bewilderment. Instead, humans are directed to witness God's signs and attributes.

In the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and blessings be upon the Messenger of Allah. And upon the family of Allah, and curses be upon their enemies, the enemies of their Shia, the enemies of Allah, until the Day of Encounter with Allah.

Last night, the discussion was regarding the issue of the inability of the human being, the inability of the human creature, to know or approach the Holy Essence of Allah, the Exalted. We addressed the research from a philosophical perspective in the issue of limits and definitions. How the Creator has no limit and no definition for Him. The human creature is a limited creature who is defined and limited; he cannot comprehend the Absolute Existence which has no state, no ceremony, and no definition.
We addressed the issue of the Informative Disposition (Fitrah Istikhbariyyah) existing within the human:
O mankind, you are those in need of Allah, while Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy.

We clarified the intended meaning which the mystics express regarding the issue of the Disposition of Poverty (Fitrah Iftiqariyyah), which also manifests the inability of the impoverished human to comprehend the Self-Sufficient, Enriching, Praiseworthy Essence.
We quickly pointed to a group of honorable verses and what was reported from the Household of Infallibility, prayers of Allah upon them all, which forbid thinking about the Divine Essence.
For every time you increase in thinking about it, you only increase in bewilderment! Rather, think about His attributes and do not think about His Essence, for if you think about His Essence...
Thus, the narrations clarified this.

We arrived at this firm conclusion: if the human is not capable of comprehending the Essence, then what does the human attain from the research into the issue of the Creator's existence, the issue of the Creator's Unity, and the issue of Monotheism in general? What can the human attain from the important? We said that what is attained from knowledge is this meaning which was pointed to:
Is it not sufficient concerning your Lord that He is, over all things, a Witness?
That He is a witnessing Witness, encompassing things, inside things. The meanings which were clarified.
Segment 2: Differentiating Between Knowledge ('Ilm) and Gnosis (Ma'rifah)

The Sheikh transitions to the crucial distinction between "Knowledge" ('Ilm) and "Gnosis" (Ma'rifah). He notes that while the narrations sometimes use the terms interchangeably, they often draw sharp distinctions. 'Ilm often refers to academic, theoretical information acquired through effort, which deals in generalities (universals). Ma'rifah, on the other hand, is the profound, action-inducing insight that comes through divine inspiration and heart-witnessing. It grasps particulars and personal realities that logic simply cannot reach.

Upon this trace, the discussion arrived at the issue of knowledge ('Ilm) and gnosis (Ma'rifah). What is the difference between knowledge and gnosis? I said that even in the honorable narrations, knowledge and gnosis sometimes come with one meaning—meaning knowledge comes with the meaning of gnosis, and gnosis comes with the meaning of knowledge. This happens often. But what indicates an elevation?

Sometimes, gnosis is closed, preferred over the knowledge that prompts action. The knowledge which prompts the human to act. As for knowledge, it is upon the existence of scientific mental images. In a third instance, gnosis is applied to the sciences and gnoses which the human attains from other than the path of acquisition, but rather from the path of inspiration, from the path of illumination, from the path of unveiling (Kashf), from the path of witnessing by the heart. And as for knowledge, it is the scientific demands which the human attains from the path of acquisition, effort, and reading.

I mentioned these differentiations which I stated before the meanings present in the narration. The research regarding the differentiation mentioned by the philosophers and mystics between knowledge is from the important point: that knowledge relates to universal issues, and the path to reaching these universal issues is the general path. As for gnosis, it researches into particular issues. Let alone the universal ones.

But gnosis, concerning knowledge, is that it relates to issues, even the particular and personal ones. The path to knowing them is the witnessing of the hearts. In their terminology, it is Presential Knowledge (Ma'rifah Huduriyyah). We said Presential Knowledge or Presential Witnessing differs according to the ranks of solutions, even for the Prophets, because the illuminated hearts which He supervises with jealousy, with His light, differ. As He said, these hearts are vessels, so the best of them and the widest of them...

There are heart ranks, and there are different ranks of solutions from here to here. There are hearts whose qualitative gnoses or heart gnoses settle at the Presence of Names. Here, the One Free Ink settles. There, to the Presence of Oneness. And in this, there are many ranks mentioned by the people of gnosis in its chapters and in detailing these presences.

I pointed to an issue in my view, that among the books that researched this demand—that this premise from the demands of the science of consensus which relates—one of the best books that researched this issue is Misbah al-Hidayah (The Lamp of Guidance), may Allah have mercy on him. As I said in my view, I say this book is better than the rest of the books. Science, Arabic, until our present day. From these honorable infallible texts which were reported from our Imams, prayers of Allah upon them all. From the aspect of its contents, from the aspect of the meanings and ideological dimensions mentioned in this honorable book.
Segment 3: Acquired Knowledge ('Ilm Husuli) vs. Presential Knowledge ('Ilm Huduri)

To explain Presential Knowledge deeply, the Sheikh introduces the philosophical division of knowledge into two categories: Acquired Knowledge ('Ilm Husuli) and Presential Knowledge ('Ilm Huduri). Acquired knowledge is merely the mental image or reflection of a thing in the mind (like the image of a book when reading it). Presential Knowledge, however, is when the actual reality of the known object is present with the knower, without any mediating mental image—like a person's direct knowledge of their own soul or pain.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 5
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 9

In the ninth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the epistemological discussion on how humanity can (and cannot) comprehend God. He reiterates the critical difference between intellectual **"Knowledge" ('Ilm)**—which is flawed, mediated, and restricted to the logical mind—and true **"Gnosis" (Ma'rifah)**—which is the direct, unmediated illumination of the heart. The Sheikh explains that while philosophical methods (like the Peripatetic and Illuminationist schools) attempt to define this "Presential Knowledge" ('Ilm Huduri), they still fall short of the truth taught by the Ahlulbayt. The core message of the episode asserts that **true Monotheism (Tawhid) is absolutely inseparable from the Wilayah (Authority) of the Ahlulbayt.** Without recognizing the Prophet and the Imams, any intellectual belief in God remains a mere academic exercise, not the saving Gnosis required by Allah.
Segment 1: Recap of Knowledge ('Ilm) vs. Gnosis (Ma'rifah)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by summarizing the previous episodes, emphasizing the human inability to comprehend the Divine Essence due to human limitations. He reviews the distinction between Knowledge ('Ilm)—which is acquired through intellectual effort—and Gnosis (Ma'rifah)—which is received directly by the heart. He briefly touches upon the philosophical schools (Peripatetics and Illuminationists) and their definitions of "Presential Knowledge" ('Ilm Huduri).

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 6
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 10

In the tenth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi delves into the second core issue of Monotheism (Tawhid): **the Unity (Wahdaniyyah) of the Creator.** He first examines the profound meaning of Unity by dissecting a sermon from Imam Ali, emphasizing that God's existence is proven by His signs and that true Monotheism requires **distinguishing Him from His creation through His attributes, not through physical isolation.** The Sheikh then transitions to the logical and textual proofs used to establish this Unity. He presents the theologians' primary argument, **the Proof of Mutual Hindrance (Dalil al-Tamanu')**, which logically demonstrates that the existence of multiple gods would inevitably lead to the corruption of the cosmic system. Finally, he outlines the auditory proofs from the Quran and the Sunnah, as well as the consensus of the Prophets, reaffirming that **the entire universe operates under the single, unchallengeable will of one Almighty Creator.**
Segment 1: Introduction and the Meaning of Unity

Sheikh al-Ghizzi introduces the second major topic of Monotheism: the Unity of the Creator. He explains that this topic requires understanding both the meaning of Unity and the proofs used to establish it. He sets the stage by turning to the words of the Master of Legatees, Imam Ali, to provide a foundational understanding before diving into theological arguments.

In the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and blessings be upon the Messenger of Allah and his family. And curses be upon their enemies and the enemies of their Shia, the enemies of Allah, until the Day of Encounter.

Our speech in the past night was regarding the issue of gnosis and its definition, and what came in the narrations of the House of Infallibility, prayers of Allah upon them all, regarding this meaning. Today, we take up the second issue from the issues of Monotheism. We researched the first issue, which relates to proving His existence, the Exalted. And we held upon that some clarifications which were before us in the past night.
