title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Study of Monotheism Part 12 (Divine Attributes Continued)
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 16

In the sixteenth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi deepens the exploration of the **Divine Attributes (Al-Sifat Al-Ilahiyyah)**, drawing heavily from the narrations of the Ahlulbayt. He focuses on clarifying the crucial distinction between **Attributes of Essence (Sifat al-Dhat)** and **Attributes of Action (Sifat al-Fi'l)**. By citing traditions from Imam al-Sadiq, he demonstrates that God has always possessed His Essential Attributes (like Knowledge, Hearing, Seeing, and Power) completely independent of the existence of any creation. In contrast, Attributes of Action (like Speaking, Creating, and Willing) only emerge when God acts upon creation. The Sheikh warns against the philosophical and theological errors of claiming that God's Will or Speech is eternal and uncreated, asserting instead that **God creates His Will.** The episode concludes with a strong admonition for students to actively memorize, study, and internalize these ideological demands to **fortify their faith against the severe trials of the End Times (Akhir al-Zaman).**
Segment 1: Recap and the Essential Attributes of Allah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by recapping the previous lesson regarding the Divine Attributes, specifically reminding the audience that philosophical categorizations of God's attributes are mere "Mental Constructs." He then introduces a foundational narration from Imam al-Sadiq found in Sheikh al-Saduq's Kitab al-Tawhid. This narration clarifies that Allah's core attributes—Knowledge, Hearing, Seeing, and Power—are identical to His Essence and existed eternally before there was anything to be known, heard, seen, or overpowered.

We are the Party of Allah, and prayers be upon the Messenger of Allah. May Allah curse their enemies. We continue the lessons. I spoke firstly about entering the stage of moderation, and in the past night, about other demands in this regard. We researched the terminologies used in the books of theology and philosophy.

Tonight, we take a quick look at the honorable narrations reported from the House of Infallibility, prayers of Allah upon them all, specifically regarding the researches of the Divine Attributes. Consider the honorable narrations which Al-Saduq, may Allah have mercy upon him, mentioned in his book, Al-Tawhid. The honorable report of Abu Basir, who said: I heard Abu Abdullah (Imam al-Sadiq), peace be upon him, saying:

Allah never ceased to be. And Knowledge is His Essence when there was no known. And Hearing is His Essence when there was no heard. And Seeing is His Essence when there was no seen. And Power is His Essence when there was no overpowered.

So when He originated things, and the known came to be, His Knowledge fell upon the known, His Hearing upon the heard, His Seeing upon the seen, and His Power upon the overpowered. Abu Basir said: I asked, "So did Allah never cease to be Speaking?" He said:
Speech is a created attribute; Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, existed when there was no speaker [or speech].

This honorable narration of the Imam, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, speaks generally to the meaning I discussed regarding the Essential and the Actual Attributes. The **Actual Attributes are extracted from the link between the Cause and the effect.** The Imam says: "Allah, the Majestic and Exalted, never ceased to be."

From the Essential Attributes is Knowledge. Why? Because the ruling is **eternal without the known.** The known is you and I and these things. The attributes of the Creator are eternal: "Allah, the Majestic and Exalted, never ceased to be. And Hearing is His Essence when there was no heard. And Seeing is His Essence when there was no seen. And Power is His Essence when there was no overpowered."
Segment 2: The Actualization of Knowledge and Power

Continuing with Imam al-Sadiq's narration, the Sheikh explains how God's eternal Essential Attributes translate into action. When God creates the universe, His eternal Knowledge "falls upon" the newly created known objects. This manifestation of His knowledge in creation is called "Actual Knowledge" ('Ilm Fi'li). He further explains that attributing "Knowledge" and "Power" to God is not to compose Him of parts, but to describe the single, indivisible reality of His perfection.

These attributes belong to the Creator firmly without the existence of the created. This speech expresses the internal attributes: the Essential Knowledge, the Essential Hearing, the Essential Power. These Internal Attributes are the Essence without the created. "And Knowledge is His Essence. And Hearing is His Essence when there was no heard. And Seeing is His Essence when there was no seen. And Power is His Essence when there was no overpowered."
Because the truth is united, absolute, and enduring, as advanced in the honorable narrations reported from them, prayers upon them.

"So when He originated things, and the known came to be." He originated causes, created them, and creatures came into being. "And the known came to be, His Knowledge fell upon this." This is what we termed in the past night as **Actual Knowledge ('Ilm Fi'li)**. In the terminology of the scholars, there is Essential Knowledge, which is the Eye of the Essence, and there is Actual Knowledge, which is the knowledge the Creator executes in what is around Him, the creations He created. "The known, His Knowledge fell."

When he said "the known, His Knowledge fell," the creations He created corresponded to the scientific knowledge. And therefore, if one reviews theological books, they find the terminology of Actual Knowledge. This harmonizes with the honorable narrations. "So when the known acted, His Knowledge fell from Him upon the known, and His Hearing upon the heard, and His Seeing upon the seen."
Segment 3: The Distinction Between Hearing and the "Ear"

The Sheikh addresses another narration to clarify how God "Hears" and "Sees." God does not possess physical organs like ears or eyes. Instead, He "Hears by Himself" and "Sees by Himself." The Sheikh uses the Imam's explanation to show that human language forces us to use separate words (like "He" and "Himself"), but in God's reality, there is no separation between His Essence and His perception.
"And His Power upon the overpowered." I said: "So did Allah never cease to be Speaking?" He said: "Speech is a proven [created] attribute."

The narration after it from Hamad bin Isa says: I asked Abu Abdullah, peace be upon him: "Allah never ceased to be a being and no known." He said, "He does not cease to be that." I asked, "By Allah, Knowing, Hearing, Seeing?" The end of the hadith speaks about the Essential Disposition. The Imam says: **"He hears by Himself."**

Meaning, He does not hear with a limb. Rather, He hears by Himself, He interprets by Himself. The Imam adds: "And it is not meant that He hears by Himself that He and the self are a separate thing. But I wanted a phrase to calm my delusion." So I say **He hears with all of Himself**, not that He hears with a limb. But the expression about the soul is within the limits of the words we use.

This detail enters into the transactions regarding Monotheism in Essence. Why did we talk about the building of Essential Monotheism? It is the logic which came in the honorable narrations in the meaning of Essential Monotheism which we must rely upon.
Segment 4: The Attributes of Action: Will (Iradah) and Volition (Mashiyyah)

The Sheikh pivots to a critical area of theological dispute: the attributes of Will (Iradah) and Volition (Mashiyyah). While some philosophers claim God's Will is eternal and part of His Essence, the Ahlulbayt emphatically state that God's Will is a created Attribute of Action. God created His Will, and through His Will, He created all things. If God's Will were eternal and essential, it would imply that creation is eternally forced upon Him, robbing Him of true free choice.

"Allah never ceased." The Will of Allah is from the rational periods. We do not accept from the philosophers their speech when they say that the Will is an Essential Attribute. The Infallible Imam, peace be upon him, says that it is from the actions.

**If we said that Allah's Will is eternal, and the Owner eternally wills, it implies creation is forced.** This is a false saying. The Will and Volition are created. The narration states that the first thing Allah originated was Volition. He is Knowing and Capable, but the Will comes after that.

I said to Abu Abdullah, peace be upon him: "Is the knowledge of Allah the Will?" So he said: "Knowledge is not the Will. Do you not say 'I will do such if Allah wills'?" So the Will is an origination. His command, when He intends a thing, is only that He says to it, 'Be,' and it is. It does not mean that He eternally wants the thing.

As for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, His Will is an origination. He does not need a process of contracting; He does not think internally after that or want distances. **His Will is an action.** Yes, if we said that Allah willed it was. This is correct even in the narrations. Is there a consequence between the Will and the limits? Will is an origination.
Segment 5: The Crucial Importance of Active Learning and Memorization

The Sheikh ends by stressing the difference between the actual attribute of God's Will and the created nature of His Volition (Mashiyyah). He notes that many sects mistakenly believe God's Will is eternal. He then shifts to giving sincere advice to the students: simply listening to the lectures is not enough. True ideological learning requires active study, note-taking, and review. He warns that a superficial understanding of beliefs will leave them vulnerable to the severe trials and tribulations of the End Times before the reappearance of Imam al-Mahdi.

We must differentiate between the Essential Attributes (Life, Power) and the originated attributes like Volition and Will. We find this repeating in the honorable narrations because there are those believing that Volition and Will are eternal attributes. This emphasis in the narrations is to pass the sound belief for the Saved Sect against other sects, so there is a clear picture that the **Will is a created action.**

As it appears from the honorable narrations, the Volition is the first created. The narration of Salim from Abu Abdullah, peace be upon him, says: "He created Volition by Himself. Then He created things by Volition." The Essential Attributes of the Creator (Life, Power) were not a creation. But the Will is. Therefore, this meaning is honored in many narrations in our books.
Regarding these details, we try to take up the existent picture about the Divine according to the Noble Book and the texts of the Infallibles, prayers of Allah upon them all.

I advise the brothers: perhaps you do not possess a strong memory to remember the demands mentioned in the lesson. Therefore, you should review with negotiation among yourselves. We are here upon a pulpit so you hear the demands, but for the sake of the brothers, they may **write and record notes benefiting from this.** If the human has a strong capacity for receiving demands, he needs administration and start-up, reviewing in debates and schools.

If there were discussions, negotiations, summarizing, renewing with texts, and memorizing some Quranic texts, there is a benefit. The human must be general. And if this science which we carry does not walk the internal path achieving the true truth from the certain knowledge, what is not realized is lost.

We must have a clear picture even if overall about our beliefs. The human must be informed of the dimensions of his belief. A clear picture of belief aids the human upon facing the trial. The narrations show that there will be a **severe trial before the appearance of the Imam**, prayers of Allah and peace be upon him. The trial of the End Times will be severe. The Imam connects Monotheism with these demands.

The issue from the aspect of training the human is preparing the human to learn. The Creator prepares the argument upon the human. So if the human was not benefiting from these matters, he will lose. Incoming in the honorable narrations is that the training must be like **water dripping upon a rock, leaving a mark.** With this amount, God willing, our speech is completed.

The coming Sunday the brothers will have the lessons, God willing. But Saturday the lesson is absent considering the occasion of the Great Truthful upon her. Sunday, Monday, Thursday, and Friday also. The ethics class this week is disabled considering these days in the Fatimiyyah domain. Our final prayer is that praise be to Allah.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - On the Meaning of Monotheism
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 17

In the seventeenth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the foundational discussions on the meaning of Monotheism (Tawhid) by connecting the intellectual study of beliefs to its true spiritual purpose: **Sincerity (Ikhlas)**. He emphatically warns against studying theology merely to debate and defeat opponents, stating that such knowledge becomes a dangerous veil and a burden on the Day of Judgment. True knowledge ('Ilm) must harmonize with the heart to become **Gnosis (Ma'rifah)**. The Sheikh then reveals the ultimate truth of Shia monotheism: **Sincerity and Monotheism are absolutely identical to the Wilayah of the Ahlulbayt.** Through the famous **Hadith of the Golden Chain**, he demonstrates that the fortress of "There is no god but Allah" is the exact same fortress as "The Wilayah of Ali ibn Abi Talib." The episode culminates in a profound explanation of Imam Ali's absolute sincerity, defining true Islam as sitting at the door of the heart and allowing nothing to enter but Allah.
Segment 1: Recap of the Ranks of Monotheism and Mental Constructs

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by summarizing the previous lessons regarding the ranks of Monotheism (Essence, Attributes, and Actions). He distinguishes between Affirmative Attributes (attributing perfection to God) and Negative Attributes (stripping deficiency from God). He reiterates the core Shia belief that God's attributes are exactly identical to His Essence. Finally, he reminds the audience that all theological divisions and categorizations are merely "Mental Constructs" (Mafahim I'tibariyyah) created by the limited human intellect to grasp the Unlimited.

In the Name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and blessings and peace be upon our Master and Prophet, Muhammad, and his pure, immaculate family. And may His permanent curse be upon their enemies from now until the Day of Judgment.

In our past lessons regarding the beliefs, we progressed and paused at the end of these lessons in the discussions of Monotheism in Essence, Attributes, and Actions. We said that Monotheism, which is the foundation of Islamic beliefs, has specific ranks. One section is Monotheism in Essence, another is Monotheism in Attributes, and another is Monotheism in Actions. The summary of what passed in the lessons of Monotheism in Essence is the belief in His existence, the Exalted, and His Unity. And that He is the First who has no beginning to His priority, and the Last who has no end to His finality. He is the Eternal who has no opposite and no equal. He is the One who was when nothing else was. This is what is called Monotheism in Essence.

Monotheism in Attributes is that we believe that the Divine Essence contains all attributes of perfection and is transcendent above every deficiency and flaw, and that **His attributes are the exact essence of His Essence**, as is our belief which the hadiths of the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all, explicitly stated. The intended meaning of the **Affirmative Attributes (Sifat Thubutiyyah)** is clear, meaning the established attributes attributed to Allah. The **Negative Attributes (Sifat Salbiyyah)** are the attributes from which the Divine Essence is stripped; we say that Allah, the Exalted, is not ignorant, stripping ignorance from Him. We say that Allah, the Exalted, is not a body, stripping corporeality from the Holy Divine Essence. We say that Allah, the Exalted, is not incapable. Thus, whenever ignorance, inability, or corporeality is stripped away, it is called a Negative Attribute, meaning the attributes of transcendence, where we transcend Him above deficiencies.

As for the Affirmative Attributes, it is when we attribute to Him complete descriptions, when we say the Knowing, the Living, the Hearing, the Seeing, and the rest of the established divine descriptions which are expressed in terminology as Affirmative Attributes. All these Affirmative Attributes trace back to the attribute of Knowledge and the attribute of Power. When we say that Allah is Hearing, the intended meaning is that He is Knowing of audible things. When we say that Allah is Seeing, the intended meaning is that He is Knowing of visible things. When we say that Allah is a Creator, the intended meaning is that He is Powerful over creation. Thus, the attributes of the Creator contain an essential aspect and an actual aspect. If we look at the creations, that Allah created the human, this is an actual attribute. We looked at the creations and said Allah is a Creator after He created. But if we looked at the issue of power, that Allah is Capable of creation, the attribute is essential.

We also spoke about the meaning of effectiveness. Knowledge and Power are among the Essential Attributes. The Creator, the Exalted, is described by the attribute of Knowledge and the attribute of Power. In the human being, through his knowledge and his attribute of power, power in the human is different from knowledge. A human may be powerful or not powerful, and a human may be knowing while there is a deficiency in his power. As a result, the human has limited power, but this power differs from one person to another. As a result, in the human, his knowledge is a word, his power is different from his knowledge, and so on for most creatures. **As for the Divine Attributes, His Power is the essence of His Knowledge, His Knowledge is the essence of His Power, and His Attributes are the essence of His Essence.** This is what we call Monotheism in Attributes.

As for Monotheism in Actions, it is that the actions present in this universe branch from the Power of the Creator, the Exalted, and submit under His authority, His control, and His Power, Exalted is His state. This brief clarification and summary statement regarding the meaning of Monotheism in Attributes, Monotheism in Essence, and Monotheism in Actions advanced.

In truth, this division into Monotheism in Essence, Actions, and Attributes, and dividing attributes into Negative and Affirmative, and dividing attributes into Actual and Essential, these divisions and branchings were only in this manner for the sake of being comprehended by the human intellect. We spoke about the issue of **Mental Constructs (Mafahim I'tibariyyah)**. We said these concepts were considered by the intellect so that the human can acquire information and reach the desired goals. Otherwise, these meanings in their reality and in the truth of their realities differ from all these expressions, all these divisions, and all these branchings. But the limitation of the human intellect and the limitation of human perceptions push us to extract these divisions and branchings, which enter into the circle of Mental Constructs, so that the human can encompass these meanings.
Segment 2: The True Purpose of Studying Beliefs

The Sheikh addresses a critical flaw in traditional religious education: studying theology to debate and defeat opponents. He explains that the Prophets did not seek to merely win arguments, as opponents rarely yield to proof out of sheer arrogance. If a student seeks ideological knowledge merely for rhetoric, fame, or argumentation, that knowledge will become a curse and a veil between them and Allah. He emphasizes the profound difference between mere academic "Knowledge" ('Ilm) and true "Gnosis" (Ma'rifah), which occurs only when the mind harmonizes with the heart.

There is an issue of utmost importance. We are now in the process of researching the issue of Monotheism, researching this fundamental ideological ruling from the pillars of our belief, and from the pillars of belief in general. What is the purpose of studying beliefs? Why have these beliefs become studied, and these ideological researches discussed? What is the goal of knowing beliefs?

The intended purpose of this information and this knowledge which the human takes up is not that he learns this information for the sake of argumentation, for the sake of writing, rhetoric, and teaching before any other action. Why do we learn the meanings of belief? When the human learns the meanings of belief, does he learn this information to argue with people? **Therefore, there is a clear and evident mistake in studying beliefs and in teaching beliefs, which is the focus on studying how to silence the opponent, and how to defeat the opponent!** This is a portion of the delusion.

To direct the lessons of beliefs or to tie our ideological life for the sake of silencing our opponents is from the affairs of argumentation, and it is not from the nature of the Prophets, despite the vastness of their knowledge, to convince all their opponents, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all. They could not convince everyone, and the opponent does not always yield to the proof. The human does not always yield to the truth. Even if he knows that this is the truth and the clear truth, they oppose it out of arrogance. Human history and the history of the Prophets is a witness to that. Therefore, from the great mistakes and illusions present in the ideological sciences or in teaching beliefs is this issue: the focus on defeating the opponent. We are not tasked with defeating the opponent.

Therefore, we find those who study these researches, especially when these principles of theology expanded, that the greatest focus is on how to convince the opponent of the correctness of our belief. This issue in the first stage of the student's life, in the beginning, must first implant these meanings in our own souls, to convince ourselves with these meanings. After that, we move to the second stage in thinking about convincing opponents. Otherwise, the primary goal of studying beliefs is not convincing opponents. Therefore, we in these lessons, whether in the past lessons or the coming lessons, face the issue. We do not search much for the proofs that convince opponents, as much as we search for the beliefs that were reported in our verses and in the hadiths of our Imams, peace be upon them all. We may mention some proofs, but the original goal and the primary purpose of studying the correct beliefs in their honorable narrations is not convincing the opponent.

From here, we do not find that the Imams, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all, took up argumentation and argumentative methods initially, except when necessary. But what we find in the life of the Imams and in their method of guiding people and taking them by their hands to the shore of safety and security is the **conscientious (Wijdani) method** which they dealt with. This has multiple manifestations and multiple applications in the life of people, and we find it clearly in the life of the Imams, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all. This issue is of utmost importance and must be paid attention to. Unless the lesson was convened and the research was convened to debate opponents or to reply to opponents, that is another issue. But the general matter and the first matter which relates to us is knowing our belief.

Otherwise, this learning of this information for the sake of debate and argumentation, as the honorable hadiths mention, that the scholar or the seeker of knowledge who seeks knowledge for the sake of arguing with the foolish and the people of misguidance, this is for the Hellfire. He who seeks knowledge for argumentation, debate, and discussion which does not bear fruit, these sciences which take up the issues of beliefs are not sought for the sake of argumentation. Spiritual beliefs are not sought for the sake of debate, nor are they sought for the sake of showing off information. And they are not sought for the sake of authoring, and for the sake of teaching. That these sciences be utilized in debate is sometimes true; in correct debate, these sciences may be utilized in writing, teaching, rhetoric, and calling to Allah. But for that to be the ultimate goal, this is not the desired goal, especially in some disciplines, because the original goal of beliefs is that the human learns them to live by them.

Otherwise, the human stores information, and this stored information may sometimes be disastrous. Therefore, we read the supplication reported from the Master of Legatees, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, which our Sheikh Al-Misbah mentioned. In this honorable supplication:

O Allah, grant me a guiding intellect, and a pure heart, and a piercing knowledge, and abundant knowledge, and make all of that for me, and do not make it against me, by Your mercy, O Most Merciful of the merciful.

In some cases, this capability, all these faculties will be against him. Look at the paragraphs of the supplication: the piercing intellect, the pure heart, the abundant knowledge. These are the most beautiful faculties that a human can attain. Yet, despite that, the honorable supplication says: "And make all of that for me, and do not make it against me." Because in many cases, the accepted information and the stored knowledge present in the human's mind, and these scientific images which fill the human's memory, may be against the human. How can they be against the human? This has many cases. Among them is that this information will be a burden against the human on the Day of Resurrection, it will be a proof against the human. Therefore the supplication says: "And make all of that for me, and do not make it against me." Meaning that this information will be against the human, it will be a burden on the human on the Day of Resurrection. This is the first way it can be against the human.

Meaning, instead of benefiting the human, when this information distracts him from what benefits him. Meaning the human becomes preoccupied with acquiring sciences and acquiring mere information, or for the sake of fame and reputation. In such cases, the information he attains for these purposes will distract him from other things that would benefit him in his religious life and in his afterlife. He spends a lot of time in his life attaining this information for this purpose, and thus he spends most of his time in this. In such a case, it becomes a reason for distracting him from achieving good deeds, and this information becomes against him and not for him.

The third is that this information and these sciences which are sought for these purposes and sought for these goals will be a veil between the servant and his Lord. And thereby, it will also be a great burden against him, where the information gathered and attained by the human for argumentation, or for the sake of debating, writing, or teaching, or for judging only, becomes a veil. Rather, these are not the praiseworthy purposes with the Creator, the Exalted, if they were in such cases, between them and the Creator, the Exalted. Thus, this information will be against him and not for him.

Especially in some beliefs, this case is clearer, because the topics of beliefs and the issues of beliefs, when the human takes them up by sitting, reading, teaching, and researching, it is required of the human to live them, to interact with them. And definitely, interacting with ideological meanings does not come through the path of proofs, nor does it come through the path of teaching. Interacting with ideological meanings only comes with the conscientious (Wijdani) method.

The Imams, upon them the best of prayers and peace, used the conscientious method, and this is clear in their hadiths. We explained in the past lessons that there is a difference between Knowledge ('Ilm) and Gnosis (Ma'rifah). We mentioned the differences in the past lessons between Knowledge and Gnosis, and we said that Knowledge is the stored information lying in the mind; this is called knowledge and information. As for Gnosis, it is when the scientific information in the human's intellect harmonizes with the heart's concepts. **When there is a harmony between the heart's concepts and the intellectual concepts, this is what is expressed as Gnosis.** Therefore, the narrations did not say, "Whoever does not know (yalam) the Imam of his time," they said, "Whoever does not recognize (ya'rif) the Imam of his time." Therefore, mostly the hadith is about the Gnosis of Allah and not about the Knowledge of Allah. Some narrations were reported, but most narrations and most supplications speak in this manner.

When we read the true beliefs, we find that the narrations speak in the aspect of beliefs under the title of Gnosis (Ma'rifah). While in other issues, for example in matters related to natural issues in life or jurisprudential issues that regulate human life, they express this information and these truths as Knowledge. But the conscientious demands, the heart demands, and the true demands related to Divine Gnosis and beliefs are expressed as Gnosis. Therefore, there is a clear difference.

We in the past lessons stated the differences between Knowledge and Gnosis. The summary of what we reached regarding the difference between Knowledge and Gnosis is that Knowledge is the stored information and the mental images. As for Gnosis, it is when the concepts harmonize with the scientific concepts in his mind. For example, regarding the issue of reliance (Tawakkul) upon Allah, the Exalted. The human in his intellect proves with definitive proof and with experience from the lives of others, and from the texts reported from the Book, the hadiths, and the supplications, that whoever relies on Allah, Allah suffices him. From a rational perspective, this is proven. But when he wants to face the problems of life, and when he wants to face the preoccupations of the world, and when a problem, an obstacle, or a danger befalls him, we find that the human does not try to rely on Allah to solve these matters. Why? Even though in his mind and in his intellect, he knows that reliance on Allah suffices. This is a type of Knowledge.

This is Knowledge and not Gnosis. When the unseen motive and the heart concept harmonize with the intellect's concept, and the soul aligns with the intellect's concept, this is Gnosis. It transforms Knowledge into Gnosis, because Gnosis is a cause, and because Gnosis is a symptom, and because Gnosis is clearer and more manifest. The human in his intellect knows that whoever seeks help from other than Allah is humiliated. But in practical reality, we find that the human seeks help from creatures, forgetting the Creator, the Exalted. Yes, there are types of seeking help; seeking help from the Ahlulbayt, peace be upon them, is seeking help from Allah. But the human sometimes seeks help from those hostile to Allah in overcoming situations. Why? Because of a lack of balance between the heart concept and the ideological concept.

Beliefs are meant to be balanced within the human. And naturally, this balance does not come from studying based on proofs and based on theological or logical evidences. This balance comes from righteous deeds, it results from correct spiritual discipline, it results from proper reflection, it results from isolation from sins, it results from the clarity which the human acquires. Through this, he attains clarity in knowledge and sound gnosis, and his rational information turns into balance with the heart information, creating a harmony between his rational and heart concepts. Otherwise, the information stored in the mind does not yield good benefit, nor does it make the human stand on the true path, the clear and sound path.
Segment 3: Sincerity (Ikhlas) as the Reality of Monotheism

The Sheikh introduces Sincerity (Ikhlas) as the true essence of Monotheism. He quotes a divine Hadith Qudsi stating that if Allah finds a servant's heart completely sincere in seeking His pleasure, He directly takes over the management and guidance of that person's life. However, he notes that absolute, perfect sincerity is a rank exclusive to the Infallible Imams, as described in Ziyarat al-Jamiah al-Kabirah. All human attempts at sincerity ultimately pour into the supreme manifestation of love and devotion to the Ahlulbayt.

The human, naturally, in these matters, this truth does not enter inside unless it is available except in a few. We are now in the chapter of Monotheism, and Monotheism is Sincerity (Ikhlas). Monotheism is the reality of Sincerity, the reality of Monotheism is the reality of Sincerity. Therefore, the Surah of Monotheism which we read in our prayers is called the Surah of Sincerity. The meaning of Monotheism is Sincerity, and Sincerity as the Second Martyr (Al-Shahid Al-Thani), may Allah have mercy upon him, defined it.
But this narration from Imam al-Sadiq, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, from the Great Prophet, may Allah bless him and his family, from Allah Almighty:
I do not look into the heart of a servant and find in it the love of sincerity for My obedience, seeking My pleasure, except that I take charge of his evaluation and management.

I will repeat the words of the narration so you can grasp its meaning. The meaning present in this honorable narration: "I do not look into the heart of a servant," and the heart is the station of unseen concepts and the station of Islamic concepts. It is the sincerity which accepts the practical action, not in the intellect here. Perhaps the human in his intellect reaches the conclusion that sincerity in his action is necessary, but he does not act with sincerity. And with his intellect he believes that sincerity is necessary, but he does not perform it. This is the disease that afflicts us.

Therefore, the hadith says: "I do not look into the heart of a servant and find in it the love of sincerity for My obedience, seeking My pleasure, except that I take charge of his evaluation and management." If the matter is that the Creator takes charge of his evaluation and management, meaning taking charge of evaluating this action and managing it. If the human reaches the point where he does not carry evaluation from anyone but the Creator, nor management from anyone but the Creator, this is the utmost meaning of this hadith. A clear hadith: "I do not look into the heart of a servant and find in it the love of sincerity for My obedience, seeking My pleasure, except that I take charge of his evaluation and management." "Evaluating him" means placing him on the straight path, on the straight way. Also with this meaning, meaning nurturing, meaning that the Creator is the one who takes charge of evaluating him in any case. If sincerity is found. And what I said now is regarding the meaning of Monotheism, and Monotheism is Sincerity. And the hadith here points to the Creator, the Exalted, taking charge of the general heart.

And the truth is that we, even if we reach this degree which ethics mentions and call it sincerity. And they say they purify this sincerity, because true sincerity is a specific rank for the closest ones. Otherwise, the rank which we see as true sincerity, sincerity is from the most perfect treatments and in the meaning of the highest justice manifesting in the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them. Therefore, we read in the Ziyarat al-Jamiah al-Kabirah reported from al-Hadi, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him: "Peace be upon the Imams, the Infallibles... Peace be upon the callers to Allah, and the guides to the pleasure of Allah, and those steadfast in the command of Allah, and the perfect in the love of Allah, and the sincere ones in the Monotheism of Allah."

And we prefer the second reading: "And the purified/sincere ones (Al-Mukhlaseen)." The sincerity of the Ahlulbayt is innate, because innate creation, relative to us, the created is more expensive in rank from the important, that the sincere one (Mukhlis) is not absolute. Why? He must be sincere in the beginning, he must reach the degree of sincerity to be **purified (Mukhlas)**. As for them, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all, there is at the same time, because there are no stages in the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all. They were far from the impurity, but rather they are from the beginning closer to the first creation, from the beginning they are closer. There are no stages where they were far from it, until they say in the beginning then they ascend to another stage which is the rank of incarnation, to be, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all.

I do not want to delve into this meaning, but this paragraph from the Ziyarah is clear. The Ziyarah says: "Peace be upon the callers to Allah." Callers to His pleasure, in the meaning of the true call, not in the meaning that even the Prophets, their call is a branch of their call. And they are the guides: "Peace be upon the callers to Allah and the guides to the limits of Allah, upon the pleasure of Allah." With all, but rather to the pleasure of the call, and they were the guides to Allah upon the significance because they are second in Divine Gnosis in every sense of the word. Therefore, we do not understand the significance, and their call was the greatest of calls to Allah, considering that their gnosis is the most perfect of gnoses, considering that the Glorified is the most known.

Therefore the Ziyarah says: "And the steadfast in the command of Allah." Meaning He did not say those who act by the commands of Allah, the Exalted. "And the steadfast in the command of Allah," that they grow, meaning they do not have anything so they reflect in the love of Allah. The love of Allah relative to them, and relative to, meaning His obedience lessens from the second affection, which is Allah. The exact complete ones in the love of people, its teaching is that they love Allah, the Exalted. If they love Allah, the complete love of the Creator, the Exalted, will achieve the complete love because the light of all things. But this distinction is from the door of Mental Constructs, some consideration for scientific distinction to clarify the concepts and to raise the understanding of the love of the command, prayers of Allah upon them. Therefore they were: "Peace be upon the callers to Allah, and the guides to the pleasure of Allah, and the steadfast in the command of Allah, and the perfect in the love of Allah, and the sincere ones in the Monotheism of Allah."

Sincerity in its true meaning is this meaning, important, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all. As for the meanings of sincerity which relate to us, these ranks of Islam in the books of Islam, in the books of the science of behavior, were mentioned in Islam. Relative to the religious laws, relative to the ranks of sincerity. But these ranks all pour into this circle, into the circle of the love of Al-Hussein. And look at the honorable hadith, at the use of the word love, love. And this is the meaning which manifested in a group of many honorable hadiths which talked about the love of the Ahlulbayt. Because it is the sincerity to Allah, the love of sincerity mentioned in this hadith, and peace be upon him.
Therefore, this hadith reported in the books of the elite and the general public. The Prophet, may Allah bless him and his family and grant him peace:
Indeed, I hope for my Ummah in the Wilayah of Ali, or in the love of Ali, as I hope for them in saying, There is no god but Allah.

It is the word of sincerity, "There is no god..." It is the word of Monotheism. Meaning that the Prophet, may Allah bless him and his family, as a result the Prophet does not have a probable hope that it might happen, and it is possible for him to have salvation. It is a true scientific branch, a true scientific [fact]. When he says: "Indeed, I hope for my Ummah upon Ali," as he says, "There is no god but Allah." Meaning there is an equality of the same hope which he hopes for this Ummah from saying "There is no god but Allah," is the hope which he hopes for this Ummah from the love of Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.
Segment 4: The Inseparability of Monotheism and Wilayah (The Golden Chain)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides powerful hadith evidence that acknowledging God's Monotheism is invalid without acknowledging the Wilayah (Authority) of Imam Ali. He cites a hadith stating that on the Day of Judgment, Allah will strip the words "There is no god but Allah" from the hearts of everyone who denied Ali's authority. He reinforces this with the famous "Hadith of the Golden Chain," where Imam al-Ridha explicitly declared that entering the fortress of Monotheism requires accepting his divine authority. The fortress of "There is no god but Allah" and the fortress of "The Wilayah of Ali" are identical.
And this meaning was reported in the honorable hadiths. A narration narrated by Abu Ghalib al-Samad from Imam al-Sadiq, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him:

When it is the Day of Resurrection, Allah gathers the creations from the first and the last in one plain. What does the Creator do? He says, 'Indeed, Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, extracts "There is no god but Allah" from the hearts of all the creations, except whoever acknowledged the Wilayah of Ali!'

This is clear, and this meaning Al-Hakim al-Haskani from the scholars of the general public in his book Shawahid al-Tanzil, Part Two, page 121, cited more than one narration for this demand. That on the Day of Resurrection, the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted, gathers the creations, the first and the last, and extracts from their hearts "There is no god but Allah," except those who acknowledged the Wilayah of Ali. This was cited by Al-Hakim, the well-known scholar of the general public. Meaning he is the most knowledgeable of the scholars of hadith with them at this point. The movement does not indicate a scientific rank when their scholars say that he is the most knowledgeable of the scholars of hadith in a period. He has the power of governance over the hadiths, upon what is judged in it. Part Two, page 121. These narrations write the matter on any account. It is not important for us to mention the general public.

This narration of Maysarah is present in its ruling in the honorable Al-Kafi, that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, when He gathers the creations on the Day of Resurrection, strips their hearts of "There is no god but Allah," and does not sing upon Ali with His gnosis. Then Al-Sadiq says in the narration of Khalid: "And that is His saying, the Exalted: 'The Day that the Spirit and the angels will stand in rows, they will not speak except for one whom the Most Merciful permits, and he will say what is correct.' He said: 'Correct (Sawab) means the state of There is no god but Allah.' And he was not, and this who says correct, and this who said is the one who acknowledged the Wilayah of Ali."

And the one who did not acknowledge the Wilayah of Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, will not say "Allah Allah" on the Day of Resurrection, but it will be extracted from his heart. Therefore, in another narration when it came to us, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, that even those who say "There is no god but Allah" in the worldly life, on the Day of Resurrection it will be extracted. Therefore, from the hearts of security acknowledged the Wilayah of Ali, and peace be upon him.

And this meaning is clear and beautiful in the hadith of Al-Nawawi, from Al-Ridha, peace be upon him, while he was on his way to Khorasan, on his way to when he wanted the accepted path, he passed by the city of Nishapur. And the pure and the story came out for it detailing the books of hadith in the books of history, even in some books that there was a competition. They said a presence existed, meaning they wish that a scholar speaks, they held in the province. And they requested from Imam al-Ridha to speak to them with a hadith, and it was as in the narration in the architecture. When the mount passed and the architecture, they gathered around him. He said: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, you pass from these lands and do not speak to us with a hadith from the hadith of our Messenger, may Allah bless him and his family." They say he responded, lifting the separator to his honorable face. And he said to them. He said: "I heard Abu Ja'far bin Muhammad say, my father Muhammad bin Ali bin Al-Hussein narrated to me, from his father Al-Hussein, from his father from the British front. The Messenger of Allah, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all, said: Gabriel said upon the research of Allah, the Majestic, says:
There is no god but Allah is My fortress, and whoever enters My fortress is safe from My punishment.

Therefore one of them asks Imam al-Baqir, peace be upon him, upon the son of the Messenger of Allah, the hadith from our Messenger: "Whoever says there is no god but Allah enters Paradise, is it true?" So the man left the Imam after that, after so return, **"But O man, to 'There is no god but Allah' there are conditions, and I am from its conditions!"** The narration of the migration, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. The meaning in the Golden Chain which means there is a hadith called the **Hadith of the Golden Chain** also Imam al-Ridha, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, narrates it from his fathers, from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and his family, from Gabriel from Israel, from Allah the Majestic, from Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, that He said:
The Wilayah of Ali bin Abi Talib is My fortress, and whoever enters My fortress is safe from My punishment.

This hadith is the exact same as the first hadith. "There is no god but Allah is My fortress, and whoever enters My fortress is safe." And "The Wilayah of Ali bin Abi Talib is My fortress, and whoever enters My fortress," meaning that **"There is no god but Allah" and "The Wilayah" are the exact same** as the first hadith, the Ummah in the love of Ali, in the Wilayah of Ali, as I hope in the saying of "There is no god but Allah." Therefore, the two meanings agree clearly, in a unique way. And when Imam al-Ridha, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, was asked that Islam, that "Whoever says there is no god but Allah sincerely has entered Paradise," when they asked him about Sincerity (Ikhlas). He said: "Obedience to Allah, and obedience to His Messenger, and the Wilayah of the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams after him."

And this meaning of disrupting Monotheism, and the truth of it by this honorable hadith which the Second Master, may Allah have mercy upon him, mentioned a short while ago: "I do not look upon the heart of a servant doing from Islam except his evaluation and management. I know the acknowledgment, the love, the sincerity, and this upon the Ali, prayers of Allah upon them all."
Segment 5: The Heart of Imam Ali and Absolute Sincerity

To finalize the discussion on Sincerity, the Sheikh shares a profound narration about Imam Ali. When asked about his rank with Allah, Imam Ali described his heart as a door where he stood guard, allowing no one to enter except Allah. This represents the ultimate reality of Islam and Sincerity. The Sheikh connects this to Imam Khomeini's interpretation of Surah Al-'Asr, stating that the Infallible Imam embodies the concentrated truth of existence and Monotheism.

Therefore, the realistic picture, and the true picture of the meaning of the believers, the narration of the hadith that a Bedouin asked the Commander of the Faithful, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, that: "O Commander of the Faithful, what is the station and authority from the pulpit?" He, peace be upon him, said:
I sat at the door of my heart, so I did not let anyone enter it except Allah!

And this is the truth of Islam manifested, the Alawite. This approximate answer in these letters from the station and the mouse upon a door. "So when it enters the heart which if we came to Islam and sits any truth of Islam and peace be upon them all."

From here it was mentioned in the honorable hadiths in the Book of Monotheism by Sheikh Al-Saghir. And in others, that the Infallible Imam has Allah, and this is the meaning of Islam, it is the meaning of eternity. The truth of the human empties and the eye empties, the truth of the human empties, the personality of the human empties, the conscience of the human, the emotions of the human, the human, the desires of the human. He does not look to, but rather looks to their hearts, that the truth of the human is the human, his heart, and the heart of Ali at this time. They sat at the door of others, so why by Allah, meaning none except Allah, the Glorified and Exalted. And the sealed, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, was not poured that.

And this meaning agrees and harmonizes with the interpretation which the Ummah, may Allah's pleasure be upon him, mentioned in the meaning of the word "Al-'Asr" (The Time) in Surah Al-'Asr from the original caution. What did he say about the mother of the Ummah, may Allah have mercy upon him? The interpretation of this upon this word, this verse is an oath by the door of the Imam Al-Hujjah, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. Upon him, for what thing? Because the truth became and the present became existing truths. Meaning that the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted, the supporters of the existing truths in the essence of Al-Hussein, the Imam of our time, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, the same meaning. And the meaning is the truth of sincerity with the same meaning under upon the door of my heart. By Allah I love sincerity.
Meaning sincerity and Monotheism, and this is what the Commander of the Faithful, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, says:
Knowing Him, and the perfection of knowing Him is confirming Him, and the perfection of confirming Him is unifying Him, and the perfection of unifying Him is being sincere to Him.

And these meanings which the honorable hadiths pointed to, the word "There is no god but Allah" and "The Wilayah of Ali bin Abi Talib" ruling the meaning of confirmation in its most manifest appearances. And with this amount we suffice. And God willing in our hadith in the lessons of beliefs in the coming week. And our final prayer is that praise be to Allah the Lord of the worlds.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - Introduction Part 18: The Meaning of Decree and Destiny Part 1
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 18

In the eighteenth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi shifts the theological discussion from the attributes of God to a profound and often misunderstood topic in Islamic theology: **Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar)**. He begins by defining the linguistic and theological meanings of both terms, explaining that **Qada'** represents the absolute, universal divine laws governing existence, while **Qadar** represents the precise measurement and application of those laws to individual creations. Using a famous anecdote about Imam Ali avoiding a leaning wall, he illustrates how divine destiny operates through cause and effect rather than blind fatalism. However, the core of the episode focuses on the absolute secrecy and incomprehensibility of true Destiny. Quoting extensively from Imam Ali and the writings of Imam Khomeini, the Sheikh warns that **the true secrets of Decree and Destiny are locked in the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz)—which is the heart of the Infallible Imam—and are fundamentally inaccessible to the human intellect.** The believer's duty is not to unravel these mysteries, but to submit with complete contentment (Rida) to the will of Allah.

Segment 2: Linguistic and Theological Definitions of Decree and Destiny

Qadar means measurement, specifying the limits, conditions, and exact proportions of created things. He highlights that a true believer must accept that everything—good, bad, sweet, and bitter—falls under Allah's Decree and Destiny, though humans often misinterpret these outcomes based on their limited perspective.

Segment 3: Imam Ali and the Leaning Wall

To clarify how Decree and Destiny function in reality, the Sheikh recounts the famous story of Imam Ali moving away from a leaning wall. When asked if he was fleeing from Allah's decree, the Imam replied that he was fleeing from Allah's decree to Allah's destiny. This illustrates that Destiny operates through established physical laws (cause and effect); sitting under a collapsing wall will result in death, which is a divine law (Qada'). Moving away is applying another divine law (Qadar) regarding human capacity and choice.

Perhaps the best hadith to clarify the meaning of Decree and Destiny is this known hadith. And this hadith perhaps many have heard it or memorized it. This narration God willing we come and the crisis speech in the great week. With the power of Allah. And our final prayer is the Worlds.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - On the Meaning of Decree and Destiny Part 2
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 19

In the nineteenth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues the profound theological discussion on the meaning of **Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar)**. Building upon the previous lesson, he reiterates that **Qada' (Decree)** refers to the absolute, universal laws governing the cosmos, while **Qadar (Destiny)** refers to the specific measurements and limitations applied to each individual creation. However, the core of the episode serves as a powerful theological warning: **the true reality and secrets of Decree and Destiny are absolutely inaccessible to the human intellect.** The Sheikh analyzes several narrations from the Ahlulbayt, including Imam Ali's description of Destiny as a "dark path" and a "deep sea" that must not be traversed. He concludes by referencing Imam Khomeini's **Misbah al-Hidayah** to explain that the highest levels of Divine Knowledge—the hidden secrets of Destiny—are exclusively granted to the **"Perfect Man" (Al-Insan Al-Kamil)**, representing the Prophet Muhammad and the Infallible Imams, while remaining completely veiled from ordinary human comprehension.
Segment 1: Linguistic and Conceptual Recap of Decree and Destiny

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins by summarizing the previous lesson. He reviews the linguistic definitions, explaining that Qadar (Destiny) means "measurement" (Taqdir)—setting precise limits, forms, and capabilities for every created thing. He then defines Qada' (Decree) as "completion" (Tamam) or the final execution of the overarching cosmic laws. He references Quranic verses to show how Allah measures all things and decrees commands that are instantly executed.

In the Name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and may Allah bless our Master and Prophet, Muhammad, and his pure, immaculate family. May His curse be upon their enemies and those who wrong them all.

The speech in the past lesson was regarding the meaning of Decree (Qada') and Destiny (Qadar). Approximately, I had pointed to the most important and main demands. Our method in all lessons is to seek brevity and abbreviation. Thus, we covered the main points regarding the demands related to this issue. Today's discussion serves as a continuation of the past lesson, providing clarifications to add to our previous speech.

To summarize the past lesson—in case the demands were forgotten between then and now—I will briefly repeat what I mentioned first. I provided the linguistic meaning for Decree and for Destiny. I stated that **Destiny (Qadar) is derived from measurement (Taqdir).** The Noble Book pointed to this meaning in the forty-ninth verse from Surah Al-Qamar:
Indeed, all things We created with predestination (Qadar).

Destiny is taken from measurement, which carries the meaning of a standard, placing a thing within a specific measure. Thus, every thing the Creator created, He made within specific limits, measurements, and a specific predestination. Therefore, if we look at any creature, specific limits bound it: from the aspect of lifespan, progression, growth, change in shape, apparent descriptions, deposited capabilities, interaction with its surroundings, and its effectiveness and influence on surrounding things. All of that the Creator made with a measurement and a standard according to Divine Wisdom and His Will, the Glorified and Exalted.

As for **Decree (Qada') in the language, it comes with the meaning of completion.** Decreeing a thing means finishing it. Therefore, I also mentioned that the one who resolves the disputes and conflicts of people is called a Judge (Qadi). He gives a ruling that completes and ends the dispute. Decree is completion and finishing.
This meaning was also pointed to by the Noble Book in the one hundred and seventeenth verse of Surah Al-Baqarah:
Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is.

The Originator of the heavens and the earth initiated creation, establishing it without a similar precedent or another creator having made something resembling it. **Decree is completion and perfection.** This is approximately the overall linguistic meaning for Decree and Destiny, as pointed out in some honorable verses of the Noble Book.
Segment 2: Imam Ali's Lesson on Fleeing from Decree to Destiny

To clarify the practical meaning of these terms, the Sheikh recalls the story of Imam Ali avoiding a collapsing wall. When asked if he was fleeing Allah's Decree, the Imam replied he was fleeing from Allah's Decree to His Destiny. The Sheikh uses this to demonstrate that Qada' represents the absolute law (a falling wall crushes what is beneath it), while Qadar involves the specific measurements and choices within those laws (a human body cannot withstand the impact, so the human must choose to move).

I also mentioned a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. When he passed beside a leaning wall, he hurried his pace to the other side of the road. It was said to him: "O Commander of the Faithful, are you fleeing from the Decree of Allah?" He said:
Yes, I flee from the Decree of Allah to His Destiny!

This makes this honorable narration one of the clearest and most famous in clarifying the intended meaning of the Decree of Allah and His Destiny. I point to this narration briefly; otherwise, providing the full speech would be lengthy.

After this, I pointed out that **the reality of Destiny was not clear to human beings, and the human intellect could not comprehend its true reality.** I referenced the speech which the Imam of the Ummah, may Allah's pleasure be upon him, cited in his book **Misbah al-Hidayah**. He cited the narration which our Sheikh Al-Saduq narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, regarding the meaning of Destiny: he interpreted it as a secret from the secret of Allah, a veil from the veil of Allah, and a sanctuary from the sanctuary of Allah.

But the conclusion we reached is that the truth and true meaning of Destiny cannot be encompassed or comprehended by human intellects. These are approximately the most important demands I pointed to in the past lesson. Today serves as a continuation and footnotes on that lesson, as these are the most critical demands researched in this chapter.

Today we glance at the narrations reported from the Imams, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all, regarding the meaning of Decree and Destiny. Before taking them up, I note that many were mentioned by our Sheikh Al-Saduq in his book **Al-Tawhid**. He gathered a very large and significant group of narrations in the chapter of Decree and Destiny. If we wanted to study these honorable narrations in detail, we must categorize them into groups. Because every group of these narrations looks at a specific meaning or aspect of Decree and Destiny.
Segment 3: The Inaccessibility of Ultimate Truths

The Sheikh expands on the idea that the deepest realities of religious concepts—like the Throne ('Arsh), the Intellect ('Aql), the Soul (Ruh), and Destiny (Qadar)—are fundamentally hidden from the human intellect. He explains that while philosophers, theologians, and mystics provide definitions, these definitions only describe the characteristics or effects of these things, not their true essence. Therefore, any attempt to fully define or encompass the reality of Destiny is an exercise in futility.

As I said a little while ago, **the true, real meaning and secrets of Decree and Destiny were not clarified even by the honorable narrations** because, as the narration stated, it is a secret from the secrets of Allah. Despite the abundance of our honorable narrations in this chapter, they did not reveal the ultimate reality of Decree and Destiny.

Therefore, when we review what was written about Decree and Destiny in the books of theology (Kalam), philosophy, or the rational sciences (Ma'qul) in general, we find divergent statements and interpretations. Every interpretation perhaps looks at one aspect without encompassing all aspects, even those reported in the narrations.

There are many topics, not just Decree and Destiny, whose full reality was not clarified in the honorable narrations. Narrations talk about the topic, but do not reveal its true reality; rather, they talk about its characteristics, opposites, aspects, or sides. For example, the **Throne ('Arsh)**. Many narrations were reported regarding its descriptions and meaning, but even if we gathered them all, we cannot comprehend the reality of the Throne through them.

Therefore, theologians interpreted the Throne as one thing, philosophers as another, and mystics ('Urafa) as yet another. Every sect among them interpreted it with a different meaning. We do not say their meanings are incorrect, but they represent only a face or an aspect; they do not reveal the absolute truth.

The honorable narrations are the same regarding the Chair (Kursi), the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz), Alteration (Bada'), and Decree and Destiny. This is even true for things related to the human, such as the intellect or the soul. **To this day, we have not found a true definition for the intellect, the heart, or the reality of the soul** in theology, philosophy, mysticism, or even the narrations.

**All philosophers agree that definitions in general do not reveal the absolute realities of things!** This relates to Complete Definitions (Hudud Tammah) and Incomplete Definitions (Hudud Naqisah) in logic or wisdom. Defining by the proximate genus and the differentia, or the proximate genus and the specific property, expresses these limits.

They say complete definitions reveal the truths of things, but even then, they admit this is by way of metaphor. Even the complete definition does not reveal the ultimate truth, because **the human cannot comprehend the truths of the differentiae (Fusal).** What logicians or philosophers call differentiae are in truth just characteristics, not true differentiae.

If the human cannot comprehend the differentia—the essence which distinguishes something, like rationality (Natiqiyyah) in the definition of a human—how can they know the absolute truth? Rationality distinguishes humans from other creatures, but were philosophers able to comprehend the absolute truth of rationality? They admit they have not.

When they express "neighing," they don't just mean the sound; they intend the essence that distinguishes the horse from other animals, an essential, core issue. This is the true differentia in logic, but humans are unable to know it. Therefore, a philosopher cannot give a definition that reveals the actual, full reality of anything.
Therefore, the supplication transmitted from the Prophet, may Allah bless him and his family, states:
O Allah, show us the truths upon their realities!

This requires a power beyond our rational and explanatory capabilities. With our current power, we cannot comprehend truths upon their realities; we only comprehend information revolving around them. This supplication is a clear indication that humans cannot comprehend the truths of truths except with an addition—an illumination from a power outside rational perception.
Segment 4: The Inaccessible Secrets of Destiny (Sirr Allah)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi recounts another powerful interaction involving Imam Ali. A man repeatedly asks the Imam to explain Destiny (Qadar). The Imam sequentially describes it as a "deep sea," a "dark path," and finally "Allah's secret," warning the man not to burden himself by seeking what is intentionally hidden. The Sheikh explains that while Allah's Mercy precedes His Destiny (the overarching principle), the exact mechanics of how Destiny operates are sealed away from the human intellect.

Therefore, the Prophets' capabilities and intellects are closer to knowing truths than ordinary people. The Prophets also differ in their strength of perception, as found in the hadiths. The vastest power of perception, surpassing all human and prophetic stages, is deposited in the first creation: the Great Prophet and his family, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all.

I do not want to detail this topic further, but it supports our conclusion that the truth and secrets of Destiny cannot be comprehended by humans. Yes, the concise linguistic meanings, the indications in the Noble Book, and the honorable narrations all represent a face pointing to the truth. But this whole speech remains on the periphery.

The meaning of Destiny, as the narration expressed, is a **secret from the secret of Allah, a cover from His cover, and a sanctuary from His sanctuary.** Human intellects lack the expertise to comprehend its reality.

Therefore, the words of theologians, mystics, and philosophers differed in defining a clear meaning between Decree and Destiny. I do not say their words are incorrect, but the intellects of the people of rational sciences only reached so far. Even the narrations spoke according to what intellects can appropriate; they did not reveal the absolute truth of the secret of Destiny.
When scholars like Sheikh Al-Saduq gather hadiths on Decree and Destiny, they gather usages of the words. But here, we speak about the issue connected to Divine Monotheism and Divine Justice.
Let me point to another narration I deferred from the past week. A man came to the Commander of the Faithful, peace be upon him, and said:
O Commander of the Faithful, inform me about Destiny.
He said:
A deep sea.
The man repeated: "O Commander of the Faithful, inform me." He said:
A dark path, do not traverse it.
The man asked again. He said:
The secret of Allah, do not burden yourself with it.

This supports the human's inability to comprehend this reality. The Prince told him it was a deep sea, a dark path, and finally, Allah's secret, warning him not to burden himself searching for what he cannot know (becoming a Mutakallif).

When the man was not convinced, the Commander of the Faithful asked him: "Tell me, was Allah's mercy for the servants before the deeds of the servants? Or were the deeds of the servants before the mercy of Allah?" The man replied: "Rather, Allah's mercy for the servants was before their deeds." The Prince then said: "Stand and greet your brother. For he has submitted, and he was previously a disbeliever."

In this speech, the Commander of the Faithful did not clarify the ultimate truth of Destiny. Rather, he spoke about an aspect of it: that **Allah's Mercy precedes deeds.** He established a foundational law that Mercy preceded everything, representing the first ranks of existence. "And My Mercy encompasses everything." "My Mercy preceded My Wrath." The Prince's words confirmed three times that human intellect cannot comprehend the absolute reality of Destiny.
Segment 5: The Source of Destiny: The "Perfect Man" (Al-Insan Al-Kamil)

Concluding the episode, Sheikh al-Ghizzi draws on the mystical insights of Imam Khomeini in Misbah al-Hidayah to explain the deepest level of destiny. Destiny is intrinsically linked to Bada' (Divine Alteration). The ultimate knowledge of Destiny is the "Lordly Knowledge" ('Ilm Rububi), which is hidden from all creations, including angels and Prophets, except for the "Perfect Man" (Al-Insan Al-Kamil)—specifically Prophet Muhammad and his Ahlulbayt. Only they possess this unmediated access to the Absolute Unseen, and any knowledge other Prophets have is derived from them.

The narration continues: "The man set off not far, then returned and asked: 'O Commander of the Faithful, by the first Volition (Mashiyyah) do we stand, sit, and grasp the images?'" You notice he questions the Volition, which has a clear link with Decree and Destiny. I pointed out earlier that Decree and Destiny are two creations of Allah, a meaning clarified by joining it with the narration that Allah created Volition by itself, then created creation by the Volition.

He asked if we exist in all states by the first Volition. The Commander of the Faithful replied: "And you are still researching the Volition," meaning the man was asking about the branches (Decree and Destiny) without knowing the roots (Volition). "Allah will not make a way out for you in any of them."

The Prince asked: "Did Allah create the servants as He willed, or as they willed?" He answered: "As He willed." The Prince asked: "Will they come to Him on the Day of Resurrection as He willed, or as they willed?" He answered: "As He willed." The Prince concluded: **"So the servant has nothing of the Volition. The Volition is stored with Allah."**

Therefore, Destiny is stored in the Volition. Volition is the decision of Allah, and Destiny is from His secrets. The Volition is stored in the Knowledge of Allah, and Destiny is stored by the Volition of Allah, the Glorified and Exalted.

The narration from the Commander of the Faithful highlights the inability of human intellects, lords of creation, owners of stations, and even prophetic intellects to realize these realities. The Volition is not comprehended except by the manifestation of the Volition, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. Therefore, the **secrets of Decree and Destiny are encompassed in knowledge solely by His family.**

We find this clear in the words of the Imam of the Ummah, may Allah's pleasure be upon him, in his book **Misbah al-Hidayah** (page 31, 35th lamp). He states: "This Presence is the Presence of the Lordly Decree and Destiny."

In the 36th lamp, he says: "Now you can know, by the permission of Allah, the reality of the hadith reported in Jami' al-Kafi (the Great Comprehensive Book of Hadiths by al-Kulayni) in the chapter of Alteration (Bada')." The hadith states:
Indeed, Allah has a Knowledge, a hidden, stored Knowledge which no one knows but Him!
This divides Divine Knowledge. The hidden, stored Knowledge is specific to Him. "From that is the Alteration (Bada')." The hadith continues:
And a Knowledge He taught to His angels, His messengers, and His prophets. So we know it.

The Imam of the Ummah commented on this: "The Guardian of Allah spoke the truth! For the origin of Bada' is the Presence of Fixed Entities which no one knows but Him. And being informed of the Fixed Entity... which happens for some of the close friends (Awliya), like the **Perfect Man (Al-Insan Al-Kamil).**"
The term "Perfect Man" refers originally to our Prophet, may Allah bless him and his family, and the Infallible Imams. Its application to other Prophets is only by subordination and metaphor.

"And being informed of the Fixed Entity... for the Perfect Man, is considered from the **Lordly Knowledge ('Ilm Rububi)**, not the knowledge of the Prophets!" Meaning, the angels, messengers, and prophets learn the second Knowledge, but the Ahlulbayt know the Lordly Knowledge. No one is informed of it except the Perfect Man.
As Imam al-Baqir said:
By Allah, Muhammad is from those whom He was pleased with, so He gave him the Knowledge of the Unseen.
Giving the Knowledge of the Unseen in its reality was to the Prophet and his family. For the rest of the Prophets, it came by subordination, by the pouring of the Muhammadan Reality upon them.

The conclusion is that despite linguistic meanings, Quranic signals, and narrations clarifying aspects of Destiny and Decree, **the ultimate truth of Destiny remains unknown. It remains a secret from the secret of Allah.** The truth has tied the intellects from comprehending its absolute secrets.

From here, we have clarified the general outlines relating to the issue of Decree and Destiny. God willing, we will take up branching topics in future lessons. And our final prayer is that praise be to Allah the Lord of the worlds.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Doctrine of Bada' According to the Shia of Ahlulbayt Part 1
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 20

In the twentieth episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi transitions from the discussion on Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar) to a closely related and deeply profound Shia theological concept: **The Doctrine of Alteration (Al-Bada')**. He begins by defining Bada' linguistically as "appearance" or a change in intention due to newly acquired knowledge—a limitation strictly forbidden when applied to Allah, whose knowledge is eternal and perfect. The Sheikh emphatically clarifies that **all Shia scholars, philosophers, and infallible traditions absolutely reject the idea that Bada' implies ignorance or a change in God's eternal knowledge.** Instead, Bada' represents a change in the **manifestation** of destiny to the creations (angels, prophets, or ordinary humans). The episode systematically reviews the various interpretations of Bada' proposed by prominent Shia scholars (such as Sheikh Al-Saduq, Sayyid Al-Murtada, Mirdamad, Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i, Al-Fayd Al-Kashani, Allamah Majlisi, and Sayyid Al-Qadi), concluding that while their philosophical frameworks differ, they unanimously agree that **the alteration occurs in the "Tablet of Erasure and Establishment" (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat), while God's ultimate knowledge in the "Preserved Tablet" (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz) remains unchanging.**
Segment 1: Linguistic Meaning of Bada' and the Prohibition of Attributing Ignorance to Allah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi introduces the concept of Bada'. He explains its linguistic meaning as "to appear" or "to change one's mind" after discovering something new. He emphatically warns that attributing this human limitation to Allah is utter disbelief (Kufr). The Shia absolutely reject the accusation that they believe Allah changes His mind because He was unaware of future consequences.

In the Name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and may Allah bless our Master and Prophet, Muhammad, and his pure, immaculate family. May His permanent curse be upon their enemies and those who wrong them, all until the Day of Judgment.

Our discussion in the past lesson of beliefs regarded the meaning of Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar). At that time, I mentioned that this research falls under the studies of Divine Justice, Predestination, Free Will, and likewise **Bada'**. Tonight, our discussion will be on the doctrine of Bada' which we, the Imamiyyah, believe in.

Researching this topic requires much detail, but I will summarize the discussion in this lesson, and the rest will come in the next, God willing. First, what is the meaning of Bada'? In the Arabic language, **Bada' means "appearance."** For example, "It appeared to you," "It appeared to me that the matter is such," or "So-and-so appeared from afar," meaning he became visible.

For a human, Bada' means that something new appears to him, altering his actions or words. This is solely a result of human ignorance and lack of prior knowledge. A person resolves to do a specific action for a perceived benefit within the limits of his knowledge. Later, it becomes clear that the harm outweighs the benefit, so he turns away from that action. He might regret his choice. Therefore, Bada' in a human is the result of regret, ignorance regarding outcomes, or a lack of knowledge regarding the true nature of matters. When the truth becomes clear, his intentions and resolve change. This all traces back to his ignorance.

I brought up this linguistic meaning specifically to clarify how Bada' relates to humans. **As for applying this meaning to the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted, it is absolutely impossible!** It is impossible to say that benefits were hidden from the Creator, or that He substituted His initial decree because He did not know the truth of a matter. To claim that God's command or will changed because a new truth became apparent to Him is a statement that can only be made by a disbeliever in Allah.

Therefore, the honorable narrations commanded us to disassociate from anyone who holds this view. Yes, the general public accuses us of saying this. However, in our authentic narrations and infallible hadiths, there is a strict command to disassociate from anyone who believes that the Creator had the truth of any matter hidden from Him, and that His will changed due to a newly discovered clarification. Such a claim is outright disbelief.
Segment 2: The Core Shia Belief in Bada' and the Views of Early Scholars (Al-Saduq, Al-Murtada, Al-Tusi)

The Sheikh clarifies the true Shia position: Bada' never occurs within Allah's eternal knowledge. He then begins to summarize the various scholarly interpretations of Bada', starting with Sheikh Al-Saduq (who viewed it as changes in creation or lifespan based on human actions), and then moving to Sayyid Al-Murtada and Sheikh Al-Tusi (who interpreted Bada' primarily as abrogation or Naskh in religious law).

What, then, is our overall belief regarding Bada'? We do not say that Bada' occurs within Allah's eternal knowledge. **It is the consensus of Imamiyyah scholars, philosophers, and all Quranic and infallible texts that Bada' does not occur in the Eternal Divine Knowledge!** Everything is already present in the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified and Exalted. Bada' only occurs in a specific realm or rank of existence.

As for the true reality of Bada' according to different scholarly sayings, I will now briefly outline a group of these views. To detail them fully would require several lessons. I am summarizing the discussion to align with our premise: presenting a concise, authentic picture of Shia beliefs narrated from the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them.

First, consider our Sheikh Al-Saduq, may Allah's pleasure be upon him, and what he mentioned in his book Al-Tawhid. After citing the narrations of Bada' reported from the Infallibles, he clarified his opinion by defining Bada' through its practical applications—instances where Allah manifests Bada'.

Sheikh Al-Saduq stated: The intended meaning of Bada' in the infallible hadiths is that Allah intends to create a specific creature first. Then, He originates another creature instead. Or, He commands a thing and later forbids that exact same thing, or vice versa.
