This is evident in divine laws: He commanded the servants with the Abrahamic and Mosaic laws, then abrogated them with the Isaian (Jesus) law, and finally the Muhammadan law. Another example is changing the Qiblah from Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem to the Kaaba in Mecca.

Furthermore, a servant might be destined for a certain lifespan, but due to committing a sin, the Creator shortens it. Conversely, if he maintains family ties, the Creator lengthens it. Sheikh Al-Saduq spoke almost entirely about these practical meanings: originating or annihilating existents, abrogating laws, and lengthening or shortening lifespans and sustenance based on human actions.

Sheikh Al-Saduq concludes: The true belief in Bada'—and what is meant when narrations say "Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'"—is the profound realization that all matters return to the Creator! He is the one who lengthens, shortens, originates, annihilates, honors, and abrogates. And all of this definitively stems from a Knower Who entirely encompasses the truths and benefits of all things.

Next is the statement of Sayyid Al-Murtada, may Allah have mercy upon him. He said: **"Bada' is abrogation (Naskh). Bada' and abrogation are the same, and abrogation is only in legislation."** Sayyid Al-Murtada understood Bada' as the abrogation of laws, such as changing the Qiblah or lifting the ruling of the verse of private consultation (Najwa).

Our Sheikh Al-Tusi, the Sheikh of the Sect, similarly stated that Bada' is abrogation. In his book Al-Ghaybah (The Occultation), when discussing the timing of the Appearance of the Imam (prayers of Allah be upon him) and how the promised relief was sometimes delayed, he expanded slightly on the meaning of Bada', though he kept the discussion concise.
Segment 3: The Views of Mirdamad and Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i

The Sheikh moves to later philosophical views. Mirdamad posited that Bada' is the equivalent of abrogation but in the realm of creation (Takwin), whereas abrogation occurs in legislation (Tashri'). He argued this change only happens within the bounds of time and space, not in the eternal realm. Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i suggested that the unchanging universal laws exist in the realm of Qada' (Decree), but Bada' occurs in the realm of Qadar (Destiny)—when those universal laws are practically applied to the physical world, where external conditions (like giving charity) can alter the expected outcome.

The third view is that of Mirdamad, the teacher of Mulla Sadra. His famous phrase is: **"Bada' is abrogation in creation (Takwin), and abrogation is Bada' in legislation (Tashri')."** He suggests that both concepts involve cutting off the flow of a prior ruling to introduce a new one. In the realm of creation, the "emanation" (Ifadah) of the first measurement is cut off by the arrival of the second measurement.

To understand Mirdamad requires grasping his philosophical framework: our natural, physical world is bound by the vessels of time and space. Beyond this is the eternal vessel (Wa'a' Al-Dahr), a stable world where change does not occur. Mirdamad argues that Bada' only occurs in the lower world restricted by time and space! In the eternal realm, there is no Bada'. The erasing of the first measurement and the arrival of the second is specific to our worldly, earthen existence.

The fourth opinion comes from Sheikh Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i. He stated that **the realm of Decree (Qada') is the realm of universals (Kulliyat), and the realm of Destiny (Qadar) is the realm of the application of those universals in external existence.** The realm of Decree contains the fixed cosmic laws established by the Creator in the higher worlds (the realm of Dominion/Malakut). The realm of Destiny is our daily world, where those universal laws manifest in detail. He points to the narration of Imam Ali passing a leaning wall. The universal Decree dictates that a leaning wall falls, and a human body crushed beneath it will perish. However, this outcome only occurs if all conditions gather—specifically, the human remaining under the wall. If the human moves, the condition changes, and the wall falls without harming him.

Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i says that Bada' occurs strictly in this circle of application. The universal laws in the realm of Decree do not change; rather, the alteration happens in the stage of application. For example, charity repels affliction.

This is illustrated by a well-known story involving Jesus (peace be upon him). He passed by a wedding and informed his companions that the bride would die that night. The next day, she was still alive. Jesus went to her house and asked what she had done. She explained that she had given charity to a poor man who visited during the wedding preparations. Jesus instructed her to lift her bed, revealing a dead snake that had bitten its own tail. The snake was present according to the universal rules of Decree, destined to strike. However, the charity acted as a preventative condition. The money altered the application of the universal law in the external realm. Thus, Bada' occurs in the realm of Destiny, the realm of external existence.
Segment 4: The Two Tablets: The Preserved Tablet and the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment

The Sheikh explores the views of Mulla Sadra, Al-Fayd Al-Kashani, and Allamah Majlisi, who rely on the concepts of the "Preserved Tablet" (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz) and the "Tablet of Erasure and Establishment" (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat). Mulla Sadra believed that the Preserved Tablet contains complete, unchangeable knowledge, but when this knowledge descends to the angels, it sometimes lacks specific conditions, leading to apparent changes. Al-Fayd Al-Kashani and Majlisi similarly argued that angels and Prophets receive their knowledge from the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment, where conditional destinies reside, and when conditions (like charity or prayer) change, the destiny changes. Allah's ultimate knowledge in the Preserved Tablet, however, never changes.

Mulla Sadra conceptualized Bada' through the "Preserved Tablet" (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz). This Tablet contains all absolute and restricted truths, generalities, specifics, and particulars. From the Preserved Tablet, knowledge emanates to the higher souls and the worker angels who manage affairs under Divine authority. However, the emanation they receive is often summarized; it does not contain every exhaustive detail or future condition.

Bada' occurs in the knowledge of these high souls—the realm expressed as the "Realm of Erasure and Establishment" ('Alam al-Mahw wal-Ithbat). When new details or conditions descend, the knowledge possessed by these high souls changes. The Bada' is in their reception of the knowledge, not in the eternal knowledge of Allah.

Al-Fayd Al-Kashani offered a similar view when explaining the book Al-Kafi. He stated that Bada' occurs in the knowledge of the managing angels. For example, the Angel of Death, Azrael (peace be upon him), may know a person's lifespan is set for a specific day. However, if that person gives charity, his lifespan is extended. Azrael received the initial summarized knowledge through intermediaries from the Preserved Tablet, but the conditional causes (like the charity) were not transferred to him. When the condition is met, the angel's knowledge changes. Thus, Bada' happens internally within the angel's knowledge.

This explains why Prophets sometimes prophesy events that do not occur. When the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) told a Jewish woodcutter—who had maliciously greeted him with "Al-Sam 'Alaykum" (Death be upon you)—that he would die the next day, the man actually survived because he gave charity, which protected him from a venomous snake hidden in his firewood. The Prophet's initial prophecy was based on the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. Announcing these conditional destinies encourages servants to do righteous deeds, proving that supplication and charity genuinely repel decrees.

Our Sheikh Al-Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar synthesized this. He affirmed the existence of the Preserved Tablet (containing definitive, unchangeable knowledge based on Allah's eternal knowledge) and the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. The angels take their knowledge from the latter. If a servant does a righteous deed, the Creator alters the measurement in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. The original measurement remains in the Preserved Tablet, which relies on the Eternal Divine Knowledge that encompasses all things before they were even born.

Therefore, when we say "It appeared to Allah" (Bada' lillah), we mean a new destiny became apparent **from** Allah to His creation, not that Allah discovered something new. It is a manifestation of His grace to His angels and servants.
Segment 5: The Ultimate Secret of Bada' and Conclusion

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes by reiterating that despite all these philosophical and theological models, the true reality of Bada'—like Destiny—remains a divine secret. He notes that while scholars agree that Bada' is not a change in Allah's eternal knowledge, but rather a change in what is manifested to creation, the exact mechanics of this process are hidden. The human intellect is simply incapable of piercing the veil of the Absolute Unseen.

Sayyid Al-Qadi conceptualized Bada' through the philosophical concept of the "complete cause" (Illah Tammah). A complete cause is the sum of all factors required to bring something into existence. If the complete cause is achieved with all its conditions fulfilled, the outcome is inevitable, and Bada' does not happen (excluding miracles, which are deliberate breaches of natural law by the Creator).

However, if a preventative factor arises, or a necessary condition is missing, Bada' occurs. Returning to Imam Ali and the leaning wall: if a man stands under a falling wall, the complete cause for his destruction is achieved. But if he moves away, the condition changes, and the complete cause is broken. Similarly, the Jewish woodcutter was destined to be bitten by the snake, but his charity acted as a preventative factor, removing the danger and completing the requirements for his survival.
While I have briefly reviewed these various scholarly opinions, detailing the proofs for each would require several lessons. However, what can we extract from them?

First, absolutely none of our scholars say that Bada' implies a change in Allah's Eternal Divine Knowledge. The narrations command us to disassociate from anyone who claims God's knowledge changes. Despite their different philosophical, theological, or narrational orientations, all Shia scholars unanimously agree that Allah's Knowledge is eternal and encompasses everything.

Second, all these opinions indicate that Bada' is merely an **appearance** or manifestation for the creations. The matter appears to change for the humans, the Prophets, or the angels (like Azrael). The change occurs in their theoretical knowledge or within the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment, while the Preserved Tablet remains fixed.

Third, despite general agreement on these frameworks, the ultimate, true reality of Bada'—just like Destiny and Decree—remains a deep secret of Allah. As the narrations state, Destiny is a deep sea and a dark path; human intellects simply cannot reach the absolute secrets of Bada'.

As I have stated previously, the narrations often define things by their traces and effects, rather than their ultimate essence. Just as philosophers cannot fully define the absolute reality of the human intellect or the soul, we cannot fully define the Throne, the Chair, or the Preserved Tablet. These definitions revolve in the orbit of the truth but do not encompass it entirely.
Therefore, while we know Bada' does not imply ignorance in Allah, and that it represents a change in conditional destinies manifested to creation, its deepest mechanics remain veiled.
This concludes our discussion on this topic for today. Next week, God willing, if we remain alive, we will continue exploring our authentic Shia beliefs according to the narrations of the Ahlulbayt.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Doctrine of Bada' According to the Shia of Ahlulbayt Part 2
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 21

In the twenty-first episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues the profound exploration of **The Doctrine of Alteration (Al-Bada')** according to the teachings of the Ahlulbayt. He begins with a comprehensive recap of the previous lesson, reiterating the linguistic meaning of Bada' and the absolute prohibition of attributing ignorance or a change of eternal knowledge to Allah. The Sheikh then meticulously reviews the various theological and philosophical interpretations of Bada' presented by leading Shia scholars (Al-Saduq, Al-Murtada, Al-Tusi, Al-Mufid, Mirdamad, Ibn Abi Jumhur, Al-Kashani, and Al-Majlisi). Despite their differing frameworks—ranging from abrogation in legislation to changes in the "Tablet of Erasure and Establishment"—all agree that **God's eternal knowledge in the Preserved Tablet is immutable, and Bada' only occurs in the realm of creation and manifestation.** The episode highlights that belief in Bada' is the cornerstone of religious life, deeply intertwined with the concepts of hope, fear, repentance, and supplication, affirming that Allah remains actively sovereign over His creation.
Segment 1: Recap of the Linguistic Meaning of Bada' and its Prohibition Regarding Allah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi starts by summarizing the linguistic meaning of Bada', which means "to appear" or for a matter to become clear after being hidden. He emphatically restates that when applied to humans, Bada' implies ignorance or a change of mind due to new information. However, attributing this human limitation to Allah is fundamentally incompatible with Monotheism. The Shia strictly believe that Bada' does not mean a change in God's eternal, perfect knowledge.

In the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and may Allah bless our Master and Prophet, Muhammad, and his pure, immaculate family. May His permanent curse be upon their enemies until the Day of Judgment.

In our past lesson, before commencing with the meaning of Bada' according to the belief narrated from our Imams (prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all), our discussion centered on the meaning of Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar). Today, I will present the theological speech regarding the meaning of Bada' entirely. Before taking up a new demand, I will repeat what was mentioned in the past lesson.

I mentioned the linguistic meaning for the word Bada', which means "appearance" in the language of the Arabs. For a human, it means his opinion changed regarding a matter due to regret, or due to newly acquiring knowledge of a benefit or harm, causing his intention to change.

And Bada' in this meaning only applies to the human! This meaning is entirely invalid in the belief of Monotheism if we intended it for the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted. **Applying this to God would mean attributing ignorance and change to the Creator.** Therefore, the doctrine of Bada' which the Imamiyyah believes in does not carry this meaning and conceptualization.

In the past lesson, I reviewed a group of opinions from our great scholars conceptualizing the doctrine of Bada'. We saw differences in how they conceptualized it. The secret behind that difference is that the true reality of Bada' is not fully clear to us; it is from the specific sciences of the Imams. We are obligated to believe in it in a general manner. Just as the absolute reality of Decree and Destiny was not clarified to us even in the honorable narrations, what the Imams mentioned regarding Bada' is only to approximate the overall picture.
Segment 2: A Review of Scholarly Views on Bada' (Al-Saduq, Al-Murtada, Al-Tusi, Al-Mufid)

The Sheikh briefly revisits the differing conceptualizations of Bada' by early Shia scholars. He notes that Sheikh Al-Saduq viewed Bada' as God substituting one creation or command for another (like changing lifespans). Sayyid Al-Murtada and Sheikh Al-Tusi equated Bada' directly with abrogation (Naskh) in religious laws. Sheikh Al-Mufid explained that as a servant's actions change (e.g., sinning or doing good), God's corresponding decree for their lifespan or sustenance changes accordingly.

From this incomprehensibility arose the difference in conceptualizing the meaning of Bada'. I will quickly review what our Sheikh Al-Saduq (may Allah have mercy upon him) mentioned regarding the doctrine when he pictured it with this conceptualization.

Sheikh Al-Saduq pictured Bada' thus: **If the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted, wants to make a specific creature first in His creation and brings it into existence, He then wills to annihilate it, bringing another creature into existence to replace it as the first.** Or, He commands a thing then forbids it, or forbids a thing then commands it. This is evident in the laws themselves, such as changing the Qiblah, or altering what relates to the sustenance and lifespans of the servants based on their deeds.

His opinion is close to the opinion of Sayyid Al-Murtada, who said that Bada' and abrogation (Naskh) mentioned in the honorable narrations are the exact same thing. This meaning is clear in the honorable narrations that state the Creator manifests Bada' in a ruling. Sayyid Al-Murtada interpreted all narrations talking about Bada' as referring to legislative abrogation. Our Sheikh Al-Tusi in his book Al-Uddah held a similar opinion, though in his book Al-Ghaybah he also applied it to the advancing or delaying of the appearance of the Imam Al-Hujjah.

Our Sheikh Al-Mufid in his book Awa'il al-Maqalat stated that Bada' is built upon what appears from the servant. If a sin appears from the servant, it appears from the Creator a shortening of that servant's lifespan or a change in sustenance. If the servant maintains family ties, it leads to the lengthening of his lifespan. Whatever appears from the servant, the corresponding decree of the Creator appears. This does not mean God learns something new; rather, what the Creator had already written manifests! The Creator eternally knows all these things before creating the creation.
Segment 3: Philosophical Interpretations (Ibn Abi Jumhur, Mirdamad, Al-Kashani)

The Sheikh summarizes the more complex, philosophical views on Bada'. Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i posited that the universal laws reside in the "Tablet of Decree" (Lawh al-Qada'), while Bada' occurs in the "Tablet of Destiny" (Lawh al-Qadar) when those universals are applied to the external world. Mirdamad argued that Bada' only happens within the constraints of time and space, not in the eternal realm. Al-Fayd Al-Kashani believed Bada' occurs within the knowledge of the angels who execute God's commands based on conditional knowledge, while Allah's absolute knowledge remains in the Preserved Tablet.

Our Sheikh Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i stated that there is a Tablet of Decree (Lawh al-Qada') in which universal knowledge and laws are fixed. As for Destiny (Qadar), it is the stage of detailing and bringing down these universals into external reality. Ibn Abi Jumhur says that Bada' occurs in this stage of connection between the universals of Decree and the details of Destiny.

He cited the narration where the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) avoided a leaning wall, saying, "I flee from the Decree of Allah to His Destiny." The universal Decree states that a leaning wall falls, and a human body beneath it will be crushed. However, this only happens if the conditions gather (the human remaining beside the wall). If the human moves, the condition changes, and the wall falls without harming him. Ibn Abi Jumhur says that Bada' occurs in this circle of applying universals to the external world.

Mulla Sadra (the first of the Shia philosophers in the Safavid era) conceptualized that the Preserved Tablet contains complete information, which flows to the high souls and angels. However, the angels only receive definitive results based on current conditions. When new rules and conditions become clear, the knowledge changes with the managing angels. Thus, Bada' occurs in the knowledge of the angels, not in the eternal knowledge of Allah.
Al-Fayd Al-Kashani similarly stated that Bada' only occurs in the second creation (the world of angels and noble souls), not in the first creation (the hidden truths of the Imams).

Sayyid Mirdamad famously stated: **"Bada' is abrogation in creation (Takwin), and abrogation is Bada' in legislation (Tashri')."** He clarified that Bada' only occurs in the temporal vessel (the world of nature restricted by time), and not in the eternal vessel.

Finally, the well-known opinion of our Sheikh Al-Majlisi in Al-Bihar posits the existence of two tablets: the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz), in which all absolute truths and results are fixed, and the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat). Bada' occurs here because the angels and Prophets receive their conditional knowledge from the Tablet of Erasure. The Preserved Tablet experiences no change. This opinion is almost the most agreeable and closest to the narrations of the Ahlulbayt.
Segment 4: Understanding the Foundational Role of Bada'

Sheikh al-Ghizzi reads a key narration from Al-Kafi stating, "Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'." He explains that Bada' is the theological engine behind human action, hope, fear, repentance, and supplication. If the future were absolutely locked and unchangeable, there would be no point in striving, praying, or repenting. Recognizing that Allah can alter destinies based on human choices is the greatest acknowledgment of His absolute power and sovereignty.
Today we take up the narrations cited by Sheikh Al-Kulayni in Al-Kafi in the Book of Monotheism, chapter of Bada'.
The first narration, from Zurarah reporting from Imam Al-Baqir or Imam Al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), states:
Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'.

This insightful narration requires detailing. The doctrine of Bada' entails many principles of behavior in life. By believing in Bada', the human utilizes hope and strives for outcomes in both religious and worldly circles, because he believes outcomes develop according to his striving. If things did not change and striving yielded no results, the human would not act. Even people outside the Shia faith innately believe that striving changes outcomes; this is essentially the doctrine of Bada'.

The bad label attached to the Shia regarding this doctrine is only due to a misunderstanding of the terminology. The truth of Bada' simply means that if the premises change, the results change. While His eternal knowledge does not change, the Creator established this universe upon a dynamic system.

The human's turning to supplication, requesting fulfillment of needs, feeling fear and hope, and optimism all branch from the doctrine of Bada'. If there were no Bada', repentance would be meaningless. The existence of repentance is proof of the existence of Bada'. Therefore, the Imam says: "Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'," because this doctrine is the fundamental premise upon which a servant's relationship with his Lord is anchored.
The second narration, from Hisham bin Salim from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), states:
Allah was not magnified with anything like Bada'.

This phrase points to the greatness of the Creator's absolute power. We feel the greatness of God when we see His capability to influence, advance, delay, generate the non-existent, and annihilate the existent. The doctrine of Bada' asserts that God is capable of changing everything. This absolute, independent power over everything is the true magnification of the Creator.
Segment 5: Quranic Basis and Pre-Prophetic Covenants

The Sheikh analyzes Quranic verses that establish Bada', particularly Surah Ar-Ra'd (13:39): "Allah eliminates what He wills or confirms." He explains that this alteration happens exclusively in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat), not the Preserved Tablet. He also introduces a hadith showing that every Prophet, before being sent, had to agree to three things: acknowledging Allah's servitude, rejecting false gods, and accepting that Allah "advances what He wills and delays what He wills" (the essence of Bada').
Regarding the verse "Allah eliminates what He wills or confirms" (Surah Ar-Ra'd 13:39), which is one of the clearest verses on the doctrine of Bada', our Imam Al-Sadiq said:
And is anything eliminated except what was [tentatively] established? And is anything confirmed except what was not?

This speech refers directly to the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. In this tablet, suspended, conditional matters are established. If conditions are met, the matter is achieved; if not, it is erased. The angels operate based on the information established in this tablet, which is why their work changes when an erasure occurs. Because information in this tablet is conditional, Prophets sometimes warn of punishments that do not ultimately occur, as happened with Prophet Yunus (Jonah).
In the third narration, Muhammad bin Muslim reports from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him):
Allah did not send a Prophet until He took upon him three [pledges].
These are: acknowledging servitude to Allah, removing rivals (false gods), and acknowledging that **He advances what He wills and delays what He wills.**

This means that during the covenant of Prophethood, every Prophet had to believe in the doctrine of Bada'. Acknowledging servitude means acknowledging Lordship. Removing rivals refers to absolute Monotheism. And acknowledging that "He advances what He wills and delays what He wills" is the very definition of Bada'.
Segment 6: The Two Types of Lifespans and God's Hidden Knowledge

The Sheikh analyzes a verse concerning lifespans ("He decreed a term, and a specified term is with Him"). The Imams explain that there are two types of lifespans: a conditional/suspended lifespan (Ajal Mawquf) recorded in the Tablet of Erasure, and a definitive lifespan (Ajal Mahtum) recorded in the Preserved Tablet. The Sheikh also references a hadith stating there are two types of divine knowledge: one hidden and exclusive to Allah (from which Bada' originates), and one taught to Angels and Prophets. The true depth of Bada' remains hidden within Allah's exclusive knowledge, yet it is partially accessible to the Perfect Man (the Prophet and Imams).
Another narration from Zurarah reports that Imam Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) was asked about the verse: "He decreed a term, and a specified term is with Him." The Imam replied:
They are two terms: a definitive term (Ajal Mahtum) and a suspended term (Ajal Mawquf).

The definitive term must occur and is fixed in the Preserved Tablet. The suspended term is recorded in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment, meaning it is capable of change, advancing, delaying, increasing, or decreasing based on human actions (like charity or honoring parents).
If we divide the matters subject to Bada' according to the narrations, there are three categories:
1. The Divine Promise (Mi'ad), which must be achieved and never experiences Bada'.
2. Definitive matters fixed in the Preserved Tablet, where the probability of Bada' is extremely weak.
3. Suspended, conditional matters where the occurrence of Bada' is highly probable based on the gathering of conditions.
In a narration from Malik Al-Juhani, Imam Al-Sadiq was asked about the verse: "Does man not remember that We created him before, while he was nothing?" The Imam replied:
He was measured, not composed.
This means that before physical composition, the human existed as a measurement in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment.
Finally, a narration from Al-Fudayl bin Yasar reports Imam Abu Ja'far saying:

Knowledge is two knowledges: a knowledge with Allah which He did not inform anyone from His creation about, and a knowledge He taught His angels and His messengers... And a knowledge with Him stored; He advances from it what He wills and delays from it what He wills and establishes what He wills.

This confirms that the deepest reality of Bada' originates from God's hidden, stored knowledge. While angels and messengers are taught conditional knowledge, the ultimate stored knowledge is known only to Allah and, through His exclusive granting, to the "vessels of His knowledge"—the Infallible Imams. The Imams are the specific ones who look into the deepest secrets of the Creator.

title: Our Shia Beliefs - The Doctrine of Bada' According to the Shia of Ahlulbayt Part 3
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 22

In the twenty-second episode of Our Shia Beliefs, Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes his extensive exploration of the **Doctrine of Alteration (Al-Bada')** according to the Imamiyyah Shia perspective. He begins by summarizing the previous two lessons, reiterating the linguistic meaning of Bada' and the absolute prohibition of associating ignorance or changing knowledge with Allah's Eternal Essence. He then analyzes specific narrations from Al-Kafi to demonstrate that the Ahlulbayt emphatically declared that all things—past, present, and future—are eternally known to Allah in the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz). The Sheikh explains that Bada' only occurs in the conditional realm of the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat), which angels and prophets access. Ultimately, he reveals why the Imams stated that **"Allah was not worshipped with anything like [the belief in] Bada'."** It is because Bada' is the theological foundation for human hope, free will, striving, repentance, and supplication; without it, fatalism would paralyze all human spiritual and worldly endeavors.
Segment 1: Recap of the Doctrine of Bada' and Scholarly Interpretations

Sheikh al-Ghizzi starts with a comprehensive recap of the previous two lessons on the doctrine of Bada'. He reviews the linguistic definition (to appear, or to change one's mind due to new information) and stresses again that this human limitation cannot be applied to Allah. He then briefly summarizes the differing but complementary views of prominent Shia scholars (Al-Saduq, Al-Murtada, Al-Mufid, Ibn Abi Jumhur, Mirdamad, Al-Kashani, and Al-Majlisi), affirming that despite their varying philosophical and theological frameworks, they all unanimously agree that Bada' does not happen in Allah's eternal knowledge, but rather in the conditional realm of creation and manifestation.

In the Name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Praise be to Allah, and may Allah bless our Master and Prophet, Muhammad, and his pure, immaculate family. May His permanent curse be upon their enemies and their oppressors until the Day of Judgment.

In the previous lesson regarding our beliefs, our discussion centered on the meaning of Decree and Destiny (Al-Qada' wal-Qadar). I noted then that this research aligns with the studies of Divine Justice, Predestination, Free Will, and likewise Bada'. Tonight, our discussion will focus on the doctrine of Bada' as believed by the Imamiyyah.

Researching this topic requires much detail; therefore, I will summarize the discussion in this lesson, and the remainder will follow in the next, God willing. First, what is the meaning of Bada'? In the Arabic language, **Bada' means "appearance."** For example, "It appeared to you," "It appeared to me that the matter is such," or "So-and-so appeared from afar," meaning he became visible.

For a human, Bada' means that a new perspective appears to him in his actions or words. This is solely a result of his ignorance and lack of prior knowledge. He resolves to undertake a specific matter for a perceived benefit within his limited knowledge. Afterward, it becomes clear that the harm outweighs the benefit, causing him to turn away from that action. He might also regret his choice. Therefore, Bada' in a human is the result of regret, ignorance regarding outcomes, or a lack of knowledge regarding the true nature of matters. When the truth becomes clear, his intentions and resolve change. This all traces back to his ignorance.

I brought up this linguistic meaning specifically to clarify how Bada' relates to humans. **As for applying this meaning to the Creator, the Glorified and Exalted, it is absolutely impossible!** It is impossible to claim that benefits were hidden from the Creator, so the matter appeared differently to Him, or that He substituted His initial decree because He did not know the truth of a matter. To claim that God's command or will changed due to newly acquired clarity is a statement that can only be made by a disbeliever in Allah.

Therefore, the honorable narrations commanded us to disassociate from anyone who makes such a claim. Yes, the general public accuses us of holding this belief. However, in our authentic narrations and infallible hadiths, there is a strict command to disassociate from anyone who believes that the Creator had the truth of any matter hidden from Him, and that His will changed due to a new clarification. This is disbelief.

What, then, is our overall belief regarding Bada'? We do not say that Bada' occurs within Allah's eternal knowledge. **It is the consensus of Imamiyyah scholars, philosophers, and all Quranic and infallible texts that Bada' does not occur in the Eternal Divine Knowledge.** Rather, everything is present in the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified and Exalted. Bada' only occurs in a specific realm or rank of existence.

As for the true reality of Bada' according to different scholarly interpretations, I will now briefly review a group of these views. Detailing them fully would require several lessons, but I will attempt to summarize, as my primary intention is brevity in presenting the authentic Shia beliefs narrated from the Ahlulbayt (peace be upon them).

First, consider our Sheikh Al-Saduq (may Allah's pleasure be upon him) and what he mentioned in his book Al-Tawhid. After citing the narrations of Bada', he clarified his opinion by defining Bada' through its practical applications.

Sheikh Al-Saduq stated that the intended meaning of Bada' is that Allah intends to create a specific creature first, but then originates another creature to replace it. Or, He commands a thing and later forbids it, or vice versa. This is evident in divine laws: He commanded the servants with the Abrahamic and Mosaic laws, then abrogated them with the Isaian and Muhammadan laws. Another example is changing the Qiblah from Al-Aqsa Mosque to the Kaaba.

Furthermore, the Creator might destine a specific lifespan for a servant, but due to a sin, He shortens it. Conversely, if the servant maintains family ties, the Creator lengthens it. Sheikh Al-Saduq spoke almost entirely about these practical meanings: abrogating laws and altering lifespans and sustenance based on human actions. He concludes that the true belief in Bada' is realizing that all matters ultimately return to the Creator's active sovereignty. All changes definitively stem from a Knower Who perfectly encompasses the truths and benefits of all things.

Sayyid Al-Murtada stated: **"Bada' is abrogation (Naskh). Bada' and abrogation are the same, and abrogation is only in legislation."** He understood Bada' primarily as the abrogation of religious laws. Our Sheikh Al-Tusi also stated that Bada' is equivalent to abrogation, an opinion closely mirroring Sayyid Al-Murtada's.

Our Sheikh Al-Mufid in Awa'il al-Maqalat stated that Bada' is built upon what appears from the servant. If a sin appears from the servant, a shortening of his lifespan appears from the Creator. Maintaining family ties leads to a lengthened lifespan. Whatever appears from the servant prompts a corresponding decree to appear from the Creator. This does not mean God learns something new; rather, what the Creator had already written simply manifests. The Creator eternally knows all these things before creating the creation.

Likewise, Sheikh Ibn Abi Jumhur Al-Ahsa'i stated that there is a Tablet of Decree (Lawh al-Qada') in which universal laws are fixed. As for Destiny (Qadar), it is the stage of applying these universals into external reality. He argued that Bada' occurs in this transitional stage between universal Decree and detailed Destiny. When universal laws (like a leaning wall eventually falling) meet specific conditions (a person standing beneath it), the outcome can change if the conditions change (the person moving away).

Mirdamad famously stated: **"Bada' is abrogation in creation (Takwin), and abrogation is Bada' in legislation (Tashri')."** He argued that Bada' only occurs in the temporal vessel (the world restricted by time and space), not in the eternal vessel.
Al-Kashani believed Bada' only occurs in the second creation (the world of angels), not in the first creation (the hidden truths of the Imams).

Finally, the well-known opinion of Sheikh Al-Majlisi posits the existence of two tablets: the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz), in which all absolute truths and results are fixed, and the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment (Lawh al-Mahw wal-Ithbat). Bada' occurs because the angels and Prophets receive their conditional knowledge from the Tablet of Erasure. The Preserved Tablet, which relies on Allah's Eternal Divine Knowledge, experiences no change.

Many scholars agree with this framework, finding it closest to the narrations of the Ahlulbayt. With this, we have a clear picture of the meaning of Bada' and the most important scholarly interpretations.
Segment 2: Bada' is Foundational to Worship and Life

The Sheikh transitions to narrations found in Al-Kafi emphasizing the importance of believing in Bada'. He reads a hadith stating: "Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'." He explains that if humans believed that everything was entirely fixed and unchangeable, they would not strive in life, pray, or seek repentance. The innate human drive to act, hope, and fear relies completely on the fundamental truth that Allah can alter destinies based on human choices and prayers.
Today we examine the narrations cited by Sheikh Abu Ja'far in the first part of Al-Kafi, within the Book of Monotheism under the chapter of Bada'.
The first narration, from Zurarah reporting from Imam Al-Baqir or Imam Al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), states:
Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'.

This insightful narration requires detailing. The doctrine of Bada' underpins many principles of human behavior. Believing in Bada' motivates a person to strive in both religious and worldly circles because they believe outcomes develop according to their efforts. If things never changed and striving yielded no results, humanity would cease to act. Innately, humans believe in Bada'; if absolute fatalism dominated them, they would never strive.

The negative label attached to the Shia regarding this doctrine stems solely from a misunderstanding of the terminology. Otherwise, the essence of Bada'—that outcomes change when premises change—is universally understood. The Creator established this universe upon a dynamic system.

Turning to supplication and requesting the fulfillment of needs from the Creator stems from the branches of Bada'. The concepts of fear, good expectation, optimism, and hope in Allah all branch from this doctrine. If there were no Bada', repentance would be meaningless. The existence of repentance is proof of the existence of Bada'. Therefore, the Imam says: "Allah was not worshipped with anything like Bada'," because this doctrine is the fundamental premise upon which a servant's relationship with his Lord is anchored.
The second narration, from Hisham bin Salim from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him), states:
Allah was not magnified with anything like Bada'.

This phrasing points to the greatness of the Creator's absolute power. We feel the greatness of God when we see His capability to influence, advance, delay, generate the non-existent, and annihilate the existent. The doctrine of Bada' asserts that God is capable of changing everything. This absolute, independent power over all things is the true magnification of the Creator.
Segment 3: The Omniscience of Allah

The Sheikh addresses a crucial question: does Bada' mean that something happens today that Allah did not know yesterday? He recites narrations from Imam Al-Sadiq to completely demolish this idea. The Imam states that whoever claims Allah attains new knowledge is a disbeliever, and confirms that Allah knew everything that has happened and everything that will happen until the Day of Judgment before He even created the creation. Thus, Bada' is entirely compatible with absolute divine omniscience.
Another narration from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) addresses the verse: "Allah eliminates what He wills or confirms." He said:
And is anything eliminated except what was [tentatively] established? And is anything confirmed except what was not?

This refers to the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. Suspended matters are those which lack a final, firm divine decree maintaining their stability. They are conditional. If conditions are met, the matter is achieved; if not, it is not achieved. The angels operate based on the information established in this Tablet, which is why their work changes when an erasure occurs.

Because information in this Tablet is conditional, Prophets sometimes prophesy events that ultimately do not occur, as seen in the story of Prophet Yunus (Jonah) and the punishment of his people. Such incidents were based on knowledge derived from the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment.
In a third narration, Muhammad bin Muslim reports from Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him):
Allah did not send a Prophet until He took upon him three [pledges].
First, acknowledging servitude to Allah. Second, removing rivals (false gods). And third, acknowledging that **He advances what He wills and delays what He wills.**

This means that during the covenant of Prophethood, every Prophet had to embrace this doctrine. Acknowledging servitude means acknowledging Lordship; removing rivals refers to absolute Monotheism. Acknowledging that "He advances what He wills and delays what He wills" is the essence of Bada'.
Segment 4: The Two Terms (Ajal) and the Tablet of Erasure

The Sheikh explores the concept of lifespan (Ajal) in relation to Bada'. He cites a narration interpreting the Quranic verse "He decreed a term, and a specified term is with Him." The Imams clarify that there are two types of lifespans: a "Suspended Term" (Ajal Mawquf), which is recorded in the Tablet of Erasure and can be lengthened or shortened by actions like charity or maintaining family ties, and a "Definitive Term" (Ajal Mahtum), which is fixed in the Preserved Tablet and does not change.
In another narration, Zurarah reports from Humran that Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) was asked about the verse: "He decreed a term, and a specified term is with Him." He replied:
They are two terms: a definitive term (Ajal Mahtum) and a suspended term (Ajal Mawquf).

The definitive term must occur and is fixed in the Preserved Tablet. The suspended term is recorded in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. It is capable of change, advancing, delaying, increasing, or decreasing based on human actions. Conditions such as giving charity, maintaining relationships, and performing righteous deeds can lead to increasing a person's lifespan and blessings.
If we divide the matters subject to Bada' according to the narrations, there are three categories:
1. The Divine Promise (Mi'ad), which must be achieved and never experiences Bada'.
2. Definitive matters fixed in the Preserved Tablet, where the probability of Bada' is extremely weak.
3. Suspended, conditional matters where the occurrence of Bada' is highly probable based on the gathering or absence of conditions.

The fifth narration is from Malik Al-Juhani, who asked Abu Abdullah (peace be upon him) about the verse: "Has there not come upon man a period of time when he was not a thing even mentioned?" The Imam replied:
He was measured, not composed.

This indicates that before physical composition, the human existed as a measurement in the Tablet of Erasure and Establishment. The human was measured but not yet composed or mentioned in tangible existence.
Finally, a narration from Al-Fudayl bin Yasar reports Imam Abu Ja'far saying:

Knowledge is divided into two categories: a stored knowledge with Allah which He did not reveal to anyone from His creation, and a knowledge He taught His angels and His messengers... And a knowledge stored with Him; He advances from it what He wills, delays from it what He wills, and establishes what He wills.

This confirms that the deepest reality of Bada' originates from God's hidden, stored knowledge. While angels and messengers are taught conditional knowledge, the ultimate stored knowledge is known only to Allah. However, He grants exclusive access to this stored knowledge to the **"vessels of His knowledge" ('Aibat 'Ilm Allah)—the Infallible Imams.** The Imams are the specific ones whom the Creator entrusts with His deepest secrets.

As we swear in the honorable supplications, we appeal to the Creator based on His supreme generosity. He commands us to be generous, and generosity with one's soul is the ultimate limit of generosity. We address Him saying, "O most generous of the generous," recognizing His absolute power and infinite grace over all creation.

series: The Malignant Qutbist Cancer in the Arena of Shia Culture
title: The Malignant Qutbist Cancer in the Arena of Shia Culture - Episode 1
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 1

In this foundational episode of The Malignant Qutbist Cancer in the Arena of Shia Culture, Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi diagnoses a devastating ideological disease that has infiltrated the Shia world. He argues that the intellectual decay within the Shia community was not imposed by external enemies, but was actively imported by its own religious authorities, thinkers, and preachers. By tracing the historical and ideological roots of the Muslim Brotherhood—spearheaded by Hasan al-Banna and Sayyid Qutb—the Sheikh reveals how their pursuit of an **absolute Imamate** and their justification of terrorism have deeply corrupted modern Shia thought. He emphasizes that the authentic path lies exclusively with the Book and the Itrah, warning that the uncritical adoption of Qutbist ideology by Shia leaders has resulted in a cultural and spiritual catastrophe.
Segment 1: Introduction: The Malignant Qutbist Cancer and the Destruction of the Shia Mind

Sheikh al-Ghizzi introduces his new series by grounding his allegiance in Lady Fatima, declaring absolute loyalty to those she loves and enmity to those she hates. He identifies the core issue the program will address: a "malignant Qutbist cancer" that has deeply infiltrated and corrupted Shia culture. He clarifies that this is not a historical or political program, but rather a "medical dissection" to diagnose a devastating ideological disease. Tragically, he points out that this cancer wasn't imposed by outsiders, but was brought into the Shia milieu by their own religious authorities, thinkers, and preachers, destroying the ideological foundation of multiple generations.
In the name of Allah, the Beneficent, the Merciful. Peace be upon you.

Fatima says, and the speech is directed to me and to all of you, as it came in her well-known sermon: "What is this deficiency regarding my right, and this slumber regarding my grievance? Did not the Messenger of Allah, my father, say: 'A man is preserved through his children'?"

And our Imam, Ja'far al-Sadiq, informs us about what happened to his mother, Fatima. And the narration is in the most reliable of our books, Kamil al-Ziyarat: "And she is beaten, O Fatima! And she is beaten while she is pregnant." Until he says: "And she miscarries what is in her womb from the beating, and she dies from that beating."

And I say, and I do not know if you will say it with me or not, just as it came in her honorable visitation: "I am pleased with whomever you are pleased with, and I am enraged at whomever you are enraged at. I disassociate from whomever you disassociated from, I am loyal to whomever you are loyal to, and I am an enemy to whomever you are an enemy to. I hate whomever you hate, and I love whomever you love. O mother of Al-Hasan and Al-Hussein, I call upon the Master of the Command to bear witness to this belief of mine, in my life and in my death. And I wish, by your success, to meet you upon it in this world and the Hereafter."

My brothers and sisters, my sons and daughters. I will not hide a secret from you: **If you are looking for a vaccination or a cure for salvation from this malignant Qutbist cancer, this is the successful cure for this disastrous disease:** You ally with whomever Fatima allies with, and you show enmity to whomever Fatima shows enmity to—intellectually, heartily, in word, and in deed.

Between your hands now is the first episode of The Malignant Qutbist Cancer in the Arena of Shia Culture. May Allah Almighty protect us and you from it, and purify our intellects and yours, our hearts and yours, and our religion and yours from these filths, by the Wilayah of Fatima and the family of Fatima, prayers of Allah be upon her and upon them. We will go to a break, and after the break, we will return to you.
Today's episode is an introduction. Today's episode includes the answer to a question that places itself on the table: Why this program?

This is the first episode of our program, The Malignant Qutbist Cancer in the Arena of Shia Culture. This episode is an introduction. As for the details, I will commence explaining them tomorrow, God willing, in the second episode and what follows it. And as I said, in this episode, I will answer this question: Why this program?

I will structure my speech into several observations, into main points, call them whatever you wish. We will move between very important observations, through which you will discover the answer to this question: Why this program?

The first point from which I begin: **It is an obsession since the eighties. An obsession that inhabits me since the eighties!** And no suitable opportunity like this one had presented itself before. I direct my speech, in truth, to my sons and daughters from the youth of today and tomorrow. I present this program before your hands; I present a visual book. This program will contain many important demands. It is a visual book, charged with facts and documents.

I am not a historian here. I do not want to talk about the history of these titles mentioned in the introduction of the program: the Muslim Brotherhood, Hasan al-Banna, and Sayyid Qutb. I do not want to talk about the details of what came in the books that chronicled these titles. And if I mention something of history, it is because necessity dictates it, for I am not in the position of chronicling. And I am not in the position of political analysis at all. I have no connection to all the political developments in this program. And if they are mentioned, it is because the scientific and realistic necessity simultaneously dictates it upon me.

I do not want to prove anything to the opponent, the opponent of the Ahlulbayt. Never. I direct my speech to our Shia youth, from my sons and daughters. I am not very optimistic, but I hope that this program will be a document from which our future generations can benefit. Because the Qutbist cancer has consumed these generations in the Shia milieu: the generation of the sixties, the generation of the seventies, eighties, nineties, and even the generation of the two thousands and beyond. **These generations were crushed and ideologically destroyed by the malignant Qutbist cancer spreading in the arena of Shia culture.**

The problem is that it was not the opponents, nor the Qutbists, nor the Muslim Brotherhood who transmitted this disease to us. **Our respected Marjas, our esteemed thinkers, our preachers, our poets, the conscious class as they like to call themselves—they are the ones who transmitted this disastrous disease to us!** And the arena of Shia culture was afflicted with this malignant disease. And this cancer has eaten the Shia mind.

Some might find my speech strange, but if they follow the program, they will hold in their hands the documents, the facts, and the precise details. So as I said, this program is neither a historical program, nor a political program, nor a program meant for discussion or argumentation. Never. **This program is a medical dissection, a diagnosis of symptoms, a determination of the causes of the disease and contagion, and an explanation of the cure.** This is the program.

Therefore, I direct it to my sons and daughters from the youth of the Shia of the Master of the Command, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. Because the previous generations are hopeless! The Qutbist cancer has eaten everything they have, and the cancer has spread in all directions. I do not believe that a dissection, a description, an explanation, or a cure will benefit them.

I return to the obsession of the eighties. Since the eighties, I have been hoping that an opportunity would be made available to me, according to what I desire, to speak on this subject with these details. It was my intention previously to write a book, but I believe that a visual book in our time is far luckier than a book read on paper. And perhaps this visual book will turn into a read book in the coming days. Who knows?
Segment 2: The Infiltration of Qutbist Thought into Shia Leadership

Sheikh al-Ghizzi reveals a shocking truth: the Qutbist cancer did not infiltrate the Shia world through Sunni adversaries, but was actively imported and propagated by leading Shia religious authorities (Marjas), scholars, and preachers since the 1950s. He names several prominent Shia figures and political parties across Iraq, Iran, Lebanon, and the Gulf who embraced the Muslim Brotherhood's ideology. He emphasizes that the religious establishment is deeply infected with this thought, fundamentally distorting the authentic ideological surface of Shia culture.

And I return and say, since the eighties. And when we look into the various directions of the arena of Shia culture, Sayyid Qutb emerges to us! And his ideas become prominent to us in our circles, his books, his miracles that are relayed on our pulpits. I will talk about those who detonated this cancer into the arena of Shia culture.

And since the sixties, since the sixties, rather since the fifties. Since the fifties, the Brotherhood and Qutbist culture has been gnawing at our Shia reality. I will focus the speech on Najaf, on Karbala, on Iraq, but do not imagine that the matter stops at this limit. This is a cancer spreading in all Shia regions: in Iran, in Pakistan among the Shia of Pakistan, and among the Shia of India, and among the Shia of Afghanistan, and in Lebanon, and in the Gulf states, in the Arab Gulf states in Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and in the rest of the Gulf states, and in Yemen, and in Sudan. Wherever there were Shia, few or many, this cancer has gnawed into their Shia cultural arena!

And that is not strange. The religious authority of Sayyid al-Hakim, may Allah's pleasure be upon him, had a major role in strengthening the Qutbist condition in the Shia reality, in the person of Sayyid al-Hakim the Marja', may Allah's pleasure be upon him, and in the persons of his sons and the prominent figures among his sons. The Group of Scholars in Najaf al-Ashraf, headed by a great Marja', Sheikh Murtada Al Yasin. And another brilliant Marja', Sayyid Muhammad Baqir al-Sadr. And a Marja' in Karbala, Sayyid Muhammad al-Shirazi. And an Islamic party in Najaf, the Islamic Dawa Party, and an Islamic organization in Karbala, the Shirazi Marja'iyyah, the Islamic Action Organization.

Many speakers and writers. Sayyid Murtada al-Askari, may Allah have mercy upon him. Sheikh Mahdi al-Asefi, may Allah have mercy upon him. Sheikh Ali al-Kourani, and he later retreated from this direction. Sheikh Muhammad Ali al-Taskhiri and their likes. Sayyid Taqi al-Modaressi from the Karbala side, the master of this school. Sayyid Kamal al-Haydari. Sheikh Hassan al-Saffar. And their likes. And esteemed preachers: Sheikh al-Wa'eli. Sheikh al-Muhajir. Sheikh Fadhil al-Maliki, at that time in his beginnings he was an imitator of Sheikh al-Wa'eli, and many preachers who used to imitate Sheikh al-Wa'eli. And the essence of Sheikh al-Wa'eli's thought is between the ideas of Fakhr al-Razi and the ideas of Sayyid Qutb.

The books, the magazines. Al-Adwaa magazine, Risalat al-Islam magazine. The magazines that were issued by Najaf through the Group of Scholars and through the College of Jurisprudence affiliated with the Islamic Dawa Party. The publications and booklets that were issued from Karbala in the atmosphere of the Shirazi Marja'iyyah, and from the Islamic Action Organization. And the story is long.

These are models. And when the Iraqi Shia opposition was formed in Iran after the victory of the Islamic Revolution in Iran, this matter became concentrated in the Hawza lessons, on the pulpits, in the Husseiniyahs, in public seminars, and everywhere. The issue does not stop at this limit. The matter is exactly the same in Bahrain. The matter is exactly the same in southern Lebanon. The matter is exactly the same in all our Shia regions. Sayyid Muhammad Hussein Fadlallah, Sheikh Muhammad Mahdi Shams al-Din, and their likes. These clear names and prominent personalities, they were all pouring into this same rivulet, or into this swamp, to use a precise phrase, into this Qutbist swamp. Those are facts.

And these facts I will clarify. Some of them retreated from this direction, but the vast majority of these names are still grazing in this same pasture. Thus we were afflicted! **Thus the Shia were afflicted—and I am one of them—with this malignant cancer.**

We cannot blame Sayyid Qutb, for the man is not the one who came to us with this cancer of his. And we cannot blame the Muslim Brotherhood party, for they did not come to us with their cancer. And we cannot blame Egypt, and we cannot blame anyone. **Our problem is within us, and our disease is from us! And those who transmitted this cancer to us are our elders, our Marjas, and our scholars.** The religious establishment, to this day, is drowning in Qutbist thought! Even if it denies this matter with its tongue in these years, but if we want to enter into the depths of the religious establishment, and this is what I will do in this program, we will find that the Qutbist cancer has eaten much, much indeed.

My program, as I said a little while ago, is a dissection of this bitter reality of our Shia reality. I do not speak about other than the Shia reality, and I speak about our religious Shia reality. Absolutely, what branches from it of political affairs will be included in this reality. I do not intend to delve deeply into this aspect, for the world of politics is an open world in which politicians have the right to lie, in which politicians have the right to deny facts, in which politicians have the right to contradict their religion and bring forth any ideology that aligns with their goals and interests. Therefore, I will not prolong my pause much in this direction, but rather I will pass over it swiftly as the context dictates. **Our problem is in the ideological aspect, which is formed from the vocabulary that floats on the surface of the Shia cultural arena.**
This is one of the points that I wanted to point out within an answer to the question: Why this program? But I leave you now with a dramatic break. And after the break, we return to you.
Segment 3: Dramatic Reenactment: The Assassination of Judge Al-Khazindar

The Sheikh presents a 15-minute clip from the Egyptian historical drama series Al-Gama'a (The Brotherhood). The scene depicts the inner workings of the Muslim Brotherhood's secret apparatus. It shows the assassination of Judge Al-Khazindar, ordered by Abd al-Rahman al-Sindi, the head of the secret apparatus, after he interpreted a casual, malicious wish from Hasan al-Banna ("I wish we were rid of him") as a direct assassination order. The scene highlights al-Banna's subsequent denial of responsibility, showcasing the organization's tampering with blood, justice, and truth.

[Scene plays] A disaster upon us. Are you ready for martyrdom in the path of Allah? We are ready. Do you insist on loyalty? I swear by Allah the Great to execute all that is commanded of me and to preserve the secret, and to work in silence. Allah is over what we say a Witness. May Allah bless you. The Group.

[Phone rings] Response to the phones of the General Guide. Peace be upon you. Is the Guide present? There is an important matter I need to inform him of. If you want you can call back after a quarter of an hour like this. If it was a secret matter, okay. Good, God willing. Counselor Al-Khazindar, your eminence the Guide from Helwan, and at his house, they caught two young men like this, your eminence. Are there no more details than that, your eminence the Guide? Thank you, Abd al-Rahman al-Sindi.

[Another scene] It is over, Al-Khazindar reached them. Justice resign how? I can never allow the terrorization of justice, even if it costs my life. We will not listen to criminals, they must receive the severest punishment. A deterrent for all criminals like them. But the results of the investigations reach the state. Goodbye.

[Another scene] The shape of the country, the Egyptians in an intense state of anger and indignation, especially from the Group. Problems from it, the other parties. After the dissolution of the Mansoura branch, they no longer have confidence in the mercy of the Brotherhood. The injustice of the judge does not necessitate killing him, O brother Guide. The Group sees that they are the enemies of Allah, and the earth. What do you say about the enemies of Allah? Excellent. He ruled against this brother Mahmoud because he is a member of the Group. Dr. Assaf and members of the Group are enemies of Allah.

Allow me to say this is an extremely difficult situation. And the assassination of the Counselor is an illegal matter. And by Allah, the speed of assassination is one thing, and Jihad is another. And the results of this incident are a disaster for the Group, without a doubt. Allow me, and I want specific answers, yes or no, to direct questions if you please, our dear brother Khalid. Did you issue a direct and explicit order to Abd al-Rahman al-Sindi to kill Al-Khazindar, or did he take the decision himself? No, he took the order from your eminence the Guide. What is the problem?

What happened after my knowledge of the unjust rulings that Al-Khazindar ruled with, I said to those around me, May our Lord rid us of him, if only someone would rid us of him. Just a wish, nothing more! The questions come, your eminence the Guide, after what I heard, go ahead. Do you carry the blood of Al-Khazindar on your head and meet Allah with it on the Day of Judgment? Then your eminence did not order, and you do not bear this responsibility before Allah.

Brother Abd al-Rahman, you heard your eminence the Guide, from whom then did you take the order? Only from the Professor Guide! Do you carry the blood of Al-Khazindar on your head on the Day of Judgment? I am ready. Can the Professor deny it? Let him deny it, and let him disassociate from bearing the responsibility, and the two young men who were pushed into committing this crime when Professor Hasan al-Banna, your eminence the General Guide says in my presence: I wish we were rid of this man. A statement, and it is binding upon the head of the Secret Apparatus to carry out these actions. The head of the Daw'ah is responsible for those below him, Abd al-Rahman. These actions do not proceed with just a wish. You asked me to be specific, and the answers must be specific. Meaning, who is responsible?

You told me, whether the Professor ordered or did not order, whether it was a wish or an advice, we consider it cold-blooded murder. What happened actually, we magnify the issue because it is not worth it originally. Exactly, from the Brotherhood, a group responsible for it. Meaning, he must punish himself the maximum punishment a few of the heroes of the Brotherhood, and the blood of Al-Khazindar. Meaning, the Group, the individual, the Group, the modifications. Absolutely. The government paid. The government paid the money to the people, ten thousand pounds exactly and completely. And the government is the people's money. And we are from the people, and the people's money is our money. We will hire top lawyers to defend the two boys. The state and we will give them the money they want. What is your opinion, your eminence the Guide? Comforting. Comforting.
Segment 4: The Banning of Historical Truth by Shia Channels

Sheikh al-Ghizzi explains that the preceding clip was taken from the acclaimed Egyptian television series Al-Gama'a (The Brotherhood). He points out a deeply troubling fact: various Shia satellite channels actively banned the broadcasting of this series, particularly Part Two, which exposes the history of Sayyid Qutb. This deliberate censorship proves that elements within the Shia establishment desperately want to conceal the dark history of the Muslim Brotherhood from the Shia public, further proving the deep entrenchment of Qutbist sympathy among Shia leadership.

This scene, and other dramatic scenes that will be shown in this program, are taken, and the rest of the scenes are taken, from an Egyptian TV series titled Al-Gama'a. And it is in two parts. Al-Gama'a Part One, by its author, the well-known Egyptian writer Wahid Hamid. The director is Muhammad Yasin. As for Part Two of this series, it is also by the same author, Mr. Wahid Hamid. As for the director, it is Sherif El-Bendary.

This series, especially Part Two which talks about the history of Sayyid Qutb, Shia entities in Iraq and in other regions banned its broadcast on satellite channels! I do not say that the ban decision reached all channels, for not all channels in Iraq or in other regions show series and show dramas. Satellite channels now in our time are specialized; each channel specializes in a color or a type of programs. Shia entities banned the broadcast of these series! The answer is clear: **There are those in the Shia milieu who do not want the Shia to be informed of the facts!**

Knowing that this drama presented what it presented according to the vision of the writer and the director, and it is fundamentally an artistic vision that corresponds with the Egyptian reality. We have no connection to it. Yes, they are historical documents. What passed in this scene are historical facts present in the sources, and there are many, many, many sources and books from those who knew and mentioned this matter. And with details.

I will not prolong the speech much, but rather I will go to the second point. And you noticed the tampering with people's blood and with rulings! And that is the reality of religious parties, at the head of which is the Muslim Brotherhood.
Segment 5: The Global Damage of the Brotherhood's Terrorism

Sheikh al-Ghizzi outlines his second major point: the immense global damage inflicted by the Muslim Brotherhood, spearheaded by Hasan al-Banna and Sayyid Qutb. He notes that their terrorism harmed Sunnis before anyone else and created a global perception that Muslims and Arabs are abnormal, dangerous beings who must be controlled. He definitively traces modern terrorist groups, like ISIS, directly back to the ideological roots of the Muslim Brotherhood.

The second point that I wanted to point out is the great damage inflicted by the Muslim Brotherhood, headed by al-Banna and Qutb; the great damage they inflicted upon humanity generally. For they harmed the Sunnis before anyone else; the harm of this terrorism backfired with its branches upon the Sunnis before others.

This terrorism is what led Westerners, and led non-Westerners, to this conclusion: that Muslims, and that Arabs, are abnormal beings; therefore, they must be controlled, otherwise, they will harm themselves and harm the world! These are the results that others have reached because of this terrorism founded by Hasan al-Banna, whose structure was elevated by Sayyid Qutb, and practically executed by the Muslim Brotherhood since their beginnings.
And ISIS, and those other than ISIS, are nothing but branching tails of this ominous organization! That is the bare truth. And this is what we will talk about in the coming episodes.
Segment 6: The True Identity of Terrorism and the Complicity of the Shia Establishment

The Sheikh contrasts the physical violence inflicted upon the Shia by terrorist groups with the apologetic stance of the Shia religious establishment. He vehemently rejects the popular slogan repeated by Shia leaders that "terrorism has no religion or sect." He explicitly states that the religion of terrorism is Islam—specifically the Islam of the Saqifah (the Sunni historical narrative)—and its nationality is predominantly Saudi/Arab. He accuses the Shia leadership of mocking the intellects of their followers by denying these clear facts.

As for us, we the Shia, what damage did terrorism inflict upon us? It decimated the sons of the Shia: the killing, the bombing, the crimes committed against our sanctities, the oppression that fell upon us from these savage groups that are tails and threads of this criminal organization.

Another damage is this bad reputation for Islam and its people which attached itself. And our Shia religious establishment, our Shia parties, and our Marjas refuse to isolate themselves from this terrorist reality, and they constantly repeat that terrorism has no religion, has no sect, has no nationalism, has no nationality!

But reality belies that! **The religion of terrorism is Islam according to the reading of the Saqifah!** And its sect is the various Sunni sects, for the terrorists are not of one sect, but from all the sects. And the nationality is Arab, Saudi; the nationalism is Arab. Those who practice terrorism and execute terrorism, if they are not Arabs themselves, then the terrorist leaders and the leaders of criminality are Arabs. That is the clear truth. And when I speak about Saudi Arabia, it is not necessary that this entire speech is about the Saudi government. The Saudis who practiced terrorism and still practice terrorism emerged from the womb of the Saudi religious establishment. This religious establishment may not be in complete agreement with the Saudi government, but it is Saudi as a result.

I do not want to delve into this issue now. But the matter is clear: terrorism has a religion, its religion is Islam! This is Islamic terrorism, this terrorism that is battering the world, which tore us apart. These crimes that slaughtered us, slaughtered the Shia and destroyed the sanctities of the Shia, these crimes are Islamic crimes according to the reading of the Saqifah, and the sects are Sunni sects, the nationality is Saudi, and the nationalism is Arab. Yet our religious establishment talks about how terrorism has no religion! What a mockery of intellects this is, and what a mockery of the truth! And as we say in our popular phrases, Are we hiding behind a sieve?
Segment 7: The Ideological Ruin of the Shia Mind

Sheikh al-Ghizzi identifies the absolute worst damage inflicted upon the Shia: the ideological, cultural, and intellectual ruin of the Shia mind. He boldly claims that modern Shia platforms, Quranic exegeses, and general culture have become overwhelmingly "Qutbist." He traces this intellectual decay back to early scholars like Sheikh al-Tusi, arguing that the Qutbist wave merely deepened the pre-existing infiltration of Ash'arite, Mu'tazilite, and Sufi thoughts into Shia theology, causing modern scholars to abandon the authentic teachings, supplications, and Quranic interpretations of the Ahlulbayt.

As for the great damage, the great damage that afflicted the Shia reality is the ideological ruin. **Our Husseini pulpits are Qutbist! O Husseinis, your Marjas whose pictures you place in the Husseiniyahs drink from Qutbist thought!** The exegesis of our Quran is Qutbist, our culture is Qutbist, and this is what will be explained in the coming episodes with documents, facts, and precise details.

I do not demand that you believe my speech, but listen, and consider the possibility of truth and falsehood in it, and examine and verify for yourselves. I do not demand that you accept my speech just because I am the one saying it; accept the truth for the sake of the truth. For truths carry their value within themselves and have nothing to do with personalities, whether I said it or someone else said it.

The great ideological, cultural, and intellectual damage and ruin that destroyed the Shia mind, the collective mind, the mind of the Shia Ummah, I do not believe that the Ummah will be able to repair it, for me at least. For it has gone very deep. Therefore, I said at the beginning of my speech that my hope is that this program will be a visual book from which our youth in future generations can benefit. As for the youth from my sons and daughters, I do not think they will benefit much from this program. Because the Qutbist culture, if it hasn't completely fed their thoughts, it surely shackles them, and they will not be able to escape it. Because it was tightly secured by our Marjas, our scholars, our thinkers, our intellectuals, our preachers, our authors, and our religious and political Shia parties. The matter was tightly secured, so I do not believe anyone will escape this malignant cancer!

Our major problem began with Sheikh al-Tusi and continued. Our problem is very major. I do not want to return you to the past, for the program talks about this era, but if you want to know the facts of the past, review the program Al-Kitab Al-Natiq, and wait for me in the fourth part of the Al-Kitab wal-Itrah file in the program Khatimat al-Milaff. You will know the clear, manifest facts about the past.

What this demonic Qutbist tide brought us is merely the concentration and deepening of the concepts and ideas that invaded us through the Ash'arites, the Mu'tazilites, through the Shafi'is, and the Sufis, and the rest of the directions opposing the family of Muhammad, which ate us alive! **The utmost of what our scholars did was that they decorated all of this with a bit of the culture of the House of Infallibility, while denying most of their hadiths, denying their interpretation of the Quran, and denying most of their supplications and visitations!** This is the bitter truth we live amidst.
Segment 8: Evaluating the Literature on the Muslim Brotherhood

Moving to his third point, the Sheikh addresses the vast amount of literature written about the Muslim Brotherhood, Hasan al-Banna, and Sayyid Qutb. He categorizes these hundreds of books into works written by Brotherhood loyalists, former members, other Islamists, secularists/nationalists, and Western analysts. He cautions that simply reading these books will lead to confusion, as he personally evaluates all this literature through the uncompromising lens of "The Book and the Itrah" (The Quran and the Ahlulbayt) to extract the pure truth.

I will not prolong much in this direction, but rather I will go to a third point. The third point: I will talk in it about the books that were written in the atmosphere of the Brotherhood, Hasan al-Banna, and Sayyid Qutb. They wrote very many. Hundreds of books, hundreds of books were written around this subject and on this subject.

And whoever wants to review these books will not reach a clear result, at least from my point of view. Because I judge, and have judged, these books according to the logic of the Book and the Itrah. There are books authored by the leaders of the Brotherhood, the chiefs of the Brotherhood, and the cadres of the Brotherhood, and they are not few. And there are books authored by those who exited the Brotherhood, and they are of two types: there are those who exited the Brotherhood and did not condemn them, but rather pointed to very superficial matters; and there are those who exited the Brotherhood and exposed them scandalously. So books were authored by Brotherhood members, and books were authored by those exiting the Brotherhood. A group exited and exposed the Brotherhood, and a group exited and wrote nothing, either they are lying and afraid, or they truly know nothing about the scandals of the Brotherhood, their crimes, their malice, and their lies.

And books were written by Islamists from the people of Islam, rather from Al-Azhar and from outside Al-Azhar, from the Islamic atmosphere, talking about the Brotherhood. Some of them praised them, and some of them attacked them fiercely. And books were written by non-Islamists, non-religious people from the secularists, nationalists, communists, and others. They talked in them; some talked with documents and evidence, and some were just clowning around or transmitting information without being certain of it.

And books were written by Westerners in the United States of America, or in Britain, or in France, or in Germany; Europeans and Americans wrote these books. They analyze what is happening on the ground; they are correct sometimes and they make mistakes in other times. So if anyone wanted to return to these books, they are very many, very different, and very conflicting. But the truth does not hide! Whoever wants to search for the truth will find it in this program.

You will find an analysis of everything written about the Brotherhood from all these groups. All these groups of books, I harvested them and scrutinized them according to my understanding. I have no concern with others; I weighed the matters with the logic of the Book and the Itrah. And I harvested the demands according to the logic of scientific investigation. I will present the facts, whether you agree with me or disagree with me. What I find explicit and clear, I will point to it, and what I doubt, I will point to it as well.
We go to another dramatic break. And after the break, I will meet you.
Segment 9: The Demonic Plot from the Saqifah to the Muslim Brotherhood

Following another dramatic clip illustrating the insidious reach of the Muslim Brotherhood within the Egyptian state, Sheikh al-Ghizzi connects the historical dots. He identifies Hasan al-Banna as a "great devil" who opened the gates of hell in 1928. More profoundly, the Sheikh traces the ideological lineage of this "demonic plot" back to the Saqifah—the historical moment the Caliphate was established as an alternative to the divinely appointed path of "The Book and the Itrah" (The Quran and the Ahlulbayt). He argues that when the Ottoman Caliphate fell, the Muslim Brotherhood emerged as the modern Satanic replacement for the Caliphate. Tragically, instead of returning to the Imam of their time, Shia leaders and thinkers blindly ran after this new Nasibi (anti-Ahlulbayt) project.
[Dramatic Scene]

Since the year 1928, the year in which Hasan al-Banna founded the Muslim Brotherhood group, the gates of Hell were opened! And this dramatic scene that you watched tells a true reality; this is a crime from a long series of crimes of Hasan al-Banna and his secret organization.
